meta.sous_titre: Technical terms of the path — to understand, to meditate
meta.source_principale: Rafīq al-ʿAjam, Mawsūʿat muṣṭalaḥāt at-taṣawwuf al-islāmī (Beyrouth, Maktabat Lubnān Nāshirūn, 1999, 1204 p.)

cosmologie.label: Cosmology & eschatology
cosmologie.fr: The worlds, their structure, their end

confreries.label: Brotherhoods (ṭuruq)
confreries.description: The great initiatic ways of Sufism — each with its lineage (silsila), its founding master, its daily wird, its own practices.

entries.racine_sens: ṣūf — wool; ṣafāʾ — purity; ṣaff — the first rank
entries.definition_concise: The inner path of Islam — not a school nor a sect, but the contemplative and experiential dimension of the faith, transmitted from the Prophet through chains of masters and disciples.
entries.sens_profond: The word taṣawwuf has been the object of countless classical definitions — the Sufis say there are a thousand of them, as many as there are breaths of the sons of Adam. The most accepted etymology links it to ṣūf (صُوف), the coarse wool worn by the early ascetics as a sign of simplicity; others connect it to ṣafāʾ (صَفَاء), the purity of the heart; others again to ṣaff (صَفّ), the first rank before God. All these etymologies converge: it is an interiorisation of faith that aims at the effective transformation of the being. Taṣawwuf is not a theory about God: it is a work upon oneself for the sake of God. It has its own vocabulary, its practices (dhikr (الذِّكْر), samāʿ, khalwa (الخَلْوَة)…), its figures (shaykh (الشَّيْخ), murīd (مُرِيد), walī (وَلِيّ)), its stages (states and stations), its inner map (qalb (القَلْب), nafs (النَّفْس), rūḥ (رُوح)). Before being a school, it is a savour — the one who has not tasted does not know, the one who has tasted can no longer do without it.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Sufism is that God makes you die to yourself and makes you live through Him.
Sufism is entering into every noble character and leaving every base character.
Sufism is to grasp the realities and to despair of what the creatures possess.

entries.meditation: If Sufism is a savour — what is, within me, that savour which nothing else can nourish?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as taṣawwuf — wool, purity
entries.definition_concise: The one who walks on the path of Sufism. An honorific word that the tradition reserves for accomplished masters — the mere aspirant is called mutaṣawwif (مُتَصَوِّف) (one who aspires to become one) or murīd (مُرِيد) (the one who wills).
entries.sens_profond: One does not proclaim oneself a Sufi — one seeks to become one. The ancients carefully distinguished three ranks: the mutaṣawwif (the one who begins, who trains, who aspires), the ṣūfī (the one who has attained), and the mustaṣwif (مُسْتَصْوِف) (the one who imitates the Sufi without having the experience — the false one). For the classical authors, to say “I am a Sufi” is almost the proof that one is not — so far is the truth of this state from the feeling of having arrived. Ibn ʿAjība says that there are as many Sufis as there are divine Names manifested in them. The authentic Sufi is cleansed: his ego no longer screens, his word is just, his presence soothes. One recognises him by his fruits.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The Sufi is the one who possesses nothing and is possessed by nothing.
The Sufi is the one who wears wool, who walks according to the Real, and who has been compared to the People of the Bench (ahl al-ṣuffa) in the time of the Prophet.
The authentic Sufi is purified of all that is not God.

entries.meditation: How would I recognise a true Sufi if I met one — and what within me would recognise him?

entries.racine_sens: to strike, to hammer — hence to open a passage by striking the ground
entries.definition_concise: The way intermediate between the outer Law (sharīʿa) and the ultimate Truth (ḥaqīqa). It also denotes the brotherhood — the living lineage through which a master transmits the method to his disciples.
entries.sens_profond: Three words build the Sufi geography: sharīʿa (the Law, what one must do), ṭarīqa (the Way, how one does it inwardly), ḥaqīqa (the Truth, what one discovers in doing it). The sharīʿa is the ground, the ṭarīqa is the path traced upon that ground, the ḥaqīqa is the destination. A way without Law has no point of support; a Law without a way remains a dead letter; a truth without a way cannot be reached. The ṭarīqa in the institutional sense also denotes the brotherhood — the living organisation with a master (shaykh (الشَّيْخ)), a chain (silsila (السِّلْسِلَة)) reaching back to the Prophet, its own practices (litanies, retreats, samāʿ). Fourteen great ṭuruq still structure the Sufi world today.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The sharīʿa is my words, the ṭarīqa my acts, the ḥaqīqa my states.
The Law is like a candle that shows the way; when you walk on that way, it is the Way; when you have arrived, it is the Truth.

entries.meditation: On what way have I set my foot — and who walks ahead of me on it?

entries.racine_sens: to turn over, to transform, to turn upside down
entries.definition_concise: The subtle organ of direct knowledge and of transformation. Not the ordinary affective heart, but the centre of the being where the divine Presence is reflected and where the conversion of the soul takes place.
entries.sens_profond: The Arabic root q-l-b means to turn over — the qalb is that organ which changes, which passes from one state to another, a mobile mirror at the heart of the being. The hadith says: “The heart of the believer is between two fingers of the Merciful, who turns it as He wills.” For the Sufis, the qalb has seven concentric layers (according to certain schools): the breast (ṣadr (صَدْر)), the heart (qalb), the inmost heart (fuʾād (فُؤَاد)), the core (lubb), the secret (sirr (السِّرّ)), the secret of the secret (sirr al-sirr (سِرُّ السِّرّ)), and the hidden (khafī (خَفِيّ)). The heart is not the seat of the emotions in the psychological sense — it is the inner eye, the place where one sees. Hadith: “My heaven and My earth do not contain Me, but the heart of My believing servant contains Me.” All Sufi work is a work upon the heart — its polishing, its awakening, its purification.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The heart of the believer is the Throne of the Merciful (qalb al-muʾmin ʿarsh ar-Raḥmān (اَلرَّحْمَٰن)).
The heart is the organ by which one knows God directly — not a feeling, but a vision.
As long as the mirror of the heart is not polished, the face of the Beloved cannot appear in it.

entries.meditation: If my heart is a mirror — what has it reflected today?

entries.racine_sens: breath, oneself, individuality
entries.definition_concise: The soul in its individual and carnal dimension — the one that desires, that resists, that takes itself for the centre. To work on the nafs (النَّفْس) is to educate it, to tame it, to transform it until it is at peace.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran distinguishes three great nafs (states of the soul): the nafs that commands evil (al-ammāra bi-l-sūʾ, 12:53) — the soul given over to its passions; the nafs that reproaches itself (al-lawwāma, 75:2) — the soul awakened to its own mediocrity, which judges itself and progresses; the nafs at peace (al-muṭmaʾinna (مُطْمَئِنَّة), 89:27) — the soul returned to God, content and accepted. The Sufis extended this taxonomy to seven degrees of the nafs, corresponding to seven inner colours seen in retreat (khalwa (الخَلْوَة)). The major struggle (al-jihād (الجِهَاد) al-akbar), according to the Prophet, is the one against the nafs, greater than any outward battle: “We return from the lesser combat toward the greater combat. — What is the greater combat, O Prophet? — The combat against the soul.” But beware: the nafs is not killed, it is transformed. It is also the matter of sainthood.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Whoever conquers his nafs will conquer the world; whoever surrenders to his nafs will be conquered by the world.
The nafs is the nearest enemy — but it is also the mount that carries you to God if you know how to train it.
Your most terrible enemy is your nafs, which is between your two sides.

entries.meditation: What do I take within me for myself — and which is perhaps only my nafs in disguise?

entries.racine_sens: breath, wind, rest
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual principle breathed by God into the human being — the highest dimension of the human being, by which he is akin to the divine and not to the earthly alone.
entries.sens_profond: In verse 15:29, God says of Adam: “When I have harmoniously shaped him and breathed into him of My Spirit, prostrate yourselves before him.” This in-breathing (nafkh) of the rūḥ (رُوح) distinguishes the human being from every other creature — he is not only flesh and nafs (النَّفْس), he carries within him a gleam of the divine. In verse 17:85, to the question of men about the nature of the spirit, God has the Prophet answer: “The spirit is of the affair of my Lord, and you have been given but little knowledge of it.” The rūḥ remains, therefore, fundamentally a mystery. The Sufis often distinguish the universal rūḥ (al-rūḥ al-kullī — close to the Neoplatonic First Intellect) and the individual rūḥ breathed into each human being. The rūḥ is that in us which does not die — it returns to its Source when the body ceases.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The spirit (rūḥ) is the light of God in the human being; without it, the human being would be but clay without a face.
You are a child of the spirit — why do you believe yourself born of the earth?

entries.meditation: If a breath of God was breathed into me — where have I let it breathe today?

entries.racine_sens: to hide, to conceal — hence the secret
entries.definition_concise: The most intimate point of the heart — beyond even the awareness one can have of oneself. The place of the private meeting between God and the soul, where no other can enter.
entries.sens_profond: The sirr (السِّرّ) is, in the Sufi cartography of the inward, deeper than the qalb (القَلْب). If the qalb is the chamber where one prays, the sirr is the hidden oratory behind the curtain. The qudsī hadith says: “The human being has a secret between him and his Lord, which is shared with no one.” Every authentic disciple has a sirr — something he has told no one, and which is between him and God alone. The masters speak of several degrees: sirr (the secret), then sirr al-sirr (سِرُّ السِّرّ) (the secret of the secret), then khafī (خَفِيّ) (the hidden) — where one is no longer even aware of the content of what one carries, where the veil covers even memory. The sirr is also the seat of the final unveiling: it is there that the highest maʿrifa is received, and that, sometimes, the shaṭḥ (شَطْح) — the ecstatic utterance — bursts forth.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The sirr is the depth of the heart — what God alone knows, and what knows Him.
My sirr has been carried off by the sirr of God; there is nothing left but Him.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a secret I share with no one — and what do I share with it?

entries.racine_sens: to bind, to fetter (as one fetters a camel so it does not flee)
entries.definition_concise: The organ of principial knowledge — the nous and intellectus of the Western tradition, not the calculating reason. The faculty that in the human being sees the true directly, to be absolutely distinguished from modern reasoning.
entries.sens_profond: The word ʿaql shares its root with ʿiqāl (عِقَال), the cord that fetters the camel — the intellect is that which binds thought to its origin, prevents it from straying. For the Islamic tradition, the ʿaql is not cold reason; it is the light by which the human being recognises the One. The first hadith says: “The first thing God created was the Intellect.” But this intelligence is in us imprisoned by the nafs (النَّفْس): the ordinary ʿaql is partial (juzʾī), a prisoner of the senses and of passion; above it there exists a universal ʿaql (kullī) or an actualised ʿaql (bi-l-fiʿl), fully realised in the sages. To confuse the ʿaql with modern reason is a capital error: it is what Rūmī calls “the wooden leg,” unsteady, which carries one only to the threshold of the Real — beyond, one needs the wing.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The first thing God created was the Intellect; He said to it: ‘Advance’ — it advanced; ‘Withdraw’ — it withdrew.
The leg of the reasoner is of wood — and the wooden leg is very unsteady.
The true intellect is the soul fully actualised — not an instrument, but a light.

entries.meditation: Is my intelligence a tool that I use — or rather an eye through which I see?

entries.racine_sens: to cleave, to make hatch — hence the primordial creation, the origin
entries.definition_concise: The original, innate disposition by which the human being is naturally turned toward God — the primordial Adam present in every human being, prior to all culture and all forgetting.
entries.sens_profond: Verse 30:30 says: “Set your face to the religion as a pure ḥanīf, according to the fiṭra in which God has fashioned humankind.” And the famous hadith: “Every child is born according to the fiṭra; it is its parents who make of it a Jew, a Christian or a Magian.” The fiṭra is therefore that pure nature, attuned to the One, present at the birth of each one, which one does not learn but recovers. Spiritual work is not, for this tradition, the addition of something external — it is the cleansing that restores the fiṭra to itself, as one wipes a tarnished mirror. It is in this sense that Adam, in each of us, has already answered God, in the primordial pact: “Am I not your Lord? — Yes, we bear witness” (Quran 7:172). The whole of life is the labour of remembering that original yes.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Every child is born according to the fiṭra; it is its parents who make of it something else.
The fiṭra is the natural inclination toward tawḥīd (تَوْحِيد) — it is the Adam in us who remembers.

entries.meditation: Is there within me something that already knows what I have not learned?

entries.racine: ع ي ن — ق ل ب
entries.racine_sens: ʿayn — eye, source, essence; qalb — heart
entries.definition_concise: The organ of inner vision — not the eye of flesh that sees phenomena, but the one by which the heart perceives spiritual realities directly.
entries.sens_profond: The word ʿayn (عَيْن) means in Arabic at once the eye, the source, and the very essence of a thing. The ʿayn al-qalb (القَلْب) is therefore, in a single word, the eye-source-essence of the heart. It is, in most people, closed — covered over by the nafs (النَّفْس), by opinion, by distraction. Sufi work is precisely the polishing that opens this eye. Once open, it does not reason — it sees. It is by it that one perceives the baraka (البَرَكَة), that one reads a heart, that one hears what is not said. The baṣīra (بَصِيرَة) (spiritual perspicacity) is its light, the kashf (الكَشْف) (unveiling) is its act. No degree of knowledge equals what is seen by this eye — every verbal description is but a shadow of it.

voix_des_maitres.texte: It is not the eyes that are blind, but the hearts within the breasts.
What the eyes of the body do not see, the eye of the heart sees; what reason cannot reach, the heart grasps.

entries.meditation: The eye of my heart — is it open or closed at this moment?

entries.racine_sens: to be one, unique
entries.definition_concise: The first principle of Islam and of Sufism: God is One, without associate. But for the Sufis, tawḥīd (تَوْحِيد) is not a formula to recite — it is a realisation, the effective experience that nothing is real save Him.
entries.sens_profond: The first half of the shahāda — lā ilāha illā Llāh (لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّٰه) (there is no divinity but God) — is the formula of tawḥīd. But the Sufis distinguish three degrees: the tawḥīd of words (saying the formula); the tawḥīd of the heart (effectively believing it, acting accordingly); and the tawḥīd of experience (seeing, in the unveiling, that there is indeed nothing other than God — this is the lived tawḥīd, sometimes called tawḥīd al-tawḥīd (تَوْحِيدُ التَّوْحِيد)). At this last degree, the Sufi no longer merely says “God is One” — he sees that all that seems multiple is but His manifestation. This radical intuition (waḥdat al-wujūd (وَحْدَةُ الوُجُود) in Ibn ʿArabī) does not contradict the otherness of Creator and creature, but illumines it: there is but one Reality, of which we are the shadows.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Tawḥīd is the separation of the eternal from the temporal — without leaving a doubt along the way.
Whoever lays claim to tawḥīd associates something with it: his own tawḥīd.
There is in existence nothing other than God — all the rest is an appearance of Him.

entries.meditation: If God is truly alone in being — who am I, at this moment, reading these words?

entries.racine_sens: to be sound, at peace, to surrender oneself
entries.definition_concise: The first of the three degrees (islām, īmān (إِيمَان), iḥsān (إِحْسَان)) of the hadith of Gabriel: the outward surrender to God through acts — the testimony of faith, prayer, alms, fasting, pilgrimage. The visible foundation.
entries.sens_profond: The word islām shares its root with salām (سَلَام) (peace) and salāma (سَلَامَة) (integrity, salvation). To become muslim is to surrender oneself — not in the sense of defeat, but in the sense in which one lays down the weapons of one's own will before the greater Will, and finds peace in it. The hadith of Gabriel sets out three concentric circles: islām (the visible works), īmān (the inner faith), iḥsān (excellence — to worship as if you saw Him). Sufism does not reject islām — it is its interiorisation. Without islām (the works), no authentic Sufism; but without iḥsān, islām is only outward, a practice without a heart.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Islam is that you testify there is no divinity but God and that Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) is His Messenger, that you perform the prayer, give alms, fast the month of Ramadan, and make the pilgrimage if you have the means.
Islām is the door; īmān is the house; iḥsān is what one lives within it.

entries.meditation: To what have I surrendered today — and what did I find there?

entries.racine_sens: to be in safety, to trust
entries.definition_concise: The second degree of the hadith of Gabriel: the inner faith, the adherence of the heart, which goes beyond mere outward practice (islām (إِسْلَام)) without yet attaining vision (iḥsān (إِحْسَان)).
entries.sens_profond: The root ʾ-m-n also yields amān (أَمَان) (safety) and amīn (أَمِين) (trustworthy). To believe (āmana) is to place oneself in safety within the Real — to entrust one's being to one greater than oneself. The hadith says: īmān is to believe in God, in His angels, in His Books, in His Messengers, in the Last Day, and in the decree, good and bad. But this mental adherence is only a beginning. For the Sufis, true īmān is in the heart — not in the brain; it is felt in trust, not in recitation. Without īmān, islām is an empty practice; without iḥsān, īmān remains an opinion, however fervent.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Īmān is what is established in the heart and what the acts confirm.
A faith without works is like a tree without fruit.

entries.meditation: What do I truly believe — not with my head, but with my heart?

entries.racine_sens: to be beautiful, good — hence excellence
entries.definition_concise: The summit of the hadith of Gabriel: to worship God as if one saw Him, and if one does not see Him, knowing that He sees you. Iḥsān is Sufism, in the most condensed formulation the tradition has given.
entries.sens_profond: The Prophet, questioned by Gabriel about what iḥsān is, replied: “It is that you worship God as if you saw Him; and if you do not see Him, He sees you.” This definition contains the whole Sufi way: bringing practice from the outward (islām (إِسْلَام)) and belief (īmān (إِيمَان)) to living presence — to live under the gaze of the Real, or while seeing Him. The root ḥ-s-n also yields ḥusn (حُسْن) (beauty): to worship in iḥsān is to make one's worship beautiful, to perfect it, to render it worthy of the One whom one worships. The Sufis sometimes call it maqām al-iḥsān (مَقَامُ الإِحْسَان) — the station of excellence — or simply al-taṣawwuf (التَّصَوُّف).

voix_des_maitres.texte: Iḥsān is that you worship God as if you saw Him; and if you do not see Him, He sees you.
Iḥsān is the maqām in which the worshipper knows that he is seen, and ends by seeing the One who sees him.

entries.meditation: What I am doing at this moment — could I do it before Him?

entries.racine_sens: to trace a way, to open a path (toward a source of water)
entries.definition_concise: The revealed Law — the body of rules God has prescribed for life in society and for worship. The first of the three degrees sharīʿa-ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة)-ḥaqīqa. For the Sufis, it is never abolished, always traversed.
entries.sens_profond: The root sh-r-ʿ denotes, in its primary sense, the path that leads to water — the way of access to the vital source, in the desert. The sharīʿa is therefore, in the original image, that which leads the soul to its Source. It is not a legalistic burden; it is the trace, upon the ground, of the path traced by God so that the human being does not lose his way. Rūmī compares the sharīʿa to the candle that shows the way, the ṭarīqa to the actual walking, and the ḥaqīqa to the arrival. No authentic Sufi exempts the murīd (مُرِيد) from the sharīʿa — on the contrary, the great masters were almost all accomplished jurists. The distinction between exoterism and esoterism is not an opposition but an unfolding: the outer protects the inner, the inner fertilises the outer.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The Law is like a candle: without it, no walking. Do not cast it away until you have arrived.
All ways are barred, save the one that walks in the footsteps of the Prophet.

entries.meditation: The rule I follow — does it bring me to the source, or take me away from it?

entries.racine_sens: to be true, real; to have a right, to be due
entries.definition_concise: The ultimate Truth — not a knowledge, but the state in which the veil is lifted and one sees things as they are in God. The third and last of the degrees sharīʿa-ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة)-ḥaqīqa.
entries.sens_profond: The root ḥ-q-q means at once true, real, and due. The Ḥaqq (اَلْحَقّ) is one of the most beautiful Names of God — He is Reality itself, as opposed to what has only a borrowed being. The ḥaqīqa, as a spiritual state, is that moment in which one perceives that all that is not He is but a transient appearance. For the Sufis, the ḥaqīqa does not contradict the sharīʿa, it accomplishes it: the accomplished saint, at this level, still performs the ritual prayer, but it has become in him another thing — the appearing to the world of the Reality that inhabits him. The famous Sufi saying goes: “Every ḥaqīqa that contradicts the sharīʿa is a falsehood; every sharīʿa that does not lead to the ḥaqīqa is a dead letter.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: The ḥaqīqa is to see God without intermediary.
The ḥaqīqa is neither above nor beside the sharīʿa — it is its secret heart.

entries.meditation: Do I know something really — or only by hearsay?

entries.racine_sens: to know by experience, to recognise
entries.definition_concise: Knowledge by direct experience of God — not a knowledge about Him, but a knowing of Him, in which the knower disappears into the known. Distinct from ʿilm (science) by its transformative nature.
entries.sens_profond: The verb ʿarafa does not mean only “to know” in the neutral sense: it carries the idea of recognising, that is, of knowing anew, as if one were finding again what one already knew without knowing it. Maʿrifa is in this sense a return to the fiṭra — the soul recognises its Lord because it has already answered yes before creation (Quran 7:172). The Sufis distinguish ʿilm (عِلْم) (knowledge by study) from maʿrifa (knowledge by unveiling). One may know much and not know; one may know little and have received everything. Maʿrifa is obtained through inner work, but it is given, never conquered — it comes like a grace, sudden, after long patience. The one who possesses maʿrifa is the ʿārif bi-Llāh (عَارِفٌ بِاللَّٰه) (the gnostic in God) — a major honorific term.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Three degrees of the knowledge of God: by the signs (the common), by the proofs (the learned), by the unveiling (the Sufis).
Whoever knows himself knows his Lord (man ʿarafa nafsahu fa-qad ʿarafa Rabbahu).
Maʿrifa is that you see God in every thing, and that you see nothing other than Him in yourself.

entries.meditation: To know God — is it to add to my knowledge, or to remove what prevents me from seeing Him?

entries.racine_sens: to remember, to mention, to evoke
entries.definition_concise: The remembrance of God through the repeated invocation of His Name — a central practice of Sufism. At once recollection, vocalisation and presence, the dhikr (الذِّكْر) is the spiritual breathing of the servant.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran commands: “Remember Me, and I shall remember you” (2:152). This injunction is, for the Sufis, the heart of the way. The dhikr has several forms: vocal dhikr (with the tongue), dhikr of the heart (silent, in the breast), dhikr of the whole soul (in which one becomes what one invokes). Each ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة) has its formulas: Allāh (اَللَّٰه), Allāh… ; Lā ilāha illā Llāh (لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّٰه) ; Yā Ḥayy (اَلْحَيّ) yā Qayyūm (اَلْقَيُّوم) ; Hū, Hū… But the dhikr is not a magical technique — it is a relationship. Forgetfulness (ghafla (الغَفْلَة)) is the major illness of the soul; the dhikr is its remedy. More deeply still, the masters say that true dhikr is mutual: you remember Him, but it is He who remembers within you. In the end, it is no longer you who invoke — it is He who invokes Himself through you.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Is it not by the dhikr of God that hearts find rest?
Do not abandon the dhikr on the pretext that you are not present to God within it — your forgetfulness of Him without dhikr is worse than your forgetfulness of Him with dhikr.
In the beginning, it is you who call; in the end, you recognise that it was He who was calling you through your own voice.

entries.meditation: If I spent a day remembering Him — what would change in all the rest?

entries.racine_sens: to hear, to listen
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual listening — sacred music, chant, sometimes dance — practised in certain ṭuruq as a means of opening the heart. Far more than an entertainment, the samāʿ is a way of knowledge through the inner ear.
entries.sens_profond: The samāʿ is contemplative audition — the word literally means “listening.” It consists, according to the order, of the chanted recitation of the Quran, of psalmodied mystical poems, of instrumental music (notably the ney, the reed flute, among the Mevlevis), sometimes accompanied by the ritual dance of the whirling dervishes. But the samāʿ is not a concert: it is a guarded practice — there were heated debates between Sufis and jurists over whether it is lawful. For its defenders (the majority of the great Sufis), the samāʿ opens a door that words alone cannot open: “Where the tongue stops, music begins,” says the tradition. Rūmī founded his entire teaching upon this practice — the whirling dance of the Mevlevis is its most famous expression. But beware: the samāʿ is effective only upon a prepared heart. Upon a coarse heart, it does worse than harm — it distracts. Upon a polished heart, it tears the veils.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The music of the spheres turns in our veins; to dance is to remember.
The samāʿ reveals what is in the heart — it creates nothing, it brings to the surface.

entries.meditation: What music is my heart listening to, there, beneath the noises of the world?

entries.racine_sens: to be alone, empty, vacant
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual retreat — generally forty days of isolation, silence and intense invocation, under the direction of a master. A major initiatic practice in several ṭuruq, notably the Khalwatiyya.
entries.sens_profond: The khalwa reproduces the retreat of Moses (forty nights on Mount Sinai), of Jesus (forty days in the desert), and the seclusion of the Prophet Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) in the cave of Ḥirāʾ. The number forty has a precise symbolic reach: it marks the completion of a cycle of transformation. During the khalwa, the disciple is alone in a small cell, barely eats, sleeps little, keeps an almost continuous dhikr (الذِّكْر), sees his shaykh (الشَّيْخ) only once a day to report his visions and states. The ordeal is harsh: the veils fall one by one, the nafs (النَّفْس) wages its last battles, colours appear — each master has elaborated a cartography of the seven inner colours (yellow, red, white, green, blue, black, colourless) corresponding to the seven degrees of the nafs. On coming out, the disciple is no longer the same — often the first months are devoted to reintegrating the world without being seized by it again.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The one who is solitary with God is never alone.
In the khalwa, the traveller receives what a thousand books would not give.

entries.meditation: When was I last alone with myself — truly alone?

entries.racine_sens: to observe, to watch, to keep watch
entries.definition_concise: Sustained attention to the divine presence within oneself and to oneself before God — a continuous vigilance that distinguishes the accomplished Sufi from the distracted one. A cardinal practice, which one might render as “to stand under the gaze.”
entries.sens_profond: The word murāqaba derives from the same root as ar-Raqīb (اَلرَّقِيب), one of the divine Names (“The Watchful”). To practise murāqaba is to stand consciously under His gaze, while being oneself attentive to what is happening within oneself. It is the practical application of iḥsān: “to worship as if you saw Him.” Concretely, the practitioner takes during the day several brief moments — sometimes a few seconds — to check: where am I? What is my heart doing? Am I with Him, or am I lost in forgetfulness? This vigilance, little by little, becomes continuous. It does not tense — it frees: one ceases to be tossed about by moods, one recovers a centre. The Naqshbandis have placed it at the very heart of their method, which they call yād-dāsht (یاد داشت) (the held remembrance).

voix_des_maitres.texte: All spiritual effort comes down to these two: muḥāsaba (self-examination) and murāqaba (vigilance).
If you do not see Him, know that He sees you.

entries.meditation: There, at this very instant — who is watching my thoughts?

entries.racine_sens: to serve, to be in the service of — hence ʿabd, the servant
entries.definition_concise: The act of worship in its totality: prayer, fasting, dhikr (الذِّكْر), but also every action accomplished for God. The ʿubūdiyya (عُبُودِيَّة) (pure servanthood) is even, for the Sufis, the highest degree of the human being — higher than visible sainthood.
entries.sens_profond: The word ʿibāda comes from the root ʿ-b-d, which also yields ʿabd (عَبْد) (the servant), which is the very name the Prophet preferred to any other: “I am only a servant.” To worship is to recognise oneself as a servant — not crushed, but in the just place. The Quran recalls: “I created the jinn and humankind only that they might worship Me” (51:56). But ʿibāda is not only the ritual prayer — for the Sufis, every action consciously accomplished for Him is ʿibāda: eating, working, speaking, sleeping. More deeply, the highest maqām (مَقَام) is not that of the walī (وَلِيّ) (the saint) but that of the ʿabd (the pure servant) — the one who no longer has any interest of his own, who is wholly transparent to his Lord. The Prophet, the most perfect of human beings, is called first of all ʿabdihi wa rasūluh (His servant and His messenger) — servant before messenger.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I created the jinn and humankind only that they might worship Me.

My God, if I worship You out of fear of hell, burn me in it; if I worship You out of hope of paradise, exclude me from it; but if I worship You for Your own sake, do not deprive me of Your eternal beauty.

entries.meditation: When I act “for Him” — what changes in my gestures?

entries.racine_sens: to change, to be transformed, to pass from one state to another
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual state — a passing gift that descends into the heart of the servant, without his having acquired it by his own effort. To be absolutely distinguished from the maqām (مَقَام) (station), which is acquired and lasting.
entries.sens_profond: The masters rigorously distinguish aḥwāl (أَحْوَال) (states) and maqāmāt (مَقَامَات) (stations). The ḥāl is given — it occurs like an unexpected grace: an instant of deep peace, a tear that springs up while reading a verse, a sudden light in prayer. One does not bring it about, one receives it. And it passes — that is its nature. The maqām, conversely, is acquired by work: patience, gratitude, detachment. A station, once attained, remains; a state, as soon as one tries to grasp it, flees. The classic error of the beginner: to believe one has arrived because one has tasted a ḥāl. The Sufis warn: do not become attached to the states, it is the giver who matters, not the gift. “A thousand states will pass — a single well-established maqām is worth more.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: States are flashes of lightning; stations are dwellings.
When a ḥāl comes, withdraw before it; when it departs, do not regret it. He knows when to return.

entries.meditation: What touches me today — is it a passing state, or something settling in?

entries.racine_sens: to fear, to dread
entries.definition_concise: The fear of God — not servile dread, but a trembling reverence before His greatness. Coupled with rajāʾ (رَجَاء) (hope), it is the right wing of the traveller; the left wing is hope.
entries.sens_profond: Sufi khawf does not resemble ordinary fear. It is not the fear of hell — for Rābiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya, it is even unworthy to love God out of fear of chastisement. True khawf is the reverence that seizes the soul before the immeasurable. It is what makes the saint tremble precisely because he loves. The Quran evokes it often: “Those who fear their Lord in secret…” The Sufis always couple it with its beneficial opposite, rajāʾ (hope): these two wings are necessary. Too much khawf without rajāʾ shuts one into despairing scruple; too much rajāʾ without khawf lulls one into presumption. The right balance — al-khawf wa-l-rajāʾ (الخَوْفُ وَالرَّجَاء) — is the art of the traveller on the way.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The believer has a trembling heart: he fears he has fallen short, and he hopes he has been received.
Among His servants, only the learned fear Him.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a fear that raises — different from the dread that lowers?

entries.racine_sens: to hope, to await
entries.definition_concise: Hope in the divine mercy — a confident expectation that balances khawf (الخَوْف) (fear). The traveller on the way advances with these two wings, losing neither.
entries.sens_profond: If khawf is the acute awareness of one's unworthiness, rajāʾ (رَجَاء) is the trust in His goodness that surpasses that unworthiness. “Do not despair of the mercy of God” (Quran 39:53), because His mercy, says the hadith, “precedes His wrath.” Rajāʾ is not a passive waiting, nor a presumption — it is an opening of the heart that keeps the walking going, even in the night. Without rajāʾ, khawf becomes despair and paralysis; with rajāʾ, it becomes active reverence. The ancients say: the beginner needs more khawf than rajāʾ in order to tear himself from the nafs (النَّفْس); the accomplished one needs more rajāʾ than khawf so as not to be engulfed by the greatness glimpsed.

voix_des_maitres.texte: My mercy outruns My wrath.
What makes you despair of God is your inattention to His goodness; what reassures you too much is your inattention to His majesty.

entries.meditation: What do I truly hope for — at the deepest, beneath all my surface hopes?

entries.racine_sens: to desire ardently, to long
entries.definition_concise: The ardent desire for God — a nostalgia for His Presence that consumes the soul and tears it from every lesser satisfaction. It is the other name of love when it feels the distance.
entries.sens_profond: The shawq (الشَّوْق) is love that suffers from distance. The heart that has tasted union knows that every distance, however slight, is from then on painful. This pain is precious — it is what makes one advance. Without it, one falls asleep in the lukewarm. Shawq is different from ordinary desire: one desires what one has never possessed; one feels nostalgia for what one has known and lost. The Quran (7:172) recounts that all souls once answered yes to God, in pre-eternity — shawq is the living memory of that yes. It is the lament of the ney cut from the reed-bed, in Rūmī's prologue. A saint without shawq is not a saint — it is a heart already sated, hence already dead.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Longing for Me has made the righteous what they are — without it, they would be nothing.
The lament of the reed is itself the way toward the mother-reed; without its cry, no return.

entries.meditation: What I miss most deeply — toward Whom does it turn me?

entries.racine_sens: to be familiar, intimate — opposite of waḥsha (strangeness, painful solitude)
entries.definition_concise: Intimacy with God — a state in which the heart finds in His Presence a sweet familiarity, like a loved one rediscovered. The opposite of waḥsha (وَحْشَة) (the uncanny strangeness that marks distant hearts).
entries.sens_profond: The word uns is rich in Arabic: it evokes familiar warmth, the company that soothes, the feeling of being at home. It is exactly what the accomplished Sufi feels in the presence of his Lord: no longer the trembling reverence of the beginner, but a trusting intimacy. Intimate human households (insān = human, because we are made for company) are an earthly figure of uns. Conversely, waḥsha is the uncanny strangeness, that unease of a soul that wanders in search of its home. The hadith says: “God created Adam with His own hands, and the soul rediscovers its uns near its origin.” The saints have uns with God; children almost all have it, through their still-intact fiṭra; we have lost it, and must reconquer it.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Uns is the chamber in which the heart rests.
Whoever has tasted uns with God finds human company heavy — not out of contempt, but because he has found his home.

entries.meditation: Where, and with Whom, am I truly at home?

entries.racine_sens: to stand upright, to rise up
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual station — a degree attained and acquired by lasting effort. Unlike the ḥāl (the passing state), the maqām remains once conquered. The Sufi way presents itself as a ladder of stations.
entries.sens_profond: The word maqām literally means “the place where one stands upright.” A spiritual station is a degree one has climbed through one's practice and which one now inhabits. One can lose it if one slackens, but as a rule, what is acquired remains. The Sufi orders have drawn up ladders of stations — some count seven, others ten, others forty. The most classical are: tawba (التَّوْبَة) (repentance), zuhd (الزُّهْد) (renunciation), faqr (الفَقْر) (poverty), ṣabr (صَبْر) (patience), shukr (الشُّكْر) (gratitude), tawakkul (التَّوَكُّل) (abandon), riḍā (رِضَا) (acceptance). Each station is conquered through repeated work, and each opens onto the next. A classic error: to skip a station one believes secondary. The way is rigorous — the tree bears no fruit before it has roots.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The maqām is what the servant acquires; the ḥāl is what descends upon him.
Without a firm station, the states are but flashes of lightning with no dwelling.

entries.meditation: Where do I stand at this moment — on which station, and for how long?

entries.racine_sens: to return, to turn back
entries.definition_concise: The return to God after estrangement — the first station of the way, without which no other is possible. Not a morbid remorse, but an active turning of the face toward the Source.
entries.sens_profond: The root t-w-b does not mean “to punish oneself” but to return. The Quran calls God Himself at-Tawwāb (اَلتَّوَّاب) (the One who returns toward the penitent) — tawba (التَّوْبَة) is therefore fundamentally reciprocal: you turn toward Him, He turns toward you. It is even He who begins — it is because He returns that you can return. Tawba is the first obligatory station: without it, one does not even leave the quay. But it is never exhausted — the saints continue to repent, not of their faults (they commit them no more) but of their distractions, their inattentions, their moments of forgetting. For the masters: the tawba of the common is of the fault; the tawba of the elite is of forgetfulness; the tawba of the accomplished is even of the awareness of their tawba — every self-representation of progress being itself a veil.

voix_des_maitres.texte: If My servant draws near to Me by a span, I draw near to him by a cubit; if he comes to Me walking, I go to him running.
Come, come, whoever you are, come — even if you have broken your vows a hundred times, come.

entries.meditation: From what do I have to return, there, now?

entries.racine_sens: to turn away, not to become attached
entries.definition_concise: Detachment from the world and its seductions — not out of contempt, but out of a clear preference. The zāhid is not deprived: he has chosen something else, more essential.
entries.sens_profond: Zuhd (الزُّهْد) is not asceticism for asceticism's sake — it is a displacement of the heart. The Quran does not condemn wealth in itself (the Prophet Solomon was a king): it condemns attachment to wealth. One can be poor and greedy; one can be rich and detached. The criterion is inward. The famous hadith: “What harm is there in having a mount that carries you? — Only if it does not ride you.” For the Sufis, three degrees of zuhd: to deprive oneself of forbidden things (the degree of the common); to deprive oneself of superfluous things although permitted (the degree of the devout); to detach oneself even from spiritual graces (the degree of the lovers — for even the charisms can become a veil). The ultimate zāhid is naked not outwardly, but inwardly.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Zuhd is that you do not rejoice too much in what comes to you, and do not grieve too much over what leaves you.
The world is a bridge: cross it, do not build upon it.

entries.meditation: To what am I truly attached — and what would happen if it were taken from me?

entries.racine_sens: to lack, to be in need
entries.definition_concise: Poverty in God — not material destitution, but the inner stripping-away that recognises one possesses nothing, that all is borrowed. The faqīr (فَقِير) is the one who has nothing left to defend — hence everything to receive.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran (35:15) says: “O humankind, you are the poor before God; and God is the Rich, the Worthy of praise.” Sufi faqr is the full, even joyful, acceptance of this ontological poverty. As long as I believe I possess something — my knowledge, my virtue, my time — I am rich and have no need of God. When I see that I possess nothing (all is borrowed, at every second, from Him), I become faqīr and He becomes my only wealth. The Prophet said: “My pride is my poverty” (faqrī fakhrī). The title of faqīr (in Persian: dervish, which means the same thing) is one of the most honoured in the Sufi tradition. The accomplished faqīr begs nothing of anyone — neither of men, nor even of God: he only welcomes what comes.

voix_des_maitres.texte: My pride is my poverty (faqrī fakhrī).
The faqīr is nothing — and that is why he is everything.
Ask God for poverty more than for any other wealth — for in it is your true wealth.

entries.meditation: What do I believe I possess — which has in reality only been lent to me?

entries.racine_sens: to hold back, to contain — hence patience as the restraint of the soul
entries.definition_concise: Patience — not passivity, but the active holding of the soul within the trial, until deliverance comes. Mentioned more than 90 times in the Quran; for some, the highest virtue after faith.
entries.sens_profond: The word ṣabr (صَبْر) originally means to hold back, to contain. Patience is not the apathy of one who feels nothing — it is the holding of one who feels deeply but does not let himself be carried away. Three ṣabr according to the masters: patience in obedience (to keep practising when it is arid); patience in resistance to evil (not to yield to the nafs (النَّفْس)); patience in the trial (to receive pain without rebelling or collapsing). This last is the highest. The Quran often couples ṣabr with ṣalāt (prayer): “Seek help in patience and prayer” (2:153). And: “The patient will be fulfilled without measure” (39:10). Patience is a mount — whoever rides it crosses any desert.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Give the good news to the patient — those who, struck by misfortune, say: ‘We belong to God, and it is to Him that we return.’ Upon them are the blessings of their Lord.
Patience is to faith what the head is to the body: without it, no living faith.

entries.meditation: In which trial, at this instant, am I asked to be patient?

entries.racine_sens: to acknowledge a benefit, to bear witness to it
entries.definition_concise: Gratitude — not only verbal thanks, but an active acknowledgement that makes the benefit bear fruit: to recognise God as the source, and to use the gift according to His will.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran (14:7) says: “If you are grateful, I shall add to your benefit; if you are ungrateful, My chastisement is terrible.” Shukr (الشُّكْر) is not a formality — it is an energy that multiplies gifts. The ungrateful one loses not only gratitude: he loses, little by little, the capacity to receive. Three shukr according to the classical authors: the shukr of the tongue (to say alḥamdu lillāh); the shukr of the heart (to feel the grace in each gift); the shukr of the limbs (to use what is given for what is just — one's eyes for what deserves to be seen, one's tongue for what deserves to be said). The highest shukr is the one that forgets nothing: to see His hand in every thing, even in what seems lack or suffering — because nothing comes to us without His permission, and all is, at a deeper level, mercy.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Whoever is not grateful to people is not grateful to God.
True shukr is to see the giver in the gift — and not the gift alone.

entries.meditation: If I had to enumerate my blessings — where would I stop?

entries.racine_sens: to entrust, to delegate, to commit to
entries.definition_concise: To entrust to God the outcome of everything — not as passivity, but as an act of trust that does its part and leaves His to Him. Tawakkul (التَّوَكُّل) is the right attitude before the mystery of causality.
entries.sens_profond: The word comes from the same root as al-Wakīl (اَلْوَكِيل), the divine Name (The Guardian-Trustee). To practise tawakkul is to entrust one's affair to God after having done one's own part. The famous hadith: someone says to the Prophet “Should I tie my camel or entrust myself to God?” — he replies: “Tie your camel, and entrust yourself to God.” Everything is there: human effort is required, but the outcome does not belong to us. Three degrees of tawakkul according to Sahl al-Tustarī: to do what one must and abandon the result (the common); to live in the certainty that nothing happens but by His will (the elite); to be before Him like a child in its mother's arms — to ask nothing more, to expect nothing more, because He knows. Tawakkul does not kill action: it frees it. One acts without anguish over the result. It is the most difficult and the most liberating of arts.

voix_des_maitres.texte: And whoever places his trust in God, He suffices him.
Accomplished tawakkul is that between you and Him there is no longer any intermediation — not even your worries.

entries.meditation: What do I hold back out of fear of letting Him act?

entries.racine_sens: to accept, to be content with, to be satisfied
entries.definition_concise: The deep acceptance by the soul of the divine decree — to accept what is given and what is withdrawn, with the same peace. Considered the highest station of the classical way.
entries.sens_profond: Riḍā (رِضَا) is deeper than patience (ṣabr (صَبْر)). The patient one endures what he does not love; the contented one loves what is given to him, whatever its appearance. He knows that at a level deeper than his perception, God knows better than he what suits him. Quran 5:119: “God is content with them and they are content with Him” — it is a double contentment, of one with the other. Riḍā is the classical end of the stations: tawba (التَّوْبَة) (to return) → zuhd (الزُّهْد) (to renounce) → faqr (الفَقْر) (to be empty) → ṣabr (to hold) → shukr (الشُّكْر) (to give thanks) → tawakkul (التَّوَكُّل) (to entrust oneself) → riḍā (to be content). Beyond it is pure love and fanāʾ (فَنَاء). Contentment is not bitter resignation — it is the depth of a trust such that even pain no longer destabilises the soul: one is so deeply established in the Presence that nothing external can any longer undo it.

voix_des_maitres.texte: O soul at peace, return to your Lord, content and accepted.
My God, my contentment with You is my acceptance.
The sign that you are established in riḍā: your joy does not change when the states change.

entries.meditation: Is there a thing in my life that I do not accept — and what would I need in order to accept it?

entries.racine_sens: to love — the root also yields ḥabba (seed), as if love were a seed at the heart
entries.definition_concise: Love — the deep and lasting attachment of the heart to God. The vital centre of Sufism in all its forms. If you love, you walk; if you do not love, you merely read.
entries.sens_profond: Maḥabba is, for many Sufis, the essence of the way. Rābiʿa al-ʿAdawiyya, in the 2nd century, was the first to speak explicitly of the pure love of God — not out of fear of hell, not out of hope of paradise, but for His own sake. This inner revolution determined all of Sufism to come. The classical authors distinguish several degrees: love as will (to will the good of the beloved), love as desire (to aspire to the beloved), love as annihilation (in which there is no longer any difference between lover and beloved). The qudsī hadith gives the key: “When I love him, I become the hearing by which he hears, the sight by which he sees, the hand by which he grasps, the foot by which he walks.” Love therefore leads to an identification — not pantheistic, but in which the servant becomes transparent to his Lord.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I love You with two loves: a passionate love, and a love because You are worthy of being loved.
My servant does not cease to draw near to Me through the supererogatory works, until I love him. And when I love him, I become his hearing, his sight, his hand, his foot.

entries.meditation: If the love of God were to fill a single one of my gestures today — which one?

entries.racine_sens: to love passionately — a non-Quranic root but central among the Sufis
entries.definition_concise: Passionate, burning love, which consumes all but the Beloved. More intense than maḥabba (مَحَبَّة). A controversial word among the jurists but central among the Persian Sufis, notably Rūmī and Ibn ʿArabī.
entries.sens_profond: The strict theologians long refused to attribute ʿishq to God — too intense, too carnal, unworthy of the divine sobriety. But the Sufis went past this: for them, ʿishq is the right word, because the love of God is not a lukewarm affair. Rūmī writes: “Love is that flame which, when it blazes up, consumes all but the Beloved.” ʿishq is distinguished from maḥabba by its violence: it tears, it ravishes, it drives one mad. Majnūn is mad for Laylā, the Sufi is mad for God — the madness here is a praise, not a criticism. Iqbāl, in the 20th century, will make of ʿishq the vital force par excellence, opposed to cold reason. Like maḥabba, ʿishq culminates in fanāʾ (فَنَاء): the lover ceases to exist, the Beloved alone remains.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Love is that flame which, when it blazes up, consumes all but the Beloved.
ʿishq is the love that exceeds all bounds — and it is by exceeding the bounds that it reaches the boundless.
If you have not burned, you have not begun.

entries.meditation: What within me still burns — or no longer burns?

entries.racine_sens: to be near, to draw close
entries.definition_concise: The nearness of God — a state in which the veil between the servant and his Lord grows thin. Distinct from union (waṣl) — one is near without confusion.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran (50:16) says: “We are nearer to him than his jugular vein.” Nearness is therefore given from the origin: God is nearer to you than yourself. But this ontological nearness is not awareness of nearness. Sufi work consists in realising what is already there. Three degrees according to Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh (اَللَّٰه): nearness through dependence (you know that He holds you, without feeling Him); nearness through presence (you feel Him in your heart); nearness through disappearance (you no longer find yourself, but Him). Qurb is different from waṣl (union): in qurb, two remain; in waṣl, only One remains. For many masters, qurb is more just than waṣl — nearness preserves servanthood, which is the ultimate dignity of the servant.

voix_des_maitres.texte: We are nearer to him than his jugular vein.
I am according to what My servant thinks of Me — let him think Me near, and I am near.

entries.meditation: Nearer to me than my jugular vein — why do I have the impression that He is far?

entries.racine_sens: to join, to reach, to arrive
entries.definition_concise: Union with God — the moment in which the distance between lover and Beloved is abolished. A delicate notion: for some it is an arrival, for others a fusion; the sober masters distinguish.
entries.sens_profond: The word waṣl means “junction, arrival, meeting.” It describes that moment, in the way of the lover, when he no longer feels the distance: he is with his Beloved. But what does this mean exactly? Three schools: (1) union is a fusion — the lover disappears into the Beloved (ittihād (اِتِّحَاد)); the sober ones reject this formulation as excessive. (2) Union is a functional identification — the lover wills what the Beloved wills, acts according to His will; the classical authors hold this sense. (3) Union is a presence without intermediary — the lover is before the Beloved without a veil, but remains distinct; this is the most accomplished version. The great Sufi debate between ittihād (fusion) and tawḥīd (تَوْحِيد) (union within distinction) runs through the whole history of the way. Rūmī, Ibn ʿArabī, al-Junayd each have their subtlety.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Waṣl is to return to the beginning — where you were before being.
Union is not confusion: it is that you finally see what was always there.

entries.meditation: If the distance between me and Him were but a veil — who is holding it?

entries.racine_sens: to remove the veil, to uncover
entries.definition_concise: The removal of the veil between the servant and the Real — a moment in which a spiritual truth is seen, not thought. A mode of knowledge proper to Sufism, to be distinguished from prophetic revelation (waḥy (وَحْي)) and from ordinary inspiration (ilhām).
entries.sens_profond: For the Sufis, the veil (ḥijāb (حِجَاب)) between the human being and God is not on God's side — it is the human being who does not see. The kashf is the instant in which that veil, on the servant's side, is torn. It is not an idea that comes — it is a thing that is seen. Hujwīrī, one of the classical authors, titles his great treatise Kashf al-maḥjūb (كَشْفُ المَحْجُوب) (“The unveiling of the veiled”). The kashf may be of several orders: kashf of the invisible (to see an angel, to perceive the soul of a departed one); kashf of hearts (to read what is happening in another); kashf of realities (to grasp the hidden meaning of a verse, of an event). The highest kashf is that of the Presence itself, which is rare and brief. But the masters warn: do not become attached to the kashf — they may be graces, but also subtle temptations of the ego that wants to be the one who sees.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The kashf is not a gain for the one who sees — it is a gift to be handled with reverence and silence.
Every true unveiling leads to more humility, never to more pride.

entries.meditation: What I do not see, there, right before me — who has drawn its veil?

entries.racine_sens: to breathe, to inspire (in Arabic, as one breathes in a word)
entries.definition_concise: The in-breathing of a truth into the heart — a mode of knowledge by which God directly projects a knowledge, a certainty, or a direction, without the intermediary of teaching.
entries.sens_profond: Ilhām is below waḥy (وَحْي) (prophetic revelation, which is the exclusive prerogative of the prophets), but above ordinary reason. It is that which in us knows without having learned it. The Quran evokes this infusion concerning the soul: “And by the soul and the One who harmoniously shaped it, and breathed (ilhām) into it its waywardness and its piety” (91:7-8). Every human being has within him this twofold inspiration, like two possible voices. The saint has cultivated the way that makes him attentive to the right inspiration. Ilhām is recognised by its fruits: it brings peace, it points toward the good, it does not contradict the sharīʿa. Any inspiration that leads to agitation, to pride or to transgression is not a divine ilhām — it is an insinuation of the nafs (النَّفْس) or worse. Discernment: to tell the difference is one of the most subtle arts of the way.

voix_des_maitres.texte: And by the soul and the One who shaped it, and breathed into it its waywardness and its piety.
Any inspiration that does not bring peace is not an inspiration from God.

entries.meditation: Which voice within me breathes the most true — and do I listen to it?

entries.racine_sens: to taste
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual tasting — knowledge by direct experience, as one tastes a savour. What absolutely distinguishes the one who knows by hearsay from the one who knows by what he has lived.
entries.sens_profond: The word dhawq (الذَّوْق) literally means “to taste.” The Sufis chose it deliberately to denote their mode of knowledge — as opposed to purely theoretical science. One may know that honey is sweet without having tasted it; and one may taste honey without being able to describe it. The highest maʿrifa is of this order: dhawq, not description. When a Sufi says “hadhā dhawqun” (“this is tasting”), he means: I know it because I have lived it. This is why the Sufis insist: you may read a thousand treatises on love, as long as you have not loved, you know nothing. You may read a thousand treatises on fanāʾ (فَنَاء), as long as you have not died to yourself, you know nothing. Man dhāqa ʿarafa (whoever has tasted knows) — a canonical formula. Dhawq is what separates the scholar from the sage.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Whoever has tasted knows; whoever has not tasted will never know through words.
The ʿulamāʾ speak of honey by hearsay; the Sufis taste it at their table.

entries.meditation: What have I tasted myself, in my spiritual life — and what have I only heard told?

entries.racine_sens: to be certain, without doubt
entries.definition_concise: Certainty — not a firm opinion, but an evidence that excludes doubt, because it rests on seeing. Three degrees: by hearsay, by observation, by direct experience.
entries.sens_profond:
The Sufis classically distinguish three degrees of yaqīn, drawn from sūrat al-Takāthur (102):

1. ʿilm al-yaqīn (عِلْمُ اليَقِين) (the knowledge of certainty) — like knowing there is a fire because one has seen its smoke. Knowledge by sign.

2. ʿayn al-yaqīn (عَيْنُ اليَقِين) (the eye of certainty) — like seeing the fire with one's own eyes. Knowledge by direct vision.

3. ḥaqq al-yaqīn (حَقُّ اليَقِين) (the truth of certainty) — like being within the fire, feeling it, undergoing it. Knowledge by fusion.

Most people live at the first degree: they believe because they have been told, because they have read, because the proofs are strong. That is already good, but it is not true yaqīn. The saint has passed to the second, even the third. Complete yaqīn is what makes it impossible to doubt God any longer — not out of fanaticism, but because one sees Him or is within Him. It is this that gives the saints their quiet assurance.

voix_des_maitres.texte: If you knew with the knowledge of certainty… you would surely see the Gehenna. Then you would see it with the eye of certainty.
Were the veil lifted, I would have no more certainty.

entries.meditation: Of what am I truly certain — to the point of no longer being able to doubt it?

entries.racine_sens: to pass away, to be extinguished, to disappear
entries.definition_concise: The extinction of the self in God — the moment in which individual consciousness disappears into the consciousness of the Real. The apparent summit of the way, but in reality a passage toward baqāʾ (بَقَاء) (subsistence), which is higher.
entries.sens_profond: The word fanāʾ comes from the Quran: “All that is upon it is called to pass away (fānin); but the Face of your Lord remains, full of majesty and generosity” (55:26-27). The Sufi who realises fanāʾ does not die physically — he dies to himself: to his identifications, to his desires, to his nafs (النَّفْس). What remains is the essential in him, which is of God. Several degrees: fanāʾ of the nafs (to die to the ego), fanāʾ of love (the lover loses himself in the Beloved), fanāʾ of consciousness (one is no longer conscious of oneself or of the world, one simply is). But beware: fanāʾ is not the final goal. The goal is baqāʾ — subsistence through God, the return into the world after fanāʾ, transformed. The masters say: whoever stops at fanāʾ has stopped halfway. The true way is: fanāʾ then baqāʾ.

voix_des_maitres.texte: All that is upon it is called to pass away; but the Face of your Lord remains.
Die before you die.
Tawḥīd (تَوْحِيد) is that you be as you were before being.

entries.meditation: What should I let die within me — in order truly to begin to live?

entries.racine_sens: to remain, to subsist
entries.definition_concise: Subsistence in God after extinction — the state of the accomplished Sufi who, having traversed fanāʾ (فَنَاء), returns into the world but as transparent to God. His ordinary life has become a continuous presence.
entries.sens_profond: If fanāʾ is the passage from the I to nothingness, baqāʾ is the return from nothingness to a renewed I — an I that is no longer the old one, that no longer acts by itself, that has become a means of God. The saint who is in baqāʾ lives like everyone — he eats, sleeps, works, speaks — but in him nothing is any longer as before. It is what the tradition calls the saint in the street: invisible, ordinary in appearance, but transparent to the Light. Baqāʾ is higher than fanāʾ for the sober ones: fanāʾ may be a passing dazzlement; baqāʾ is a dwelling, lasting. The iconic Sufi formula: fanāʾ ʿan al-nafs (النَّفْس) wa baqāʾ bi-Llāh — extinction of self and subsistence through God. It is the complete programme.

voix_des_maitres.texte: When the servant returns from fanāʾ, he returns to serve — not to admire himself for having been extinguished.
The summit is to become ordinary again after having been extraordinary — and for no one to notice it.

entries.meditation: If something within me were to be reborn — what, and how would I live with it?

entries.racine: ظ ه ر — ب ط ن
entries.racine_sens: ẓ-h-r: to appear; b-ṭ-n: to be inward, hidden
entries.definition_concise: The cardinal pair — the apparent and the hidden, two divine Names. Every thing has its ẓāhir (its manifested form) and its bāṭin (its inner, hidden meaning). The Sufi way is to pass from the ẓāhir to the bāṭin without denying the ẓāhir.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran (57:3) says of God: “He is the First and the Last, the Apparent (aẓ-Ẓāhir (اَلظَّاهِر)) and the Hidden (al-Bāṭin (اَلْبَاطِن)) — and of every thing He is the All-Knowing.” This verse founds the whole Sufi metaphysics of resolved duality: God is at once all that appears (creation as the manifestation of His Names) and all that hides itself (His ineffable essence). Likewise, each thing has its ẓāhir (the husk, the form, the letter) and its bāṭin (the kernel, the meaning, the spirit). The Law (sharīʿa) is the ẓāhir; the ḥaqīqa is the bāṭin. The recited Quran is the ẓāhir; its seven hidden senses are the bāṭin. A major error in both directions: to content oneself with the ẓāhir (arid legalism) or to lay claim to the bāṭin while despising the ẓāhir (spiritual libertinism). The Sufis insist: “Every appearance is the covering of a heart; every heart is the transparency of an appearance.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: He is the First and the Last, the Apparent and the Hidden.
The bāṭin without the ẓāhir is reverie; the ẓāhir without the bāṭin is a dead letter.

entries.meditation: This thing I see there, before me — what is its bāṭin?

entries.racine_sens: elder, venerable
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual master — a living guide who has traversed the way and can lead others. For the Sufis, one does not advance alone: the shaykh (الشَّيْخ) is the bridge between the novice and the living tradition.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufi adage is strict: “Whoever has no shaykh, his shaykh is Satan.” This does not mean that any shaykh will do, nor that all submission is blind. It means that one cannot, alone, traverse the veils, the traps, the illusions of the nafs (النَّفْس). One needs an outer witness who sees what one does not see in oneself, who rectifies, who transmits the baraka (البَرَكَة), who opens the states. The shaykh is given in providence: one does not quite choose him, one meets him. The relationship between shaykh and murīd (مُرِيد) (disciple) is sacred — it is a spiritual rebirth. Three types of shaykh according to Ibn ʿArabī: the shaykh ar-rūḥī (الشَّيْخُ الرُّوحِيّ) (the spiritual master, distant or invisible — Khiḍr is its archetype); the shaykh as-suḥba (شَيْخُ الصُّحْبَة) (the master of companionship, who lives with you); the shaykh at-tarbiya (التَّرْبِيَة) (the master of education, who forms you step by step). The three may coincide — or not.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Whoever has no shaykh, his shaykh is Satan.
The master is a mirror: you see yourself in him — see what you will become.

entries.meditation: Who has taught me what I know that is most essential — and have I thanked him?

entries.racine_sens: to will, to desire
entries.definition_concise: The disciple — literally the one who wills, that is, the one who has the firm will to walk on the way. More than a pupil, he is one who is committed: he wills in the strong sense.
entries.sens_profond: The word murīd, of the same root as irāda (إِرَادَة) (will), denotes the one who has taken the step of seriously willing the way. He is not the curious one who reads books, nor the amateur who attends a few circles: he is the one who has made the inner vow to go all the way, whatever the price. The murīd commits himself to a shaykh (الشَّيْخ) through an act called bayʿa (بَيْعَة) or ʿahd (عَهْد) (pact), as the Companions swore fidelity to the Prophet beneath the tree. The murīd must then obey the shaykh in the spiritual practice — not out of servility, but because he knows that his own will is still entangled with illusions, and that a clearer will must guide it for as long as is needed. A magnificent paradox: to will the way is to will to cease willing by oneself — to will that the will of God be substituted for one's own.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The murīd must be before his shaykh like the dead body in the hands of the washer.
The murīd who does not submit his will to one greater than his own remains a prisoner of his own whirling.

entries.meditation: Do I truly will this way — in the sense that I am ready to pay the price?

entries.fr: The friend of God, the saint
entries.racine_sens: to be near, an ally, a protector
entries.definition_concise: The friend of God — the saint, in the Islamic tradition. Not one canonised by an institution, but the one whom God Himself has drawn near, and who bears the fruits of it. All Sufi sainthood is held within this word.
entries.sens_profond: The word walī comes from the root w-l-y, which means to be near and to be a protector. The walī is therefore at once near to God and protected by God — the friend in the strong sense. The Quran (10:62-63) says: “The friends of God (awliyāʾ Allāh (اَللَّٰه)), no fear is upon them and they shall not grieve — those who have believed and who feared God.” Sainthood in Islam (الإِسْلَام) is not an official promotion; it is a state of soul recognised after the fact. The accomplished walī does not proclaim himself a saint — he is almost always discreet, even hidden. A famous qudsī hadith: “Whoever opposes one of My friends, I declare war upon him.” And the other face: sainthood is given, never conquered. One does not become a walī by merit — one becomes it by grace. But this grace falls only upon prepared hearts.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The friends of God — no fear is upon them, and they shall not grieve.
Whoever opposes one of My friends, I declare war upon him.
The walī is the one through whom God makes Himself visible in the world.

entries.meditation: Who in my life has carried something more — as if he were carrying not only his own?

entries.racine_sens: pivot, central axis
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual pole — a cosmic figure of Sufism: there is in every age one human being through whom the spiritual order of the world is maintained, the summit of a hidden hierarchy of holy souls.
entries.sens_profond: For many Sufi schools, particularly the Shādhiliyya, there exists in every generation a quṭb — a being through whom the spiritual balance of the world is preserved. He is surrounded by a hierarchy: the abdāl (أَبْدَال) (the substitutes, about 7 or 40), the nujabāʾ (نُجَبَاء) (the noble ones), the nuqabāʾ (نُقَبَاء) (the deputies). This cosmology of the rijāl al-ghayb (رِجَالُ الغَيْب) (“men of the invisible”) is highly developed in Ibn ʿArabī. The quṭb does not always know himself to be quṭb — often he is hidden, ordinary in appearance. He may be a mosque sweeper as well as a great scholar. At the death of each quṭb, another takes his place — the chain never breaks. This doctrine is debated: the sober ones see in it a cosmological metaphor (the spiritual axis mundi); some take it literally. The accomplished walī (وَلِيّ), without necessarily being quṭb, takes part in this invisible economy: the world stands upright through the saints.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The earth will never be empty of a witness of God (lā takhlū al-arḍ ʿan ḥujjati Llāh).
The quṭb does not refer to a title: it refers to a cosmic function, which a sweeper may hold as well as a sultan.

entries.meditation: Are there, without my knowing it, beings who maintain the balance of the world — and what has one of them taught me?

entries.racine_sens: to kneel — hence the settling of the good in a place, like water that accumulates
entries.definition_concise: The divine blessing — a good that is added to things, makes them fruitful, lasting, multiplying. It may reside in a place, an object, a person, an act, an instant. It is through baraka that the way is transmitted.
entries.sens_profond: The word baraka comes from a root evoking water that accumulates and fertilises. The baraka is what, in a thing, exceeds that thing: blessed bread nourishes more than it should; a saint carries away the baraka of a place merely by passing through it; a true word leaves a trace that fertilises for a long time. The Quran evokes the blessed nights (laylatun mubāraka), the blessed lands (Jerusalem, Mecca), the blessed books (the Quran). The baraka is contagious: it is communicated through the companionship (suḥba) of the saint, through the visit (ziyāra) of the tomb of a walī (وَلِيّ), through contact with a blessed object (the Prophet's cloak, the autograph writings of the saints). The whole Sufi tradition rests on the transmission of this baraka, from master to disciple, through the silsila (السِّلْسِلَة) (chain) that reaches back to the Prophet. It is this that separates living Sufism from mere bookish study: there is a continuity of baraka.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Glory to the One who by night made His servant journey from the sacred Mosque to the far-distant Mosque, whose surroundings We have blessed.
Seek baraka with your elders — they are its keepers.
Baraka is not seen — it is recognised by what it leaves behind it.

entries.meditation: Where do I feel, without being able to explain it, that there is something more?

entries.racine_sens: ilāh — divinity, that which is worshipped; or underived, the proper Name of the Essence
entries.definition_concise: The proper Name of God, which gathers within itself all the other divine Names. For the Sufi tradition, it is the Name of the Essence, prior to all description, and the key to the supreme invocation.
entries.sens_profond: The Arab grammarians long debated: is Allāh derived from al-ilāh (الإِلَه) (“the god,” the Worshipped), or an underived Name, a pure Name of the Essence? The Sufi tradition leans toward the second: this Name imitates nothing, it is first. This is why it is called al-ism al-aʿẓam (الاِسْمُ الأَعْظَم) — the greatest Name. All the other Names (al-Raḥmān (الرَّحْمَٰن), al-Karīm (اَلْكَرِيم), al-Wadūd (اَلْوَدُود)…) are within it as the rays are within the sun. To say “Allāh” is to name simultaneously all the beauties and all the majesties. This is why the supreme dhikr (الذِّكْر) of most ṭuruq is this Name alone, repeated until it inhabits the breath. Ibn ʿArabī teaches: “All that is not Allāh is a name — He alone is the named.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: When you pronounce ‘Allāh,’ you have nothing more to ask for — everything is asked.
Allāh is the Name that gathers all the Names, as the trunk gathers the branches.
The most beloved of words before God are: Subḥān-Allāh, al-Ḥamdu li-Llāh, Lā ilāha illā Llāh (لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّٰه), Allāhu akbar (اَللَّٰهُ أَكْبَر).

entries.meditation: If I were to pronounce this Name — a single one, in presence — what within me would receive it?

entries.racine_sens: oneself, one's own
entries.definition_concise: The divine Essence itself — God inasmuch as He is, independently of all that can be said of Him. Unknowable in itself, the source of all the Attributes and of all the Names.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufi tradition rigorously distinguishes three orders: the Dhāt (الذَّات) (the pure Essence), the Ṣifāt (الصِّفَات) (the Attributes — living, knowing, willing…), and the Asmāʾ (الأَسْمَاء) (the Names — which denote Essence and Attributes). The Dhāt in itself is beyond all description: it is called al-dhāt bi-lā ism wa-lā rasm (the Essence without name or description). No analogy reaches It, no comparison encloses It. It is what Suhrawardī and Mullā Ṣadrā call the absolute Absolute. No saint, however elevated, knows the Dhāt — he knows only the theophanies (tajalliyāt) of the Names. This is why the qudsī hadith says: “I was a hidden treasure and I loved to be known, so I created the creatures.” The treasure is never exhausted.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The Dhāt is the Inaccessible — what one says of It is external to It.
The Essence veils itself in Its Names, and reveals Itself through Them; It shows itself only veiled.

entries.meditation: If I cannot know the Essence — what, then, can I know?

entries.racine_sens: to describe, to qualify
entries.definition_concise: The essential Qualities of God — living, knowing, willing, powerful, speaking. Distinct from the Dhāt (الذَّات) (the Essence) without being separable from It. It is the Attributes that manifest themselves through the Names.
entries.sens_profond: Classical Islamic theology long debated the relation between Dhāt and Ṣifāt (الصِّفَات): are the Attributes the Essence itself, or other than It? The Sufis hold the middle way: “The Attributes are neither the Dhāt, nor other than the Dhāt” — they are distinct in the intellect, indissociable in reality. Seven principal Attributes are recognised: ḥayāt (حَيَاة) (life), ʿilm (عِلْم) (knowledge), irāda (إِرَادَة) (will), qudra (power), samʿ (سَمْع) (hearing), baṣar (بَصَر) (sight), kalām (كَلَام) (speech). Each Attribute is the foundation of several Names: knowledge founds al-ʿAlīm (اَلْعَلِيم) (the All-Knowing), al-Khabīr (اَلْخَبِير) (the Perfectly-Informed), al-Ḥakīm (اَلْحَكِيم) (the Wise)… For the human being who wants to know God, it is through the Attributes that he can draw near — the Dhāt remaining forever veiled. To know God is to know His Knowledge that knows you, His Sight that sees you, His Life by which you live.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The Attributes are neither He nor other than He — they are the way in which we think Him.
You grasp of God only the gleam of one Attribute — and already you stagger.

entries.meditation: Which of the divine Attributes have I most forgotten — and which one already inhabits me without my knowing it?

entries.racine_sens: to rise, to name (the name as that which raises the thing)
entries.definition_concise: The Names by which God names Himself — the 99 (and more) Beautiful Names (al-asmāʾ al-ḥusnā (الأَسْمَاءُ الحُسْنَى)) which are at once what one invokes, what manifests itself in creation, and the structuring principles of the real.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran says: “To God belong the most beautiful Names; invoke Him by them” (7:180). And: “He taught Adam all the Names” (2:31). The tradition retains 99 canonical Names (according to Tirmidhī's hadith), but some Names are kept secret — al-Ghazālī says there are in reality an infinity. For Ibn ʿArabī, each Name has its face in creation: nature, for example, manifests al-Khāliq (اَلْخَالِق) (the Creator) and al-Mubdiʿ (the Originator); the trials manifest al-Qābiḍ (اَلْقَابِض) (He who Contracts); the graces manifest al-Bāsiṭ (اَلْبَاسِط) (He who Expands). To know the Names is to learn to read the world. And each Name calls for a servant's share: one becomes merciful by contemplating al-Raḥmān, one becomes patient by contemplating al-Ṣabūr. All the Sufi way is, at one level, a work upon the Names.

voix_des_maitres.texte: God has ninety-nine Names — a hundred less one; whoever enumerates them enters Paradise.
The whole world is the appearing of the Names — each thing bears the signature of a Name.

entries.meditation: Which divine Name is manifesting itself, at this instant, in what I am living?

entries.racine: ن ز ه — ش ب ه
entries.racine_sens: n-z-h: to declare pure, to raise above; sh-b-h: to make similar, to compare
entries.definition_concise: The fundamental theological pair: tanzīh (تَنْزِيه) (to declare God radically beyond all comparison) and tashbīh (تَشْبِيه) (to recognise His similitude with His creatures). All right thought of God holds the two together.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran says at once: “Nothing is like Him” (42:11) — this is tanzīh, pure — and: “He is with you wherever you are” (57:4), “We are nearer to him than his jugular vein” (50:16) — this is tashbīh, which speaks of nearness.
