The strict theologians (the tanzīhites) wanted to cast out all tashbīh as idolatry; the literalists (sometimes tashbīhites) on the contrary took the anthropomorphic attributes in the literal sense. The Sufi way, particularly in Ibn ʿArabī, refuses both extremes: God is at once beyond all comparison and manifested in every thing. “He who believes he sees only the transcendence is ignorant; he who believes he sees only the similitude is a polytheist; he who unites the two is a gnostic.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: Nothing is like Him — and He is the Hearing, the Seeing.
He who says ‘He is’ associates; he who says ‘He is not’ denies. The way is to say both together.

entries.meditation: Is God, for me, rather the very-Distant or the very-Near — and is one without the other true?

entries.racine_sens: to light, to illumine
entries.definition_concise: Light — a divine Name (an-Nūr (اَلنُّور)) and a metaphysical principle: that by which all that is becomes visible. For the ishrāqiyyūn (the school of Suhrawardī), reality itself is light at different degrees.
entries.sens_profond: Verse 24:35 says: “God is the Light of the heavens and the earth.” The whole ishrāqī (illuminationist) cosmology of Suhrawardī is rooted in this verse: reality is not substance, it is light, at different degrees of intensity. God is the Light of Lights (nūr al-anwār (نُورُ الأَنْوَار)); the angels are immaterial lights; human souls are lights veiled by the body; matter itself is darkened light. Knowledge is not reasoning in the strict sense — it is illumination, light passing from a source to a receiver. This is why true knowledge transforms: it illumines the one who receives it, like a ray. The verse of Light (24:35) is a fundamental text that all the Sufis have commented upon — al-Ghazālī devoted an entire book to it (Mishkāt al-anwār (مِشْكَاةُ الأَنْوَار), The Niche of Lights).

voix_des_maitres.texte: God is the Light of the heavens and the earth. His Light is like a niche in which there is a lamp...
All that is, is light; and what is not light, is not.
The eye sees by the light of the sun; the heart sees by the light of God.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a light that is not my own?

entries.racine_sens: to reveal oneself, to show oneself clearly, to open out
entries.definition_concise: The manifestation of God — the moment in which the Invisible makes itself visible, in which a divine Attribute or Name appears in creation or in the heart. A central concept in Ibn ʿArabī: the whole world is woven of theophanies.
entries.sens_profond: The word tajallī comes from the Quranic account of Moses on Mount Sinai: “When his Lord manifested Himself (tajallā) to the mountain, He pulverised it and Moses fell thunderstruck” (7:143). The tajallī is the appearing of God — always partial (the Dhāt (الذَّات) itself remains veiled), always filtered through a Name or an Attribute. For Ibn ʿArabī, the cosmos is nothing other than an infinite sequence of theophanies — each instant, each creature, each event is a letter of the divine appearing. More deeply still, the heart of the accomplished saint becomes a mirror in which God contemplates Himself: this is the tajallī fī-l-qalb (القَلْب) (the theophany in the heart). But beware: no theophany repeats itself identically — each instant carries its singularity, as though God never manifested Himself twice in the same way. “God does not manifest Himself twice under the same form to a single heart” (Ibn ʿArabī).

voix_des_maitres.texte: When his Lord manifested Himself to the mountain, He pulverised it and Moses fell thunderstruck.
No theophany repeats itself, and no place receives the same one twice.

entries.meditation: If God manifested Himself at this instant — through which Name, and where in me?

entries.racine_sens: to veil, to hide, to separate
entries.definition_concise: The veil that separates the servant from the divine Presence. For the Sufis, this veil is never on God's side — it is the soul that does not see. Spiritual work is precisely the removal (kashf (الكَشْف)) of the veils.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran says: “It was not granted to a human being that God should speak to him, save by revelation, or from behind a veil” (42:51). The whole drama of the human condition is held in this veil: God is not absent (He is nearer than the jugular vein), but we do not see Him — a veil is drawn, on the side of our consciousness. The Sufis have enumerated these veils: the veil of the nafs (النَّفْس) (the ego), the veil of the passions, the veil of opinions, the veil of culture, the veil of knowledge acquired without realisation… More subtly still, there are veils of light (according to a famous hadith) that are harder to pierce than the veils of darkness — the spiritual graces themselves can become veils, if one becomes attached to them. The hadith says: “God has seventy thousand veils of light and of darkness; were He to remove them, the splendours of His Face would burn all that His gaze reaches.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: God has seventy thousand veils of light and of darkness; were He to remove them, the splendours of His Face would burn all that His gaze reaches.

If the light of certainty were to rise, you would see the hereafter nearer to you than your own journey toward it; and you would see the beauties of this world as the darkness that weighs upon what must pass away.

entries.meditation: What veil, at this instant, prevents me from seeing Him — and of what is it made?

entries.racine_sens: to be absent, hidden, invisible
entries.definition_concise: The invisible — all that is withdrawn from sensory perception: God, the angels, destiny, the hereafter, the soul. To believe in the ghayb is, according to the Quran, the first characteristic of the believer.
entries.sens_profond: Sūra 2 opens with: “This Book, no doubt in it — a direction for those who fear God, who believe in the ghayb…” (2:2-3). The ghayb denotes the whole order of realities that escapes the five senses: God first, the angels, the decree, the garden and the fire, the souls. For the Sufi tradition, it is also the world of the rijāl al-ghayb (رِجَالُ الغَيْب) (the men of the invisible) — the hidden hierarchy of saints who maintain the cosmic balance. To see the ghayb (by unveiling, kashf (الكَشْف)) is a rare gift; but to believe in it is asked of all. The hadith of Gabriel specifies: “Iḥsān is that you worship God as if you saw Him; and if you do not see Him, know that He sees you.” The “as if” is the right attitude before the ghayb: not to reduce it to fiction, not to lay claim to vision, but to live as if. This posture transforms everything.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Those who believe in the ghayb, perform the prayer and spend of what We have given them.
The ghayb is more real than the visible — but it is through the visible that one enters it.

entries.meditation: What within me do I believe without seeing — and what does that belief change?

entries.racine_sens: womb, maternal breast — hence mercy as the tenderness of the origin
entries.definition_concise: The divine mercy — an all-encompassing Attribute. The Prophet says: “My mercy has enveloped all things” (qudsī hadith). All of creation is born of the raḥma and returns to it; it precedes wrath.
entries.sens_profond: The root r-ḥ-m is that of raḥim (رَحِم) (the womb, the breast that carries). Divine mercy is therefore imagined as a maternal containing — God carries the world as a mother carries her child. The Quran opens each sūra (save one) with: Bi-smi Llāhi-r-Raḥmāni-r-Raḥīm — “In the Name of God, the All-Merciful, the Most Merciful.” Two mercies: al-Raḥmān (الرَّحْمَٰن) (the All-Merciful), universal, which gives being to all that is, believer or not; al-Raḥīm (الرَّحِيم) (the Most Merciful), elective, which bends tenderly over the one who returns. The qudsī hadith says: “My mercy outruns My wrath,” and: “My mercy has enveloped all things.” For Ibn ʿArabī, the raḥma is even the first Attribute through which God manifests Himself: “the breath of the Merciful” (nafas (النَّفَس) ar-Raḥmān (اَلرَّحْمَٰن)) is that continuous breath by which God maintains the worlds in being. Without this breath, all would return to nothingness at every instant.

voix_des_maitres.texte: My mercy outruns My wrath.
God created mercy in a hundred parts; He sent down one upon the earth, and kept ninety-nine with Him.

entries.meditation: If I receive at every instant the breath of His mercy — what prevents me from feeling it?

entries.racine_sens: to be pleasant, sweet; to enjoy a good
entries.definition_concise: The free benefit that God pours upon His servants — life, faith, health, intelligence, love, and still more the hidden benefits. Its human counterpart is shukr (الشُّكْر) (gratitude).
entries.sens_profond: The Quran asks: “And if you counted the benefits of God, you could not number them” (16:18). All that you have has been given to you — your breath at this instant, your capacity to read these words, the fact that your heart beats, the image your eye receives. The naʿma (النِّعْمَة) is not only material: the greatest graces are invisible. The hadith says: “Health and security — two graces of which many people are deprived.” For the Sufis, the ultimate grace is faith — without it, all the other graces would be distractions. And higher still, the grace of the knowledge of God: the one that makes all the others legible as being His gifts. The Sufi master is the one who sees the naʿma in all that happens, including in what seems lack or pain — because all bears His signature, and nothing is deprived of His generosity once one looks deeply enough.

voix_des_maitres.texte: If you counted the benefits of God, you could not number them.
When you see Him in His benefits, you thank Him; when you see Him without His benefits, you love Him.

entries.meditation: Let me name three graces received this morning that I had not seen as such…

entries.racine_sens: to exceed, to surpass, to overflow
entries.definition_concise: The divine favour — what God gives beyond merit, out of pure overflow. If the naʿma (النِّعْمَة) is the gift, the faḍl is the gift that exceeds — that answers more than was asked.
entries.sens_profond: The root f-ḍ-l evokes what overflows, what is in surplus. The divine faḍl is that gift which is not measured by what one has done: it is the pure generosity of God who gives beyond the just. The Quran says it often: “That is of the favour of God; He gives it to whom He wills” (62:4). The accomplished saint knows that he has merited nothing — all that he has is faḍl. This acknowledgement is the very mark of sainthood: not the pride of accomplishment, but the vertigo of the favour received. For the Sufis, it is the faḍl that saves: no human being, were he the most pious, would enter Paradise by his works alone — the hadith says that even the Prophet specified: “None of you will enter Paradise by his works. — Not even you, O Messenger of God? — Not even I, unless God envelops me in His favour and His mercy.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: None of you will enter Paradise by his works — including me, unless God envelops me in His favour and His mercy.
He precedes you by favour before you bring the service; do not, then, attribute the merit to yourself.

entries.meditation: What have I received, in my life, that I clearly did not merit?

entries.racine_sens: to praise, to celebrate
entries.definition_concise: The praise of God — an acknowledgement that goes beyond mere thanksgiving (shukr (الشُّكْر)): one praises God not only for what He gives, but for what He is. The Fātiḥa opens thus: “al-ḥamdu li-Llāh, Rabbi-l-ʿālamīn (عَالَمِين)”.
entries.sens_profond: The classical distinction: one thanks (shukr) for a gift received; one praises (ḥamd) for the perfection of a being, independently of the gift. Praise is therefore purer than thanksgiving — it does not depend on what one has received, but on what one perceives of the divine beauty. The Prophet is called Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) (“the much-praised”) and Aḥmad (أَحْمَد) (“more praising than all”) — he is the one who praises God more perfectly than anyone, and who deserves more praise than anyone among the creatures. The maqām (مَقَام) al-Maḥmūd (the praised station) is promised to him on the Day of Judgement. For the Sufis, the supreme ḥamd is the one that asks for nothing — that celebrates in order to celebrate, that rejoices in God for His own sake. It is the essential praise, that of the saints who have understood that God is, and that this suffices for all joy.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Praise be to God, Lord of the worlds.
The best dhikr (الذِّكْر) is: Lā ilāha illā Llāh (لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّٰه); the best invocation is: al-ḥamdu li-Llāh.

entries.meditation: Can I praise God for what He is, without asking anything in return?

entries.racine_sens: to know, to know by study
entries.definition_concise: Science — knowledge acquired by study, transmission or reasoning. Distinct from maʿrifa (gnosis, knowledge by experience). The Sufis often say: ʿilm without maʿrifa is vain; but maʿrifa without ʿilm is dangerous.
entries.sens_profond: The hadith says: “The seeking of ʿilm is obligatory for every Muslim.” But the ʿilm of which classical Islam speaks is not modern information — it is a transforming knowledge, which brings the one who knows to another state of being. The Quran praises the learned: “Among His servants, only the learned fear Him” (35:28). But a distinction: there is transmitted ʿilm (Quran, hadith, law, language — what one learns from others) and unveiled ʿilm (what one discovers within oneself). The Sufis use a still more subtle distinction: ʿilm al-yaqīn (عِلْمُ اليَقِين) (certain knowledge, by sign), ʿayn al-yaqīn (عَيْنُ اليَقِين) (certain vision, by unveiling), ḥaqq al-yaqīn (حَقُّ اليَقِين) (certain truth, by fusion). Maʿrifa rises above ʿilm as experience above information. But ʿilm remains the foundation — without correct grammar, no right recitation; without studied law, no correct practice; without knowledge of the masters, no discernment.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The seeking of ʿilm is an obligation for every Muslim.
ʿilm is the lamp; maʿrifa is what the lamp allows one to see. Do not confuse the container with the content.

entries.meditation: What I know — has it transformed me, or only enriched me?

entries.racine_sens: to judge, to set in order — hence wisdom as just judgement
entries.definition_concise: Wisdom — a knowledge that knows how to set each thing in its just place. In Islam (الإِسْلَام), the equivalent of the Greek philosophia prima: metaphysics in the noble sense. Suhrawardī, Mullā Ṣadrā and Ibn Sīnā are ḥukamāʾ (حُكَمَاء).
entries.sens_profond: The Quran praises those to whom God has given ḥikma: “And he to whom wisdom is given has received much good” (2:269). Ḥikma is not only to know much — it is to know how to act rightly. The sage (ḥakīm (حَكِيم)) knows the proper place of each thing in the order of creation: what belongs to science, what belongs to faith, what belongs to action. For the Islamic tradition, ḥikma has two faces: theoretical ḥikma (metaphysics — the knowledge of first principles) and practical ḥikma (ethics — knowing how to live in harmony with those principles). Suhrawardī speaks of ḥikmat al-ishrāq (حِكْمَةُ الإِشْرَاق) (the wisdom of illumination), Mullā Ṣadrā of al-ḥikma al-mutaʿāliya (الحِكْمَةُ المُتَعَالِيَة) (the transcendent wisdom). For Nasr and the perennialist school, ḥikma = sophia perennis, the eternal wisdom that says itself again across the traditions. The authentic sage is rare: he lives what he knows, without dissociation.

voix_des_maitres.texte: He gives wisdom to whom He wills; and he to whom wisdom is given has received much good.
The word of wisdom is the lost property of the believer; wherever he finds it, he has a right to it.

entries.meditation: What I know — do I also know how to live it?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as maʿrifa — to know by experience
entries.definition_concise: A Persian-Shia synonym of maʿrifa. It denotes particularly the Iranian gnostic tradition — Mullā Ṣadrā, Sabzawārī, ʿAllāmah Ṭabāṭabāʾī. The ʿārif (عَارِف) or ʿirfān-shenās is the gnostic in the Iranian sense.
entries.sens_profond: The word ʿirfān, in Persian and Turkish, largely covers the same semantic field as maʿrifa in Arabic — the direct knowledge of God. But it has, in Iranian usage, particular connotations: it often denotes the Twelver Shia school of mystical metaphysics, which synthesises kalām (كَلَام) (theology), falsafa (Peripatetic philosophy) and taṣawwuf (التَّصَوُّف) (Sufism). Its great representatives: Mullā Ṣadrā (d. 1640) with his ḥikma (حِكْمَة) al-mutaʿāliya, Sabzawārī, and in the 20th century ʿAllāmah Ṭabāṭabāʾī (d. 1981), Khomeini himself as a metaphysician. Iranian ʿirfān has the particularity of having institutionalised the esoteric transmission within clerical formation itself: an accomplished Shia ʿālim (عَالِم) is often also an ʿārif. It is this tradition that Henry Corbin and Seyyed Hossein Nasr made known to the West.

voix_des_maitres.texte: ʿirfān is not a school against philosophy; it is philosophy carried to its summit.
Iranian ʿirfān has kept alive a metaphysics of light that the West has lost.

entries.meditation: If the highest knowledge is also the most intimate — why have so many scholars never heard of it?

entries.racine_sens: to see — hence baṣīra as penetrating sight
entries.definition_concise: The penetrating sight that passes through appearances. Not physical sight (baṣar (بَصَر)), but that spiritual lucidity which sees the true beneath the false, the intention beneath the act, the essence beneath the form.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran (12:108) says: “This is my way: I call to God upon baṣīra (in full lucidity), I and those who follow me.” Baṣīra is what distinguishes the conscious follower from the blind follower: one acts not out of habit or pressure, but because one sees what one is doing. It is, among the Sufis, the proper gift of the eye of the heart (ʿayn al-qalb (عَيْنُ القَلْب)). The hadith says: “Fear the baṣīra of the believer — for he looks with the Light of God.” The accomplished saint, through his baṣīra, can read hearts: he knows, before you, where you are. This capacity is frightening; but it is never used to judge — it serves to help, because it sees also the right help. Baṣīra develops through practice: the less one is encumbered with oneself, the more clearly one sees. It is the eye that opens as the veil of the self grows thin.

voix_des_maitres.texte: This is my way: I call to God upon baṣīra (in full lucidity), I and those who follow me.
Fear the baṣīra of the believer — he looks with the Light of God.

entries.meditation: Is there someone whose gaze, upon me, sees more than what I show — and have I listened to him?

entries.racine_sens: to think, to meditate
entries.definition_concise: The meditative thought that lingers upon the signs (āyāt) — of the Quran, of nature, of the soul. A practice praised by the Prophet: “An hour of tafakkur (التَّفَكُّر) is worth seventy years of worship.”
entries.sens_profond: Tafakkur is different from the rapid reasoning that solves a problem — it is the dwelling of the mind upon an object, without haste to leave it. The Quran calls for it on numerous occasions: “They meditate upon the creation of the heavens and the earth” (3:191); “Why do they not meditate the Quran?” (4:82); “In the creation of the heavens and the earth, in the succession of day and night, there are indeed signs for people endowed with intelligence — those who invoke God standing, sitting or lying on their side, and who meditate upon the creation of the heavens and the earth.” Tafakkur is that practice which transforms mere reading into reception, mere looking into contemplation. The Sufis consider it one of the three major disciplines of the heart, alongside dhikr (الذِّكْر) (remembrance) and murāqaba (vigilance). An hour of tafakkur — say the masters — is worth seventy years of distracted worship. Why? Because it opens the inner eye; without it, the rituals remain external.

voix_des_maitres.texte: An hour of tafakkur is worth more than seventy years of worship.
Tafakkur is the key that opens the door of unveiling; without it, the acts remain barren.

entries.meditation: When did I last think, without haste to leave it, about a single thing?

entries.racine: ن ف س + أ م ر
entries.racine_sens: nafs (soul) + ammāra (commanding, that orders)
entries.definition_concise: The first degree of the nafs (النَّفْس) (Quran 12:53). The soul given over to its passions, which commands evil: appetites, anger, pride, jealousy. It is the state of most human beings before any spiritual work.
entries.sens_profond: Verse 12:53 cites the words of Joseph: “I do not exonerate my soul — the soul is inclined to evil, save the one upon which my Lord has had mercy.” The nafs ammāra is the primitive state: not through an original perversity, but through dispersion. As long as the human being lets himself be led by his appetites, his anger, his desire for recognition, he lives under the reign of this nafs. It is not the demon (which is external) — it is the inner enemy, more dangerous because one identifies it with oneself. But beware: one does not kill the nafs ammāra (it is the matter of transformation); one tames it. It is what the Sufi way calls al-jihād (الجِهَاد) al-akbar, the great combat. The fruits of the taming are the nafs lawwāma (نَفْسٌ لَوَّامَة) (the soul that reproaches itself) — the one that sees its own failings and blames itself for them — then, higher, the nafs muṭmaʾinna (نَفْسٌ مُطْمَئِنَّة) (the soul at peace). The Sufi way has elaborated seven degrees, but these three — ammāra, lawwāma, muṭmaʾinna — are the Quranic pivots.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I do not exonerate my soul — the soul is greatly inclined to evil, save the one upon which my Lord has had mercy.
We return from the lesser combat toward the greater combat. — What is the greater combat? — The combat against the nafs.

entries.meditation: Which impulse did I obey this morning, without noticing that it was commanding me?

entries.racine: ن ف س + ل و م
entries.racine_sens: nafs + lawwāma (that blames, that reproaches itself)
entries.definition_concise: The second degree of the nafs (النَّفْس) (Quran 75:2). The soul awakened to its own mediocrity, which sees itself doing evil and blames itself for it — the tipping point between unconsciousness and spiritual awareness.
entries.sens_profond: Verse 75:2 says: “I swear by the soul that reproaches itself.” That God should swear by this soul is a sign: the nafs lawwāma (نَفْسٌ لَوَّامَة) is not a fallen state — it is an awakening. The person at the stage of nafs ammāra does not see herself: she acts, but does not question. The person at the stage of nafs lawwāma sees herself — and what she sees in herself, she no longer accepts. It is painful; it is precious. This pain is what makes one advance. Scruple, remorse, dissatisfaction with oneself: all are signs that the nafs lawwāma is at work. But there is danger: one must traverse this stage, not stop at it. Too much lawwāma without mercy becomes spiritual morbidity — the pathological scrupulous person who forgives himself nothing, which is also a form of inverted ego. The way asks one to see oneself without hating oneself, to acknowledge without condemning oneself — water and fire in balance.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I swear by the soul that reproaches itself.
A fault that leads you to humility is worth more than an obedience that leads you to pride.

entries.meditation: When I blame myself — do I grow, or do I lower myself?

entries.racine: ن ف س + ط م ن
entries.racine_sens: nafs + muṭmaʾinna (at peace, tranquil, reassured)
entries.definition_concise: The third degree of the nafs (النَّفْس) (Quran 89:27). The soul returned to God, accepted and content. At peace not because it has everything, but because it has found its place. It is the state of the accomplished saints.
entries.sens_profond: Verse 89:27-30 is one of the most beautiful in the Quran: “O soul at peace, return to your Lord, content and accepted. Enter among My servants, enter into My Paradise.” Four attributes meet there: muṭmaʾinna (مُطْمَئِنَّة) (at peace), rāḍiya (رَاضِيَة) (content), marḍiyya (مَرْضِيَّة) (accepted), ʿabd (عَبْد) (servant). The soul at peace is no longer pulled by its passions (it has traversed the ammāra), no longer torn by remorse (it has traversed the lawwāma): it is established. But this peace is not lukewarmness — it is an active fullness, a quiet joy that no longer depends on circumstances. For the Sufis, it marks the entry into high sainthood. Beyond it, some masters distinguish four further degrees (mulhama, rāḍiya, marḍiyya, kāmila) for the very accomplished. But already, to reach the muṭmaʾinna is the work of a lifetime. And the sign is clear: one is no longer shaken by what shook one before, one acts without tension, one loves without possessing, one loses without collapsing.

voix_des_maitres.texte: O soul at peace, return to your Lord, content and accepted. Enter among My servants, enter into My Paradise.
The soul at peace tastes peace before Paradise — Paradise is the confirmation of what it already has.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a place that no longer trembles — and what does it live when the rest trembles?

entries.racine_sens: to hide, to conceal
entries.definition_concise: The deepest of the subtle organs of the heart — beyond the sirr (السِّرّ). There where one is no longer even aware of what one carries, where the meeting with God takes place with no witness, not even the one one is to oneself.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufi cartography of the heart distinguishes several layers: ṣadr (صَدْر) (the breast), qalb (القَلْب) (the heart), fuʾād (فُؤَاد) (the inmost heart), lubb (the core), sirr (the secret), sirr al-sirr (سِرُّ السِّرّ) (the secret of the secret), khafī (the hidden), and sometimes akhfā (أَخْفَى) (the most-hidden). The khafī is deeper than the sirr. If the sirr is the place where one knows one has a secret with God, the khafī is the place where one knows nothing more of it — the veil covers even the awareness of the deposit. It is there, for the masters, that what is incommunicable is received — not because it would need to be hidden, but because no word, not even an inner one, can formulate it. The Naqshbandiyya in particular has developed a precise map of the laṭāʾif (لَطَائِف) (subtleties) of the heart, of which the khafī is one of the highest. The saint established in the khafī, says the tradition, lives in a presence so pure that it no longer even needs to be thought as presence.

voix_des_maitres.texte: My servant has a secret with Me that no one knows.
The deeper the soul sinks, the less it speaks; at the khafī, it has even forgotten its tongue.

entries.meditation: Is there within me something that I carry without even knowing that I carry it?

entries.racine_sens: to be inattentive, negligent
entries.definition_concise: The forgetting of God, the distraction that prevents the awareness of His presence. It is the major illness of the soul — the exact opposite of dhikr (الذِّكْر) (remembrance). All the Sufi way is, in a sense, a struggle against ghafla.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran warns: “And do not be among the ghāfilīn (the forgetful).” Ghafla is not only a passing forgetfulness — it is a mode of being, in which one passes through the day without presence to what is happening, without presence to God, without presence to oneself. It is the default mode of a modern humanity saturated with information. The hadith says: “People are asleep; when they die, they awaken.” That sleep is ghafla. The Sufis identify several causes: the nafs (النَّفْس) (which turns one toward its desires), attachment to the dunyā (which overloads), inattention to time (which one kills instead of inhabiting), distracted company (which contaminates). And the remedies: dhikr (constant recollection), murāqaba (vigilance), suḥba (the company of the awakened). Ghafla is insidious: one always believes oneself to be coming out of it, when one is only at its surface — it takes a continuous vigilance, without tension, truly to inhabit one's own instant. Hadith: “Remember God until they say: he is mad.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: People are asleep; when they die, they awaken.
The aspirant who suffers from his ghafla has already begun to come out of it; the one who does not even see it is engulfed in it.

entries.meditation: During these last hours — how long have I truly been present?

entries.racine_sens: bond, connection (from the root meaning to bind)
entries.definition_concise: The canonical prayer — one of the five pillars of Islam, performed five times a day according to precise postures. For the Sufis, it is far more than a ritual: it is the privileged moment of the meeting, the ascension of the believer.
entries.sens_profond: The word ṣalāt probably comes from the root ṣ-l-w which evokes the bond. To pray is to bind oneself — in the strong sense, as one re-establishes a communication. The Prophet said: “The ṣalāt is the miʿrāj (the ascension) of the believer” — a reference to his own nocturnal journey to the heavens. At each prayer, the believer re-enacts, in his measure, that ascension. For the Sufis, the badly performed ṣalāt is purely external: one recites, one prostrates, but one is elsewhere. The hadith says: “How many who pray have of their prayer only the fatigue.” The well-performed ṣalāt is presence: each word carried, each posture inhabited, each silence received. The fātiḥa (الفَاتِحَة) (the opening sūra) is recited at each rakʿa — each verse is an exchange with God, according to the qudsī hadith. The degrees of the ṣalāt according to al-Ghazālī: prayer of the body alone (mechanical), prayer of the body and the tongue (conscious recitation), prayer of the body and the heart (presence), prayer of the whole soul (in which one disappears into the rite).

voix_des_maitres.texte: The ṣalāt is the miʿrāj (the ascension) of the believer.
How many who pray have of their prayer only the fatigue; how many who pray have of their prayer the vision of their Lord.

entries.meditation: When I last prayed — was I truly there?

entries.racine_sens: to hold back, to abstain
entries.definition_concise: The ritual abstinence from food, drink and carnal pleasure from sunrise to sunset — a major practice during the month of Ramadan. For the Sufis, it is also a school of the mastery of the nafs (النَّفْس).
entries.sens_profond: The qudsī hadith says: “Fasting is for Me, and it is I who reward it.” An unusual distinction: all other acts are attributed to the servant (“his prayer,” “his alms”), only fasting is attributed to God. Why? Because it is an invisible act — no one knows, save God, whether you truly fast or whether you eat in secret. Fasting is therefore the school of sincerity par excellence. The Sufis distinguish three fasts: the fast of the body (to abstain from eating), the fast of the tongue and the organs (to abstain also from lying, slander, the covetous gaze), and the fast of the heart (to abstain from any thought turned toward anything other than God). The first without the others bears little fruit. And fasting is not limited to Ramadan — many Sufis practise the ṣawm dawūdī (صَوْمٌ دَاوُودِيّ) (the fast of David: every other day), or weekly fasts (Monday and Thursday). Beyond that still, some speak of the ṣawm al-khāṣṣ (صَوْمُ الخَاصّ) — a perpetual fasting of the senses, which never ceases.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Fasting is for Me, and it is I who reward it.
The fasting of the common is to abstain from food; the fasting of the elite is to abstain from pleasures; the fasting of the lovers is to abstain from all that is not Him.

entries.meditation: From what could I fast, today, that would draw me closer to the essential?

entries.racine_sens: to reckon, to make the account
entries.definition_concise: The examination of the soul — a practice consisting in doing one's accounts before God does them. A daily practice, generally in the evening: one reviews one's acts, one's words, one's intentions of the day.
entries.sens_profond: ʿUmar ibn al-Khaṭṭāb said: “Reckon with yourselves (ḥāsibū) before you are called to account.” Muḥāsaba is the retrospective counterpart of murāqaba (vigilance): murāqaba is attention to the present, muḥāsaba is the examination of the past. The two complete each other. Concretely: in the evening, the traveller sits down, reviews the hours that have passed, identifies his faults (committed in word, in act, in thought, in intention), asks forgiveness, takes resolution. But beware — just muḥāsaba is neither complacent (“I was rather good today”) nor morbid (“I am wretched”). It is lucid, precise, without drama, like an accountant looking at a ledger. Al-Muḥāsibī (d. 857) is the Sufi master who wrote the reference treatise on this practice (al-Riʿāya li-ḥuqūq Allāh (اَللَّٰه) — “The Care for the rights of God”). His very name comes from this practice: al-Muḥāsibī (المُحَاسِبِيّ), the one who reckoned with himself.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Reckon with yourselves before you are called to account; weigh your acts before they are weighed.
The unexamined soul is left to itself; the examined soul is given over to God.

entries.meditation: If I did the accounts of this day — what weighs, and what flies away?

entries.racine_sens: to come, to arrive at a watering place
entries.definition_concise: The body of invocations, verses and prayers assigned to a disciple by his shaykh (الشَّيْخ), to be recited each day at fixed hours. A daily discipline that structures the spiritual life.
entries.sens_profond: The root w-r-d evokes the watering place where one comes to drink — the wird is, by image, that point of daily return to the source. Each ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة) has its own wird: they may include chosen sūras of the Quran, formulas of tasbīḥ (subḥān Allāh (اَللَّٰه)…), of istighfār (the asking of forgiveness), of ṣalāt upon the Prophet, and a dhikr (الذِّكْر) of the Name (often Lā ilāha illā Llāh (لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّٰه) or Allāh). Regularity is crucial: the hadith says that “the work most beloved of God is the most regular, even if it is small.” A modest wird kept every day is worth more than a great wird abandoned. The wird is not magical — it is discipline, which carves a furrow in the soul. At first, one recites mechanically; after weeks, one begins to hear what one is saying; after months, the wird begins to live. And one day, without one's noticing it, it is the wird that recites you — you are no longer anything but the breath through which it passes. It is also through the wird that the baraka (البَرَكَة) of the shaykh is transmitted across the generations: a wird sometimes reaches back several centuries, through an unbroken silsila (السِّلْسِلَة).

voix_des_maitres.texte: The work most beloved of God is the most regular, even if it is small.
Do not abandon the wird on the pretext that you are not present within it — your presence without the wird is worth less than your absence with the wird.

entries.meditation: Is there a simple practice, kept every day, that would bring me back to myself?

entries.racine_sens: to direct oneself toward, to aim
entries.definition_concise: The inner intention that precedes and orients every act. The most famous hadith: “Acts are worth only by their intentions, and each one will have only what he intended.”
entries.sens_profond: The hadith of ʿUmar — which opens almost all the collections — sets out a fundamental principle: it is not the act that counts, it is what inhabits it. Two people pray side by side: one is in the presence of God, the other wants to be seen; the gesture is identical, the value is opposite. For the Sufis, the niyya (النِّيَّة) always precedes the act (in the morning, one sets the niyya of the day; before each prayer, one sets it anew), but it also imbues the act from within — it is not declared once, it is renewed at every second. Three levels of intention according to the classical authors: to act out of fear of chastisement (the intention of the common); to act out of hope of reward (the intention of the devout); to act for God Himself, without fear or hope, because He is worthy of it (the intention of the lovers — it is Rābiʿa's intention). Pure niyya is rare; it is also the key. The ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص) (sincerity) is precisely the purity of the niyya — that no other motive mingles with it.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Acts are worth only by their intentions, and each one will have only what he intended.

My God, if I worship You out of fear of hell, burn me in it; if I worship You out of hope of paradise, exclude me from it; but if I worship You for Your own sake, do not deprive me of Your eternal Beauty.

entries.meditation: Why am I reading these words — for what true intention?

entries.racine_sens: to preserve, to protect — hence taqwā as a protecting attitude
entries.definition_concise: The reverential awareness of God — a fundamental attitude that in Arabic means far more than “piety.” The muttaqī (مُتَّقِي) is the one who walks carefully, who does not lose God from sight.
entries.sens_profond: The root w-q-y is that of protection: taqwā (تَقْوَى) is the attitude that protects you from what would separate you from God. It is more than a virtue: it is a tonality of being. The Quran (49:13) says: “The noblest among you, before God, is the most pious (atqā).” This verse annuls every distinction of birth or of rank: taqwā alone makes nobility. ʿAlī (اَلْعَلِيّ) gave this unforgettable image: “Taqwā is like walking on a thorny path — you raise your garments, you set down each step with attention, you do not rush.” The muttaqī is not a fearful one — he is an attentive one. He knows that he is under the gaze; he knows that his choices have consequences; he knows that every instant may draw him near or far. This attention transforms the walking into a path. Without taqwā, even the best works remain superficial. With it, even small works become great. Hadith: “Taqwā is here” — the Prophet said, pointing to his heart, three times.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The noblest among you, before God, is the most pious.
Taqwā is like walking on a thorny path: you raise your garments, you set down each step with attention.

entries.meditation: If I were walking on a thorny path — how would I set down the next step?

entries.racine_sens: to make pure, to free from impurities
entries.definition_concise: The purity of intention — that no external motive mingle with the act accomplished for God. Sūrat al-Ikhlāṣ (sūra 112) bears its name. For the Sufis, it is the summit of practice.
entries.sens_profond: The root kh-l-ṣ means to separate, to purify by eliminating the mixtures. Ikhlāṣ is that purity of intention which has no admixture: neither the seeking of human praise, nor material gain, nor the satisfaction of the ego. You act for Him alone, because He is worthy of it. The hadith says: “God accepts no work unless it is done with ikhlāṣ and conforms to the sunna.” Two inseparable conditions: inner (ikhlāṣ) and outer (conformity to the way). But ikhlāṣ is paradoxical: as soon as one believes oneself sincere, one ceases to be so (since one secretly boasts of one's sincerity). The Sufis therefore speak of ikhlāṣ al-ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاصُ الإِخْلَاص) — the sincerity of sincerity, in which one is delivered even from the awareness of being sincere. It is very rare. Sūrat al-Ikhlāṣ (Qul huwa-Llāhu Aḥad (اَلْأَحَد)…) is recited as a protection — it sets out verbally what ikhlāṣ sets out existentially: God Alone, without associate, without admixture, without sharing.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Acts are worth only by the intentions; and each one will have only what he intended.
Ikhlāṣ is a pearl in the sea — to take it, you must disappear into the sea.

entries.meditation: Is there in what I do today a single act truly pure of any other motive?

entries.racine_sens: to speak truly, to be true
entries.definition_concise: Truthfulness — not only to speak the true, but to be true: that there be no division between what one says, what one thinks, what one does, what one is. For the Sufis, ṣidq is the very foundation of the way.
entries.sens_profond: The title aṣ-Ṣiddīq (الصِّدِّيق) (the utterly truthful) was given to Abū Bakr, the first successor of the Prophet. Ṣidq goes beyond merely “not lying”: it is integrity, in the etymological sense of integral — in which all the dimensions of the being are in accord. The liar speaks falsely; the boaster speaks truly but with exaggeration; the diplomat speaks truly but watered down; the ṣiddīq speaks truly simply, with no more and no less. And more still: his word is true because his being is true. The hadith says: “Ṣidq leads to piety, and piety leads to Paradise.” Three ṣidq according to the classical authors: ṣidq of the tongue (to never lie — including to oneself, which is the hardest); ṣidq of the heart (that the feelings be what they claim to be — no feigned love, no feigned respect); ṣidq of the acts (that the acts confirm the words). The accomplished ṣiddīq, says the tradition, does not even lie in his dreams — his very night is woven of truth.

voix_des_maitres.texte: O you who believe, fear God, and be with the truthful.
The ṣiddīq can be turned over a thousand ways without ceasing to be true; the liar turns over only once, and falls.

entries.meditation: When did I last lie — including to myself?

entries.racine_sens: to serve
entries.definition_concise: The service rendered to others — particularly to one's brothers on the way and to the shaykh (الشَّيْخ). For the Sufis, service is the practical school of humility: one must do before one is.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufi way is run through by a simple intuition: you will not progress by your meditations alone. One must serve. To serve the shaykh, to serve the brothers, to serve the poor, to serve the community. Service is the antidote of the nafs (النَّفْس), which always wants to be at the centre, to be served, to be recognised. To serve is to place oneself below — and it is through that descent that the soul grows light. The Chishtiyya (India) made khidma a major pillar: their khanaqāhs (zawiyas) fed thousands of poor people every day for centuries. Hadith: “The master of a people is the one who serves them.” And another: “Whoever lightens the burden of a believer in this lower world, God will lighten his burden on the Day of Judgement.” But beware: authentic khidma is without expected return, without publicity, without ego-satisfaction. To do good in secret, like a hidden action between oneself and God — that is pure khidma. And the more of it you do discreetly, the more the baraka (البَرَكَة) accumulates in the hidden.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The master of a people is the one who serves them.
Service is the oration of the lovers — they pray with their hands.

entries.meditation: Whom have I served today without anyone knowing it?

entries.racine_sens: to be sad, grieved
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual sadness — not depression nor melancholy, but a fruitful pain of the heart which measures the distance from God and thereby advances toward Him. A state often considered a sign of spiritual progress.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran sets the tone: Jacob, losing Joseph, wept so much that his eyes went out, and God praised his beautiful patience. Sadness, in the Islamic tradition, is not a sin — it is a depth. The hadith says: “God loves the sorrowful heart.” Why? Because the sorrowful heart is awakened; the superficially joyful heart is often asleep, satisfied, distracted. Sufi ḥuzn is neither morbidity nor depression — it is that fine pain of one who knows how far he is from the Beloved. It is the sister of shawq (الشَّوْق) (ardent longing). The hadith says: “Whoever is saddened with a sadness for love of Me, I shall give him the joy of My gaze.” An important distinction of the Sufis: there is the ḥuzn for God (fruitful) and the ḥuzn for lost things (which impoverishes). The first is encouraged, the second is to be transformed. The accomplished saint often carries this gentle sadness — visible in his gaze — which is in reality the other face of his deep joy: he knows what others do not yet know.

voix_des_maitres.texte: God loves the sorrowful heart.
Sadness for God is light; sadness for what leaves you is darkness.

entries.meditation: The sadness I carry — is it for Him, or for the things that leave me?

entries.racine_sens: to dread, to respect with a seizing
entries.definition_concise: The reverential seizing before the divine majesty — distinct from fear (khawf (الخَوْف)), which is more precise. Hayba is that sacred amazement which makes the soul draw back before the greatness glimpsed.
entries.sens_profond: If khawf is the precise fear (fear of a chastisement, of an estrangement), hayba is the diffuse seizing before the Mystery. When the servant glimpses, were it but for an instant, the greatness of his Lord, he is seized — not by fear, but by the disproportion. It is what is sometimes called awe in the Christian traditions. Hayba is coupled by the Sufis with uns (الأُنْس) (intimacy): these two states alternate in the servant. Now he feels the majesty (jalāl (جَلَال)) and is seized with hayba; now he feels the beauty (jamāl) and is in uns. Neither of the two is definitive — it is in their balance that spiritual maturity is measured. Too much hayba without uns paralyses; too much uns without hayba lulls to sleep. Hadith: “If you knew what I know, you would laugh little and weep much.” It is the effect of hayba upon the one who has seen.

voix_des_maitres.texte: If you knew what I know, you would laugh little and weep much.
Hayba is the sister of uns — one holds back, the other draws; the traveller comes and goes between the two.

entries.meditation: When was I last seized — without being able to put words to it?

entries.racine: ق ب ض — ب س ط
entries.racine_sens: qabḍ: to clasp, to close the hand; basṭ: to extend, to open the hand
entries.definition_concise: The pair of alternating spiritual states — contraction (qabḍ (قَبْض), the tightened heart) and expansion (basṭ, the dilated heart) — which give rhythm to the inner life of the traveller, without his being able to command their coming.
entries.sens_profond: Qabḍ and basṭ correspond to the two divine Names al-Qābiḍ (اَلْقَابِض) (He who Contracts) and al-Bāsiṭ (اَلْبَاسِط) (He who Expands). Every servant, on the way, knows their alternations: days on which the heart is narrow, heavy, as if shut (qabḍ); days on which it is ample, joyful, as if open onto the horizon (basṭ). The beginner's error: to believe that in qabḍ he has regressed, and that in basṭ he has arrived. The master's truth: neither of the two is definitive, and each has its use. Qabḍ deepens (the fruits of qabḍ are rarely recognised at the moment); basṭ rewards and gives the strength to continue. Hujwīrī compares the traveller to a bird: he has two wings, qabḍ and basṭ. Without qabḍ, he does not hold; without basṭ, he does not fly. For the advanced masters, qabḍ becomes even more precious than basṭ — it penetrates deeper, and it frees one from attachment to the states. To reach a stability beyond qabḍ and basṭ is to establish oneself in a joy that no longer depends on moods.

voix_des_maitres.texte: And it is God who contracts (yaqbiḍu) and who expands (yabsuṭu); and it is to Him that you will be brought back.
Qabḍ and basṭ are the two wings — the bird that has only one does not fly.

entries.meditation: There, now — is my heart tightened or dilated, and what does this state tell me?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as ʿishq — passionate love
entries.definition_concise: The lover — the one who journeys by the way of ardent love (ʿishq (عِشْق)). A particular human type in Sufism: not the scholar (ʿālim (عَالِم)) nor the gnostic (ʿārif (عَارِف)), but the one whom love consumes.
entries.sens_profond: Three great figures of the traveller in the tradition: the ʿālim (the scholar — who knows by study), the ʿārif (the gnostic — who knows by unveiling), the ʿāshiq (the lover — who knows by love). For many Sufis, this last is the highest, because his engine is not knowledge but the burning. The ʿāshiq does not seek to understand — he loves. And that love gives him knowledge, without his having pursued it. It is the way of Rūmī, of Rābiʿa, of Ḥallāj, of Yūnus Emre. The ʿāshiq is often mad in the eyes of the world — Majnūn became mad from loving Laylā, the Sufi became mad from loving God. This madness is in reality another wisdom, which surpasses calculation. But there is danger: the ʿāshiq without discipline, without knowledge, without a master may stray into vague emotions that he takes for love. True ʿishq is burning and lucid at once — the flame and the eye.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The lover is a madman — but his madness is the other face of the wisdom of God.
I am neither Muslim nor unbeliever — I have become mad with love, I no longer belong to anything.

entries.meditation: Am I in love with something, at this instant — in the sense that my heart burns?

entries.racine_sens: the same root — the object of passionate love
entries.definition_concise: The Beloved — the one who is loved with ardent love. In the Sufi way, it denotes God, toward whom all authentic love converges. The ʿāshiq seeks his maʿshūq; the union is waṣl.
entries.sens_profond: The maʿshūq is the pole of the ʿāshiq's love. In Sufi poetry, particularly Persian (Ḥāfiẓ, Rūmī, Saʿdī), He is denoted by images borrowed from human love: the Beloved, the Beautiful One, the Cupbearer, the Face. Why this “carnal” imagery? For two reasons: first because human love is, for those who live it, the most intense experience — it is therefore the best earthly mirror of divine love. Second because this imagery protects the secret: a poem of Ḥāfiẓ can be read as an ordinary love song, and the hidden meaning reveals itself only to the initiate. The distinction is crucial: for the Sufi, the Beloved is not a metaphor for God — God is the reality of which all human beauty is only the trace. To reverse this movement is to lose oneself. The beloved woman, the cherished friend, the adored child may be paths toward the divine maʿshūq; they can never replace Him.

voix_des_maitres.texte: All human beauty is a trace; every trace is an invitation to the Beloved who hides behind it.
The Beloved is not in the face you seek — the face is the echo.

entries.meditation: All that I love — is it that which I love, or something else through it?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as tawḥīd — union
entries.definition_concise: The apparent fusion of the servant into God — a controversial concept. The intoxicated (sukārā) speak of it as a reality; the sober (ṣaḥw) reject it as an imperfect expression. A subtle distinction with tawḥīd (تَوْحِيد).
entries.sens_profond: Ittiḥād is, in certain ecstatic utterances (shaṭaḥāt), the expression of an experience in which the lover feels himself melted into the Beloved: “I am the True” (Ḥallāj), “Beneath my garment, there is nothing but God” (Bisṭāmī). These utterances have been understood by some as doctrinal affirmations of an ontological identification — God and the saint having become a single reality. But the great tradition (notably Ibn ʿArabī, despite his reputation) has always qualified this: there is no fusion, because the creature remains a creature and God remains God. What disappears in the experience is the awareness of duality — not duality itself. The servant, in fanāʾ (فَنَاء), no longer sees himself as distinct; but he still is, ontologically. This is why al-Junayd, the sober one, corrected Ḥallāj: “he spoke of what he saw, but he should not have said it.” Ittiḥād is therefore to be understood as lived, not as doctrine. The doctrine is tawḥīd (Unity within distinction). Ittiḥād is the lightning flash that makes one feel, in an instant, that nothing else is.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I am He who loves, and He who loves is I; we are two spirits incarnate in a single body.
Tawḥīd is to separate the eternal from the temporal; ittiḥād is to believe there is no longer any separation — the first saying is just, the second is a vision one must know how to veil.

entries.meditation: When did I forget who I was — and what within me permitted it?

entries.racine: ح ق ق — ي ق ن
entries.racine_sens: ḥaqq (reality, truth) + yaqīn (certainty)
entries.definition_concise: The third and highest degree of certainty (yaqīn): no longer only to know or to see, but to be within. Like being within the fire, and not seeing its smoke or its flame.
entries.sens_profond: Sūra 102 distinguishes three degrees: ʿilm al-yaqīn (عِلْمُ اليَقِين) (to know by sign), ʿayn al-yaqīn (عَيْنُ اليَقِين) (to see directly), ḥaqq (حَقّ) al-yaqīn (to be united with reality). For the Sufis, this third degree is the total experience — in which there is no longer any distance between the knower and the known, in which knowledge and existence merge. The classic image: three people speak of fire. The first knows there is a fire because he sees its smoke (ʿilm al-yaqīn). The second sees the flame itself (ʿayn al-yaqīn). The third is within the fire — he is burned, he is consumed (ḥaqq al-yaqīn). This last knowledge is unexchangeable, undesertable, indubitable. It is the knowledge of the great saints, who no longer argue because they are what they teach. It is also why their word, even the briefest, carries such weight: it comes from the ḥaqq al-yaqīn, and not from rumour.

voix_des_maitres.texte: This is indeed the truth of certainty (ḥaqq al-yaqīn).
ʿilm is the echo; ʿayn is the glimpse; ḥaqq is the flame that passes through you.

entries.meditation: Am I within what I know — or only facing it?

entries.racine_sens: death
entries.definition_concise: The central Sufi expression: “To die before dying” — a reference to the hadith. To die to oneself (to the nafs (النَّفْس), to attachments, to illusion) before physical death occurs — it is fanāʾ (فَنَاء) itself.
entries.sens_profond: The hadith says: “Die before you die” (mūtū qabla an tamūtū). This brief saying is the key to the whole Sufi way. You will die one day; everyone dies; but the sage dies before, voluntarily, in order to enter, from this very life, into the light. To die to what? To the nafs (which believes itself the centre), to the attachments (which hold you prisoner), to the illusion (that your I is what you truly are). This death is what the Sufis call fanāʾ — the extinction of the self in God. It is not suicide nor depression: it is a rebirth, because in place of the I that dies, it is the deep Self, the rūḥ (رُوح), the appearing of God in the human being, that begins to breathe. Rūmī sang it tirelessly: “As long as the servant does not die, he is not resurrected.” And: “I was dead, I became alive; I was weeping, I became laughter; the joys of love came, I became eternal joy.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: Die before you die (mūtū qabla an tamūtū).
As long as the servant does not die, he is not resurrected.

entries.meditation: What within me should die so that something may truly be born?

entries.racine_sens: chain, linkage
entries.definition_concise: The chain of spiritual transmission which, from master to master, reaches back to the Prophet. It is through it that the baraka (البَرَكَة) and the authentic teaching are transmitted living across the centuries.
entries.sens_profond: The hadith says: “Knowledge is a deposit; one takes it from human beings, not from leaves of paper.” To read books is not enough — one must receive from a master who has himself received. That is the silsila (السِّلْسِلَة): an unbroken chain of awakened souls, each transmitting to the next what is not written. The disciple, in attaching himself to a shaykh (الشَّيْخ), attaches himself not only to a man — he attaches himself to a whole lineage, of which his shaykh is the last living link. Each ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة) has its own silsila, and preserves it with care. Most reach back to the Prophet through the intermediary of ʿAlī (sometimes also through Abū Bakr — this is the case of the Naqshbandiyya). When the silsila breaks — when a master dies without having transmitted to an authentic successor — it is the end of a living lineage, even if books remain. This is why the Sufis insist: it is through the silsila, and not through books, that the way is perpetuated. The transmission is oral, present, secret in certain respects — and always living.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Knowledge is a deposit; one takes it from human beings, not from leaves of paper.
A living silsila is more precious than all books; a book without a silsila is a sea without a boat.

entries.meditation: Through whom has what I know that is most essential reached me — and to whom will I transmit it?

entries.racine_sens: to take a path, to walk
entries.definition_concise: The wayfaring of the disciple upon the way — the methodical practice that leads him from station to station. The sālik (سَالِك) (the traveller) is the one who walks, unlike the mere curious one or the scholar in the pulpit.
entries.sens_profond: The root s-l-k means to take a path. Sulūk is therefore the effective walking upon the ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة) — not the study of treatises on the way, but the fact of walking. It is the practical dimension of all Sufism. Everything is there: one can know the way without practising it (this is the erudite), or practise it without having its doctrine (this is the simple soul) — but the true sālik has both: he knows the map and he walks the path. Three classical stages of sulūk: (1) the purgative way — purification of the nafs (النَّفْس), repentance, renunciation of faults; (2) the illuminative way — the acquisition of the virtues, the opening of the states, growing light; (3) the unitive way — fanāʾ (فَنَاء), baqāʾ (بَقَاء), the transformed return. But these phases are not separate as in a programme — they intertwine. The sālik repurifies at the very moment he is illumined, and his union with God reveals to him new darknesses within himself to purify. It is a walk in a spiral, never in a straight line.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The sālik does not ask himself where he is going — he walks, and the road opens beneath his steps.
You will be a sālik as long as you walk; at the end, the walker himself disappears, and only the walking remains.

entries.meditation: At what pace am I walking, at this instant — and toward what?

entries.racine_sens: to accompany, to be a companion
entries.definition_concise: The companionship of the master and of the brothers on the way — a major means of transmitting the baraka (البَرَكَة). Frequenting, more than reading, transforms. Aṣ-Ṣaḥāba (الصَّحَابَة) are the Companions of the Prophet.
entries.sens_profond: The word ṣuḥba denotes companionship in the strong sense: not mere physical presence, but the sharing of life. The Companions of the Prophet are called aṣ-Ṣaḥāba (الصَّحَابَة) precisely because they shared his life — not only listened to his words, but breathed the same air, walked the same roads, saw his face in fatigue and in joy. This intimacy made them what they became, far more than his written teachings. For the Sufis, it is exactly the same principle with the shaykh (الشَّيْخ): suḥba is what transforms. Ibn ʿArabī says: “A day spent with a saint is worth more than a thousand books read alone.” Why? Because baraka is transmitted by contact, not by information. The accomplished saint radiates — his atmosphere is a school. This intuition is just as true in reverse: bad suḥba (the company of the ignorant, the distracted, the wicked) corrupts too, without one's noticing it. Hadith: “A person is upon the religion of his intimate friend; let each of you take heed whom he takes as a friend.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: A person is upon the religion of his intimate friend — let each of you take heed whom he takes as a friend.
A day with a saint is worth more than a thousand books read alone.

entries.meditation: With whom do I spend the most time — and what does that make of me?

entries.racine_sens: to visit, to pay a visit
entries.definition_concise: The visit — particularly to the tombs of the saints and the prophets, and to living masters. A very widespread practice, underpinned by the belief in the continuity of the saint's baraka (البَرَكَة) beyond his death.
entries.sens_profond: The hadith permits ziyāra: “I had forbidden you the visiting of the tombs; visit them now — they remind you of the hereafter.” In the minimal sense, ziyāra is this memory-visit: to recall death, the transience of all things. But for the Sufis, it is more still: the saint, at his death, does not cease — he has left his physical body, but his baraka remains, particularly bound to his place of burial. To visit the tomb is to put oneself in relation with this baraka. One can pray at the tomb (but not to the tomb, which would be shirk), ask God through the intermediary of the saint's baraka (tawassul), receive an inspiration, a soothing. This practice has been contested by some strict currents (the Wahhabis notably), but remains widely attested by the tradition. The tombs of Rūmī in Konya, of Ibn ʿArabī in Damascus, of Muʿīn al-Dīn Chishtī in Ajmer, are high places of ziyāra. For the living, the ziyāra to the shaykh (الشَّيْخ) — even brief — can be a major spiritual event, in which locks come undone that no meditation had been able to undo.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I had forbidden you the visiting of the tombs; visit them now — they remind you of the hereafter.
The baraka of the saint does not die with his body; visit his tomb as you would visit his hermitage during his life.

entries.meditation: Is there a place where I feel, without being able to explain it, that there is something?

entries.racine_sens: to tear, to pierce — hence the patched garment of the dervish
entries.definition_concise: The mantle that the shaykh (الشَّيْخ) hands to the disciple as a sign of initiation. A garment often patched (the dervish is poor), it symbolises the spiritual transmission and belonging to a ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة).
entries.sens_profond: The handing of the khirqa is a major initiatic act: the shaykh lays his own mantle (or a symbolic mantle) upon the shoulders of the disciple, thus sealing his entry into the lineage. Three sorts of khirqa according to the classical authors: the khirqat al-irāda (خِرْقَةُ الإِرَادَة) (the mantle of the will — handed to the murīd (مُرِيد) who commits himself to the way), the khirqat at-tabarruk (the mantle of blessing — handed as a simple grace, without formal commitment), the khirqat al-khilāfa (خِرْقَةُ الخِلَافَة) (the mantle of succession — handed to the shaykh's successor, who will become his representative after his death). This last is the rarest and most solemn. The image of the mantle also refers to the mantle of the Prophet, which he is said to have handed to some of his Companions. Many silsilas reach back to this original mantle, said to have been transmitted from generation to generation. In the symbolic sense, to receive the khirqa is to enter into a spiritual body greater than oneself — one is no longer alone, one is held by a whole lineage that prays for one and watches over one.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The khirqa is the outside of an inner reality; without the reality, the mantle is but a rag.
Our khirqa is not of wool — it is the love of the Prophet that clothes us from within.

entries.meditation: With what am I clothed spiritually — and who has laid it upon my shoulders?

entries.racine_sens: to replace, to substitute
entries.definition_concise: The substitutes — hidden saints who, in the Sufi cosmology, permanently sustain the spiritual balance of the world. Usually counted as seven or forty. They replace the one who dies among them.
entries.sens_profond: Sufi cosmology, particularly developed in Ibn ʿArabī, describes a hidden hierarchy of saints who sustain the cosmic balance. At the summit, the quṭb (قُطْب) (the pole, unique in each generation). Beneath him, the awtād (أَوْتَاد) (the pegs, four, who hold the four cardinal points). Beneath them, the abdāl (the substitutes, seven or forty according to the schools). Beneath them again, the nujabāʾ (نُجَبَاء) (the noble ones), the nuqabāʾ (نُقَبَاء) (the deputies). In all, 300 invisible saints sustain the world. When one dies, another takes his place — the chain never breaks. The name abdāl comes precisely from this replacement. Hadith: “The abdāl are forty men; each time one dies, God substitutes another for him. It is through them that you receive the rain, it is through them that you are succoured.” This doctrine is not unanimously accepted (the Wahhabis reject it), but it is very widespread in the Sufi tradition. Its reach is not only cosmological — it is also moral: sainthood matters, even invisible; and each one may be called to carry, in his measure, a share of this burden.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The abdāl are forty men; when one dies, God substitutes another. It is through them that you are succoured.
The abdāl carry the world — without them, the earth would collapse.

entries.meditation: Are there, without my knowing it, beings whose prayer sustains the present instant?

entries.racine_sens: to come to the rescue, to save
entries.definition_concise: The Succour — the title of the supreme saint of the age, sometimes identified with the quṭb (قُطْب). The one whom one calls for help in extreme distress, because he has the cosmic function of coming to aid.
entries.sens_profond: The ghawth is, in some schools, one of the titles of the supreme saint — the summit of the spiritual hierarchy. He succours those who call upon him, even at a distance, even without knowing him by his physical name. This doctrine is very widespread in popular Sufism: one invokes Yā Ghawth (يَا غَوْث)! in extreme distress, hoping that the one who holds this function (whom one does not know) will hear and intervene. The most famous figure bears this title: ʿAbd al-Qādir (اَلْقَادِر) al-Jīlānī (d. 1166), founder of the Qādiriyya, is known as al-Ghawth al-Aʿẓam (الغَوْثُ الأَعْظَم) — the Supreme Succour. Even after his death, the tradition affirms that he continues to exercise this function. For the contemporary sensibility, these beliefs may seem pre-modern; but for the Sufi tradition, they say a deeper truth: we are not alone, beings watch, aids act at levels we do not perceive. Whether this is literal or symbolic, the effect on the believer's soul is immense: he does not despair. Succour may come from where one does not expect it, because the invisible is inhabited.

voix_des_maitres.texte: My foot is upon the neck of all the saints — such is my function, not my pride.
Help your brothers in distress, for succour belongs to God — through whom He wills, and when He wills.

entries.meditation: When was I succoured by what I could neither foresee nor merit?

entries.racine_sens: to honour, to gratify — hence karam (generosity, honour)
entries.definition_concise: The charisms that God grants to His saints — out-of-the-ordinary phenomena (clear-sightedness, healings, ubiquity…) which are honours and not proofs. To be distinguished from the muʿjiza (المُعْجِزَة) (the prophetic miracle).
entries.sens_profond: The word comes from karam (generosity, honour). The karāmāt are the extraordinary manifestations granted by God to His friends (awliyāʾ), as an honour that He renders visible to others or to themselves. A classical list: to know hearts without their speaking, to traverse great distances in an instant, to transform matter, to read the future, to speak to animals, to be seen in two places at once (bilocation). The tradition is abundantly filled with such accounts — but it also warns: (1) the karāmāt are not the proof of sainthood (a charlatan fakir can also produce wonders, by other means); (2) the accomplished saint prefers to hide his karāmāt — to give them too much publicity would be pride; (3) the greatest karāma (كَرَامَة) is rectitude of character — to know how to live as a just, simple, faithful person. A famous saying: “The best karāma is istiqāma” — rectitude, perseverance in the good. Many saints, says the tradition, never showed any outward wonder — and were nonetheless saints greater than those of whom a thousand miracles are told.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The best karāma is istiqāma (persevering rectitude).
If you see a man walking on water, but who contradicts the sunna in anything whatever, know that he is deceived.

entries.meditation: Am I asking for proofs, when what is given to me is more precious?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as ʿilm (knowledge) — the world as sign, that which makes God known
entries.definition_concise: The world, the worlds — that which is other than God. The Sufi tradition distinguishes several worlds: sensory, imaginal, intelligible, divine. Plural ʿawālim (عَوَالِم) or ʿālamīn (عَالَمِين) (Quran 1:2: Rabbi-l-ʿālamīn).
entries.sens_profond: The root ʿ-l-m also yields ʿilm (عِلْم) (knowledge) — the world is that which makes God known. All Islamic cosmology, particularly Sufi, distinguishes several levels of the real. The classical scheme of Mullā Ṣadrā: (1) ʿālam al-mulk (عَالَمُ المُلْك) or ʿālam ash-shahāda (عَالَمُ الشَّهَادَة) — the sensory world, what one sees; (2) ʿālam al-malakūt (عَالَمُ المَلَكُوت) or ʿālam al-mithāl (عَالَمُ المِثَال) — the imaginal world, where souls see subtle forms (angels, true dreams, spiritual states); (3) ʿālam al-jabarūt (عَالَمُ الجَبَرُوت) — the intelligible world, of the pure Spirits and the divine Names; (4) ʿālam al-lāhūt (عَالَمُ اللَّاهُوت) — the divine world, the Essence itself (properly speaking, no longer a “world”). Henry Corbin made the richness of the imaginal world known to the West — an ontological domain of its own, neither purely material nor purely intellectual, where the visions of the saints take place. To understand these levels changes everything: a true dream, an intuition, a strange encounter — these are real events, but at another level of the real than that of sensory objects. The world is not flat.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Praise be to God, Lord of the worlds (Rabbi-l-ʿālamīn).
The sensory world is the shadow of the imaginal world; the imaginal world is the shadow of the divine world.

entries.meditation: If the world were not flat — where have I touched, without knowing it, another level?

entries.racine_sens: to be near, immediate — hence dunyā: the nearest, as opposed to the hereafter
entries.definition_concise: This world — the present life, the lower world, as opposed to the ākhira (the hereafter). For the Sufis, the dunyā is not bad in itself — it is passing, and it is the attachment to it that blinds.
entries.sens_profond: The dunyā is the “nearest” (from the root d-n-w, to be near) — the immediate world of the senses, of daily action. The Quran qualifies it as play, diversion, adornment. But beware: it does not condemn the dunyā itself — it condemns the deception that consists in taking it for the goal. The hadith says: “Be in this world like a stranger or a traveller.” Not to refuse the world — to traverse it. The dunyā is a field of sowing for the hereafter: what you plant in it grows there. If you plant in it avarice, pride, cruelty — you reap hell. If you plant in it generosity, humility, love — you reap paradise (paradise is from now on, for the Sufis: it begins where the soul is ordered to God). The crucial Sufi distinction: fī al-dunyā wa laysa min ad-dunyā — in the world without being of the world. The error is not to live in the world (one must indeed live in it) — it is to be its captive. The saint lives in the dunyā like a traveller in an inn.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Be in this world like a stranger or a traveller.
The world is a bridge — cross it, do not build upon it.
Leave the dunyā before it leaves you; possess it without its possessing you.

entries.meditation: I am in the dunyā — but am I its captive?

entries.racine_sens: to raise, to set up a roof, a throne
entries.definition_concise: The divine Throne — the highest of the created realities, above which the Presence is established. For the Sufis, it is neither an object nor a place, but the symbol of the absolute sovereignty of God over the worlds.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran often evokes the ʿarsh: “The All-Merciful established Himself upon the Throne” (20:5). The literalists imagine it as a cosmic seat; the strict theologians see in it a metaphorical expression of sovereignty; the Sufis (Ibn ʿArabī notably) make of it a precise cosmological reality: the ʿarsh is the outer limit of the created world, the Throne that contains all the worlds. More deeply still, the qudsī hadith says: “The heart of the believer is the Throne of the Merciful.” What is above the heavens therefore finds its echo in the human breast — the cosmology is interiorised. This correspondence between macrocosm and microcosm is the key to all Sufi metaphysics: what one seeks outside is already within.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The All-Merciful established Himself upon the Throne.
The heart of the believer is the Throne of the Merciful.

entries.meditation: If my heart is destined to be His Throne — what encumbers it, at this instant?

entries.racine_sens: seat, base — hence kursī, the footstool or intermediate throne
entries.definition_concise: The divine Footstool — beneath the ʿarsh in the Sufi cosmology. Mentioned in the famous Āyat al-Kursī (آيَةُ الكُرْسِيّ) (Quran 2:255), the most powerful verse of the daily Sufi prayer.
entries.sens_profond: Verse 2:255 (“His Footstool extends over the heavens and the earth”) is one of the most meditated upon in Islam. For the Sufis, the kursī denotes a precise cosmological level: it is contained by the ʿarsh, but it contains the heavens and the earth. A cosmology of several stories: God → ʿarsh → kursī → sensory worlds. On the plane of knowledge, some commentators (al-Ṭabarī) identified the kursī with the divine knowledge, which contains all without being contained. The recitation of the Āyat al-Kursī is one of the most universal daily practices of the Muslim world — particularly in the evening before sleep, as a protection against the invisible darknesses.

voix_des_maitres.texte: His Footstool extends over the heavens and the earth, and their keeping costs Him no toil.
Whoever recites the Āyat al-Kursī before sleeping, God sends him a guardian until morning.

entries.meditation: If His Footstool covers the heavens — what edge of myself is not covered?

entries.racine_sens: to cut, to trim — hence qalam, the reed-pen, the instrument of the tracing
entries.definition_concise: The cosmic Pen — the first creation of God according to the hadith: “The first thing God created was the Pen.” It is by it that all destinies are traced upon the Preserved Tablet.
entries.sens_profond: The hadith says: “The first thing God created was the Pen. He said to it: Write. It said: What shall I write? He said: Write what is, what will be, until the Day of Resurrection.” The Pen is therefore the cosmic instrument of the divine decree. It writes upon the Preserved Tablet (al-lawḥ al-maḥfūẓ (اللَّوْحُ المَحْفُوظ)) all that happens. For the Sufis, this Pen is also the First Intellect (al-ʿaql (العَقْل) al-awwal) — the first hypostasis, the first light emanated from the Source. It is also the Islamic Logos: the creating Word. Its power is such that Ibn ʿArabī calls it nūr Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) (the Muhammadan Light) — the same that manifests itself later in the flesh in the Prophet. All that happens has already been written; what you are living now is read, not improvised.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The first thing God created was the Pen. He said to it: Write. It said: What shall I write? He said: Write what is, what will be, until the Day.
Nūn. By the Pen and what they write!

entries.meditation: If all that happens to me has been written — what remains for me to do?

entries.racine_sens: plank, tablet, flat surface
entries.definition_concise: The Preserved Tablet — the cosmic matrix upon which all that happens is inscribed. Evoked in Quran 85:22: “It is a glorious Quran upon a Preserved Tablet.”
entries.sens_profond: The pair Pen / Tablet is one of the most profound images of Sufi cosmology. The Pen writes, the Tablet receives the writing. All that will be in time — each breath, each encounter, each leaf that falls — is already inscribed there, outside time, in that eternal memory of God. The Tablet does not err, does not efface itself — it is maḥfūẓa, kept. But beware: to know the existence of the Tablet does not dispense with free will; the Sufis hold the paradox with finesse: “All is written, and you choose.” The writing precedes time; your choice unfolds within time. Both are true. For Ibn ʿArabī, the Tablet is the Universal Soul, which receives the determinations of the Intellect-Pen. These are the two complementary principles of all creation.

voix_des_maitres.texte: It is a glorious Quran, upon a Preserved Tablet.
The ink of the scholars is more precious than the blood of the martyrs — for they decipher what is upon the Tablet.

entries.meditation: If all is already inscribed upon the Tablet — what choice is nonetheless asked of me this morning?

entries.racine_sens: barrier, isthmus, that which separates two things while joining them
entries.definition_concise: The isthmus — an intermediate reality that separates and joins two orders. Quranically, the state of souls between death and resurrection. Metaphysically, the image itself: between pure spirit and body, the dream, the imaginal, the symbol.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran evokes several barzakhs: between the two seas (25:53), and between death and resurrection (23:100 — “behind them there is a barzakh until the day they will be resurrected”). But it is Ibn ʿArabī who raised this word to the rank of a central metaphysical concept: the barzakh is the order of the middle, neither pure spirit nor pure matter, in which images, true dreams, visions, subtle manifestations are formed. The imaginal world (ʿālam al-mithāl (عَالَمُ المِثَال)) is essentially a great barzakh. Henry Corbin made this dimension known to the West — he renders it as mundus imaginalis. It is in the barzakh that the visions of the prophets, the true dreams, the apparitions of the saints after their death unfold. The barzakh is not a fiction; it is a reality — but of another order than sensory objects. For the one who knows how to enter it, it is a world as vast as that of the stars.

voix_des_maitres.texte: And behind them there is a barzakh until the Day they will be resurrected.
The barzakh is neither the one nor the other — and it is in this that it is the most real: it carries both.

entries.meditation: Is there within me an in-between that is neither my body nor my pure thought?

entries.racine: ع ل م + م ث ل
entries.racine_sens: ʿālam (world) + mithāl (image, similitude)
entries.definition_concise: The imaginal world — an ontological level of its own, intermediate between the sensory and the purely intelligible. It is there that the angels, the souls after death, the visions, the symbolic figures reside.
entries.sens_profond: Unlike the imaginary (which is subjective, fanciful, individual), the imaginal is objective: a world inhabited by realities of its own, which one does not fabricate but which one visits. Henry Corbin coined this term in French (from Ibn ʿArabī and Suhrawardī) to distinguish this order from the mere imaginary: it is what makes true dreams true, the visions of the prophets real, the apparitions of the saints after their death existent.
