The imaginal world has its geography, its laws, its cities (Hūrqalyā, the emerald city of the Shia), its inhabitants. The accomplished saint can enter it consciously. The ordinary dreamer passes through it without noticing. To understand the existence of this world changes every reading of the Quran, of the prophetic narratives, of spiritual experiences — much of what seems incomprehensible (Muhammad journeys by night to Jerusalem and then to the heavens, Joseph sees in a dream eleven stars prostrate themselves) takes on meaning if one admits this level of reality.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The imagination is vaster than the sensory world — it contains what the senses could not reach.
This world is no less real than that of things — it is even more real, because it is more essential.

entries.meditation: My true dreams — where do they unfold, if not in my head alone?

entries.racine_sens: to be behind, last — the ākhira is the last, opposed to dunyā, the nearest
entries.definition_concise: The hereafter — the future life, opposed to dunyā (this world). For the Sufis, the ākhira in reality begins from now on: what you sow here, you reap there — and the saint already tastes it.
entries.sens_profond: The pair dunyā / ākhira structures all Muslim ethics. The life here is passing, the life there is eternal. But the Sufis qualify this: the ākhira is not after the dunyā in the temporal sense — it is beneath, more real, more lasting. The hadith says: “People are asleep; when they die, they awaken.” The ākhira is that awakening. For the saint who has realised fanāʾ (فَنَاء), the ākhira has already begun: he lives in a presence that no longer depends on material duration. His joy will not be changed at death — it will be confirmed. Conversely, the one who has lived in ghafla (الغَفْلَة) will know at death the brutal awakening: he will see what he has really been, and what he has really done. The knowledge of the ākhira transforms ethics: one chooses one's acts according to what they plant, not according to the immediate effect.

voix_des_maitres.texte: And the hereafter is better and more lasting.
Be in this world like a stranger or a traveller.

entries.meditation: If the ākhira begins from now on — what seed have I planted today?

entries.racine_sens: to cover, to hide — hence jannah, the enclosed garden, and janīn, the hidden unborn child
entries.definition_concise: The Garden — the destination of the accepted souls. Evoked in the Quran through sensory images (rivers, fruits, shade, pure companions) which the Sufis read as symbols of spiritual states.
entries.sens_profond: The word jannah literally means garden, but with the idea of being covered, protected. Paradise is an enclosed garden, where one is sheltered from the outside. The Quranic descriptions are vivid: rivers of water, of milk, of honey and of wine (47:15), trees whose fruits are never exhausted, pure companions. But the Sufis insist: these images are to be read in the literal sense and in the symbolic sense. In the symbolic sense, Paradise is the contemplation of God — true felicity, of which the sensory gardens are only a reflection. The Quran confirms it: “For those who have done good, the most beautiful, and still more (ziyāda)” (10:26) — that more, according to the hadith, is the vision of the divine Face. In the literal sense, Paradise is a real place — but of another order, in the imaginal world made bodily, where the bodies themselves are subtle, luminous, immortal. The two readings go together: the saint who loves God for His own sake receives in addition all the delights; the one who wants only the delights closes himself to the Presence that founds them.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I have prepared for My righteous servants what no eye has seen, what no ear has heard, what no human heart has conceived.
My God, if I worship You out of hope of Paradise, exclude me from it — I want only You.

entries.meditation: If the highest felicity of Paradise is to see Him — what within me already desires that vision?

entries.racine: non-arabe, mot probablement emprunté à l'hébreu (ge-hinnom)
entries.racine_sens: the valley of Hinnom — a place near Jerusalem where refuse was burned
entries.definition_concise: Hell — the destination of the souls that have rejected the light. For the Sufis, more than literal fire: the consequence of the voluntary separation from the Source — the inner torment of the soul that sees itself deprived of the Beloved.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran describes Jahannam with terrible images: fire, molten metal, bitter fruits, garments of fire. The literalists take it in the proper sense; the Sufis open onto another level. For Dante (who read the Sufis closely), “hell is the absence of God” — and that, at bottom, is what the Sufis say too. The fire is only the sensory expression of a deeper reality: the voluntary separation from what is the supreme Good. The soul that has refused to love God finds itself, at death, before itself as in a mirror — and sees that it has loved only shadows, which vanish. This emptiness is hell. More deeply still, some masters speak of a final mercy that would purify every soul: “My mercy prevails over My wrath.” The doctrine of the eternal or non-eternal end of the chastisement has been debated; the sober Sufis remain prudent — God knows best. But the spiritual experience is clear: all estrangement from God, from this very life, is already a foretaste.

voix_des_maitres.texte: My mercy prevails over My wrath.
The fire of hell is only the light of God seen through the veil of separation.

entries.meditation: If hell is separation — where, in my life, have I already known a foretaste of it?

entries.racine_sens: to weigh, to balance
entries.definition_concise: The divine Balance of the Judgement — where the acts of human beings will be weighed. For the Sufis, more than a physical instrument, it is the symbol of the divine justice that lets nothing escape.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran says: “We shall set up the just balances for the Day of Resurrection; no soul will be wronged in anything — were it but the weight of a mustard seed, We shall bring it forth” (21:47). This image grips everyone: nothing you have done, in good or in evil, is lost. All is counted with an absolute precision. The Sufis add: “Reckon with yourselves before you are called to account” (ʿUmar). Muḥāsaba (self-examination) is the exercise that anticipates the mīzān. But also: the divine mīzān is not human reckoning. A single good action may weigh infinitely if it has been done in perfect sincerity; a thousand acts done with ostentation may weigh nothing. The balance weighs what is behind the acts, not their volume. This intuition frees one from religious quantitativism — God does not count the number of prostrations, He counts the heart that bows.

voix_des_maitres.texte: We shall set up the just balances for the Day of Resurrection; no soul will be wronged.
How many prayers weigh nothing — how many fragments of prayer weigh everything.

entries.meditation: If all my acts were weighed, at this instant, which would weigh the most — and which the least?

entries.racine_sens: broad way, road, and more precisely the bridge to be crossed
entries.definition_concise: The Bridge stretched above hell, which every soul must cross on the Day of Judgement. Thinner than a hair, sharper than a sword, according to the hadith. But also: the ṣirāṭ mustaqīm (صِرَاطٌ مُسْتَقِيم), the straight path, which one asks for in the Fātiḥa.
entries.sens_profond: The famous hadith says that the ṣirāṭ is “thinner than a hair, sharper than a sword” — a striking image: any sin to one side or the other is a fall. For the swift, one crosses like lightning; for the slow, one crawls; for those who did not walk straight in life, one falls. But this eschatological bridge has its counterpart here below: the ṣirāṭ mustaqīm, the straight path, which every Muslim asks for seventeen times a day in the Fātiḥa: “Ihdinā aṣ-ṣirāṭa-l-mustaqīm” — guide us upon the straight path. This path is the very way that leads to the bridge without falling: right practice, upright intention, fidelity to the prophetic model. To walk straight here below is to train oneself to cross the ṣirāṭ there. And more deeply: “My Lord is upon a ṣirāṭ mustaqīm” (Quran 11:56) — the straight way is not only a road, it is Himself, who maintains all things in balance.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Guide us upon the straight path.
The ṣirāṭ is thinner than a hair, sharper than a sword — who will be straight enough to cross it?

entries.meditation: There, now — am I upon the ṣirāṭ, or have I deviated by a few degrees?

entries.racine_sens: to measure, to determine a quantity, to fix the extent
entries.definition_concise: The divine decree — the eternal knowledge God has of all that will be, and His act of measuring it. With its corollary al-qaḍāʾ (القَضَاء) (the accomplishment), it forms one of the six pillars of faith.
entries.sens_profond: The hadith of Gabriel mentions, as the sixth pillar of faith, “to believe in the decree, in its good and in its evil.” It is doubtless the hardest to understand. How to reconcile the freedom of the human being and the omniscience of God who knows everything in advance? The Sufis hold the paradox without resolving it: God knows; you choose. His knowledge does not constrain you, it knows. Three schools oppose one another in the kalām (كَلَام): the jabariyya (the determinists — the human being is forced), the qadariyya (the libertarians — the human being creates his acts), and the middle way (ahl al-sunna) which says: “The human being acquires (kasb) his acts; God creates them.” The Sufis go further: for them, the qadar is not a chain to be borne, it is an economy to be understood. All that happens to you is just for your path — even what seems unjust to you. The saint receives the qadar with riḍā (رِضَا) (acceptance), not as an imposition, but as a hidden gift. “Not a leaf falls without His knowing it” (Quran 6:59) — therefore not a leaf falls without love.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Īmān is to believe in God, in His angels, in His Books, in His Messengers, in the Last Day, and in the decree, good and bad.
The decree is a deep sea — do not plunge your head into it, you would drown.

entries.meditation: What has happened to me unexpectedly this week — how would I see Him at work in it?

entries.racine_sens: to decide, to accomplish, to carry out
entries.definition_concise: The accomplishment in time of what was decreed from all eternity (qadar (القَدَر)). A subtle distinction: the qadar is the divine knowledge of the future; the qaḍāʾ is its effective realisation in history.
entries.sens_profond: The distinction qadar / qaḍāʾ is one of the finest in classical theology. An image: the qadar is what is written upon the Tablet; the qaḍāʾ is what descends into time. The Pen has written; now, in time, the event happens. This distinction allows one to understand enigmatic phenomena: the duʿāʾ (the invocation) can modify the qaḍāʾ (what descends) without changing the qadar (what is written). Hadith: “Nothing repels the qadar save the duʿāʾ.” How? The divine knowledge includes, from all time, the duʿāʾ that you make now. So what seems to be a change is, on the divine scale, only a foreseen detail. But for you, in time, it is really your prayer that has changed the outcome. The saint lives this tension without anguish: he prays as if everything depended on his prayer, and accepts as if nothing depended on him. The duʿāʾ does not force God — it inscribes itself within His hidden economy.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Nothing repels the qadar save the duʿāʾ; and nothing lengthens life save filial piety.
We flee from the qaḍāʾ of God toward the qaḍāʾ of God.

entries.meditation: If nothing changes the qadar save prayer — for what am I praying at this moment?

entries.racine_sens: to concord, to accord — hence the grace that accords the act with the divine will
entries.definition_concise: The particular grace of God which accords the human act with His will — makes one manage to do what is needed, at the moment it is needed, in the spirit it is needed. Without tawfīq, even the best intention fails.
entries.sens_profond: The human being wants to do good. He performs his prayers, his fasts, his practices. But something in him resists — he forgets, he strays, he does just beside. The tawfīq is that subtle grace by which, suddenly, all is in accord: you rise on time for the prayer, you find the right words, you choose the exact gesture. You have not done better — something in you has coincided with what was needed. For the Sufis, one does not merit the tawfīq — it is given. Its negative counterpart is the khidhlān (abandonment): when God withdraws His grace and leaves you to yourself, all goes awry, even your good intentions. This may be a trial, but also a consequence of negligence (ghafla (الغَفْلَة)). The saint ceaselessly asks for the tawfīq: “O God, be my associate in this act; without You, I shall do ill even while willing the good.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: My success (tawfīqī) depends only on God.
The servant does not progress by his effort — he progresses by the tawfīq that God grants him.

entries.meditation: When something accords perfectly in my life — is it my merit, or a gift?

entries.racine_sens: to announce a piece of news, to communicate a message — hence nabī, the prophet
entries.definition_concise: Prophethood — the function by which God makes His Word known to a chosen human being, so that he may transmit it to others. 124,000 prophets according to the hadith; Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) is the seal, the last.
entries.sens_profond: Prophethood is a gift of God, not a human merit. The prophet is chosen, never self-appointed. He receives the revelation (waḥy (وَحْي)), transmits it to his people, and bears the charge of embodying his message himself. A classical distinction: nabī (a prophet receiving the revelation) and rasūl (a messenger, who in addition has the mission of founding a community or bringing a new Law). Every rasūl is a nabī, but the reverse is not true. The Sufis distinguish further: nubuwwa (النُّبُوَّة) and walāya (وَلَايَة) (sainthood). For Ibn ʿArabī, sainthood is in reality higher than prophethood in the essential order — the prophet is a saint plus a messenger; his sainthood is the soul of his prophethood. But with a qualification: prophethood has a public function that comes to an end (the Seal has been set); sainthood continues, through the awliyāʾ, until the end of time. Sainthood is therefore the living inheritance of prophethood among the friends of God.

voix_des_maitres.texte: God has sent a hundred and twenty-four thousand prophets — three hundred and thirteen or three hundred and fifteen are messengers.
Prophethood is completed; walāya continues until the end.

entries.meditation: If a prophet had spoken to me in person, would I have listened differently than I do when reading his words?

entries.racine_sens: to communicate swiftly, in a subtle manner
entries.definition_concise: Revelation — the direct and immediate communication of God to His prophet. Reserved for the prophets (to be distinguished from ilhām, an inspiration possible in the saints). The Quran is waḥy.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran (42:51) distinguishes three modes of divine communication to the human being: (1) by direct revelation (waḥy in the strict sense) — God inspires it in the soul of the prophet, without intermediary; (2) from behind a veil — this is what happens to Moses at Sinai; (3) by the sending of a messenger — generally the angel Gabriel. Waḥy in the technical sense denotes these three modes, reserved for the prophets. For the Sufis, the opening is wider: God does not cease to speak to His servants, but in more discreet forms — ilhām (clear inspiration), khāṭir (a passing, just thought), the true dream. None of these forms equals prophetic waḥy, which is literal: its words are the very words of God. The distinction is crucial so as not to fall into illuminism: the saints receive, but never claim to add to the revelation. “Prophethood is sealed,” says the tradition. What remains open is the reception, the commentary, the application.

voix_des_maitres.texte: It was not granted to a human being that God should speak to him, save by revelation, or from behind a veil, or by sending a messenger.
Prophethood has been interrupted, but its first signs remain — the true dream of the believer.

entries.meditation: Is there a word, in the Quran, that speaks to me directly — as if it were addressed to me?

entries.racine_sens: to send — hence rasūl, the envoy
entries.definition_concise: The mission of the Messenger — a function additional to prophethood, by which the prophet is sent with a message to transmit. Every rasūl is a nabī, but not every nabī is a rasūl.
entries.sens_profond: The distinction nabī / rasūl is subtle. Every prophet (nabī) receives the revelation. But only the rasūl is sent with a public mission: to bring a new Law, to found a community, to transform a people. The rasūl are fewer than the nabī: according to the hadith, 124,000 prophets for about 313 messengers. The principal rasūl: Adam, Idrīs (Enoch), Nūḥ (Noah), Ibrāhīm (Abraham), Mūsā (Moses), Dāwūd (David), Sulaymān (Solomon), ʿĪsā (Jesus), Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد). Each rasūl brings a particular face of the same Truth — adapted to his time and his people. For the Sufis, particularly Ibn ʿArabī (who devotes his Fuṣūṣ al-Ḥikam (فُصُوصُ الحِكَم) to the twenty-seven prophets), each rasūl manifests a particular divine Name: Abraham the Khalīl (the intimate friend), Moses the bearer of the Law, Jesus the Breath of God and the Spirit, Muḥammad the unifying perfection. All complete one another; none contradicts. The khātim (the Seal) does not close — it accomplishes.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Messengers, bringers of good news and warners, so that after the messengers, human beings might have no argument before God.
Each prophet is a face of the Truth — all together they make a star without equal.

entries.meditation: If each prophet manifests a Name — which one speaks to me most, in my depths?

entries.racine_sens: to be incapable — hence muʿjiza, that which renders others incapable of imitating
entries.definition_concise: The miracle proper to the prophet — an extraordinary phenomenon that renders the gainsayers incapable of imitation, and which authenticates his divine mission. To be distinguished from the karāma (كَرَامَة) (the charism of the saint).
entries.sens_profond: Each prophet is accompanied by signs (āyāt) that confirm his mission. For Moses: the staff that becomes a serpent, the white hand, the ten plagues of Egypt. For Jesus: the raising of the dead, the healing of the blind and the lepers, the creation of a clay bird by his breath. For Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد): the Quran itself is the muʿjiza par excellence — the iʿjāz al-Qurʾān, its linguistic and spiritual inimitability. The Quranic challenge is posed several times (10:38; 11:13; 17:88): “Bring a sūra like it, or even a single verse like it.” No one, in fourteen centuries, has succeeded. For the Sufis, however, the greatest muʿjiza of Muḥammad is not so much the outer Quran as his transformation of those who approached him: illiterate and brutal Bedouins became, in his company, sages, saints, civilisers. The whole Islamic civilisation followed from it. The muʿjiza is not a magical proof — it is a proof by the fruits.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Say: If human beings and jinn united to produce something like this Quran, they could not produce anything like it, even if they supported one another.
The Quran is the miracle that does not grow old: each generation finds in it what none before had seen.

entries.meditation: What sign has already been given to me — that I have not known how to read as such?

entries.racine: أ ن س + ك م ل
entries.racine_sens: insān (human being) + kāmil (perfect, complete)
entries.definition_concise: The universal or Perfect Human Being — a central concept of Ibn ʿArabī. The human being who has realised within himself all the divine perfections, an integral mirror of the Presence. The Prophet Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) is its archetype.
entries.sens_profond: The doctrine of the insān kāmil (إِنْسَانٌ كَامِل) is one of the most profound in Sufism — Ibn ʿArabī develops it in the Fuṣūṣ al-Ḥikam (فُصُوصُ الحِكَم) and the Futūḥāt al-Makkiyya. ʿAbd al-Karīm (اَلْكَرِيم) al-Jīlī (d. c. 1424) devoted a famous work to it. The central idea: the whole of creation manifests the divine Names, but in a dispersed manner — the Names of majesty in the thunder, the Names of beauty in the flower, the Names of knowledge in the scholar. No created being, save the insān kāmil, manifests them all together. The universal Human Being is the mirror in which God contemplates Himself integrally. The Prophet Muḥammad is the archetype of this reality (the ḥaqīqa muḥammadiyya, the Muhammadan Reality). But after him, each accomplished saint, in his measure, partakes of this fullness. The doctrine echoes universal intuitions: the Adam Kadmon of the Kabbalah, the cosmic Anthropos of the late gnoses, the cosmic Christ of certain Christian Fathers. All say: the human being is not simply a creature among the creatures — he is the goal of creation, the place where the universe recognises itself.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I was a hidden treasure, and I loved to be known — so I created creation to be known through it.
The universal Human Being is the eye by which God sees Himself in the world.

entries.meditation: If I am called to be an integral mirror — which divine Name is still missing in me?

entries.racine_sens: to make a sustained effort
entries.definition_concise: The sustained effort — in the primary sense, the combat on the path of God. The Prophet distinguished the lesser jihād (the outward, defensive combat) and the greater jihād (the struggle against the nafs (النَّفْس)), which is by far the more important.
entries.sens_profond: The word jihād has been largely distorted in contemporary media discourse. Its root means first effort, with no warlike connotation. The highest effort, according to the Prophet, is al-jihād al-akbar, the greater combat — that against the carnal soul (nafs). Returning from a battle, the Prophet is said to have declared: “We return from the lesser combat toward the greater combat. — What is the greater combat? — The combat against the soul.” All the Sufi way is therefore, in a sense, this continuous greater jihād. Four dimensions of jihād according to the classical authors: the jihād of the heart (against the nafs), the jihād of the tongue (the just word and counsel), the jihād of the hand (to correct injustice to the measure of the possible), the jihād of the sword (only defensive and strictly framed — a just war). The first three concern all believers permanently; the fourth is exceptional, subject to rigorous conditions. To confuse the outward sense and the inner sense of jihād is to miss all the depth of the tradition.

voix_des_maitres.texte: We return from the lesser combat toward the greater combat — the combat against the soul.
The true combatant is the one who fights his nafs; the outward battles are childish beside it.

entries.meditation: What combat have I waged today against myself — and what ground remains to be conquered?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as jihād — to make an effort
entries.definition_concise: Inner effort — a rigorous discipline applied to oneself to purify the nafs (النَّفْس) and to progress on the way. A fundamental practice of the first stages: without mujāhada, no muʿāmala, no mushāhada.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufis have a famous triad: mujāhada (effort), muʿāmala (dealing with God, fidelity to the practices), mushāhada (vision, contemplation). One begins with effort, passes to faithful practice, and arrives at vision. To skip a stage is impossible. Mujāhada is the phase of resistance: the nafs does not want to be transformed, it defends itself. You rise for the dawn prayer — it wants to sleep. You fast — it is hungry. You do the dhikr (الذِّكْر) — it thinks of something else. Mujāhada is to persist despite its objections, until it yields. The hadith says: “Whoever strives for Us, We guide him upon Our ways” (Quran 29:69). That “for Us” is crucial — the effort must be for Him, not in order to admire oneself for having made an effort. Also: mujāhada must be measured, never excessive — extreme asceticism (rahbāniyya) is rejected by the sunna. The Prophet said: “This religion is easy; no one makes it hard without its breaking him.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: And those who strive for Us, We shall guide upon Our ways.
Thirty years of mujāhada won me one hour of muʿāmala; thirty years of muʿāmala won me an instant of mushāhada.

entries.meditation: What resistance within me, at this instant, asks to be gently opposed?

entries.racine_sens: to break in, to tame (a horse) — hence riyāḍa, the training
entries.definition_concise: The methodical training of the soul — as one breaks in a horse. Repeated practices of the mujāhada that progressively educate the nafs (النَّفْس) toward obedience. More systematic than the mujāhada (which is more combative).
entries.sens_profond: The metaphor of the breaking-in is ancient: the nafs is like a spirited horse. If one lets it go, it rushes into the passions. If one breaks it too hard, it rears, it breaks itself. Riyāḍa is the right measure: one progressively brings it to recognise its rider (the heart), to accept the bit (the sharīʿa), to follow the direction (the ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة)). Concretely, riyāḍa includes: the control of food (without excess one way or the other), the regulation of sleep (enough to live, little so as not to grow torpid), the practice of silence (to speak little, to the point), the discipline of the gaze (not to seek what distracts), fidelity to the practices (wird (الوِرْد), ṣalāt, dhikr (الذِّكْر)). The beginner's error: to believe that the harder it is, the holier it is. A twofold error: one breaks oneself (and abandons), or one grows proud of having held out (and loses all the value). Just riyāḍa is persistent, not extreme. Like a path one follows every day for years — without haste, without slackening.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Eat a third, drink a third, keep a third empty — that is the riyāḍa of the belly.
The soul is like the sea — one does not cross it in a single leap, but by patient strokes of the oar.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a horse still unbroken — that I must learn to handle?

entries.racine_sens: to stand upright, straight — hence istiqāma, rectitude
entries.definition_concise: Persevering rectitude — a fidelity held over time to the straight way, without wavering or turning back. Considered by the classical authors one of the highest karāmāt (charisms): to be straight in the long term.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran commands the Prophet: “Be straight (istaqim) as you have been commanded, you and those who have repented with you” (11:112). Sufyān al-Thawrī asked the Prophet: “O Messenger of God, tell me a word of Islam after which I shall need to question no one.” The Prophet answered: “Say: I believe in God — then stand straight.” All the way is held in those two words. And the great Sufi maxim: “The best karāma (كَرَامَة) is istiqāma.” Why? Because many can launch themselves in a flash of fervour. Few can hold for ten, twenty, fifty years, without slackening. For the ancients, a saint who walks on water for an instant is less admirable than an ordinary man who prays his five prayers without fail for fifty years. Istiqāma is rare because it demands endurance without the goad of novelty. Everyone loves to begin; few love to continue. It is in the continuing that sainthood proves itself.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Say: ‘I believe in God’ — then stand straight.
The best karāma is istiqāma.

entries.meditation: Is there a practice I have abandoned out of weariness — and that I could take up again, simply, this evening?

entries.racine_sens: to resolve firmly
entries.definition_concise: Firm resolve — a decision taken in the heart to hold to an act or a commitment, despite the difficulties. Distinct from irāda (إِرَادَة) (ordinary will) by its unshakeable firmness.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran evokes the ūlū-l-ʿazm — the prophets endowed with resolve: Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد). Five great envoys whose firmness exceeded that of the other prophets; they traversed major trials without bending. ʿazīma is therefore that holding in the face of adversity. For the Sufi traveller, it is essential: without ʿazīma, every practice slackens at the first obstacle. The cold night when one must rise for the prayer, the fatigue that tempts one to cut short the wird (الوِرْد), the illness that suggests one has an excuse to slacken — all this yields only before ʿazīma. Hadith: “Let one of you resolve firmly in his invocation: let him not say ‘if You will,’ for God cannot be constrained.” ʿazīma stands against the rukhṣa (the dispensation): there are always reasons to slacken; the way asks one to choose the high road, the one that has no excuse. But without tension — authentic ʿazīma is taut yet peaceful.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Endure as the prophets endowed with resolve (ūlū-l-ʿazm) endured.
ʿazīma makes the man; the hesitant arrive nowhere.

entries.meditation: Is there a decision that I keep putting off to tomorrow — and that asks for a true ʿazīma now?

entries.racine_sens: to educate, to fashion — hence adab: politeness, courtesy, culture
entries.definition_concise: Inner and outer etiquette — not a surface politeness, but the just measure in everything: with God, with the master, with the brothers, with oneself. For the Sufis, “the whole way is in adab.”
entries.sens_profond: The word adab is broad: it means at once courtesy, etiquette, culture, formation. For the Sufis, adab is the body of right manners that make one act as is fitting to each situation. Adab toward God: never to address Him with coarse familiarity, but to serve Him with reverence and love. Adab toward the shaykh (الشَّيْخ): not to precede him, not to raise one's voice before him, not to sit before he invites you. Adab toward the brothers: not to wound, not to interrupt, not to envy. Adab toward oneself: not to despise oneself, but also not to flatter oneself. Adab toward time: not to kill it. Adab toward food: to begin with the name of God, to eat without gluttony. Adab toward speech: to say only what is needed, at the moment it is needed. Adab is therefore a complete spiritual ecology. The classical authors say: “Adab is worth half of religion; some say: it is the whole of religion.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: The whole way is in adab — to forget it is to forget everything.
Adab is half of ʿilm (عِلْم); without it, knowledge becomes dangerous.

entries.meditation: Is there a situation in which I lacked adab today — without even noticing it?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as khalq (creation) — character as a second creation of oneself
entries.definition_concise: Character or the virtues — the body of inner dispositions fashioned by practice. The Prophet said: “I was sent to perfect the akhlāq.” All the prophetic mission lies in the transformation of character.
entries.sens_profond: The root of akhlāq is the same as that of khalq (creation) — character is a second creation of oneself, fashioned by effort and grace. The famous hadith: “I was sent to perfect the noble akhlāq” — the goal of the whole prophetic mission is held in these words. And: “The best of you is the one who has the best character.” Character is more important than the ritual practices: one can perform a thousand prayers with a base character; a thousand prayers are worth nothing if one is avaricious, jealous, mean. Conversely, a man of beautiful character, even if little given to practice, is loved by God. The prophetic virtues are many: sincerity, reliability, generosity, patience, gentleness, humility, justice. For the Sufis, the work upon character is the true measure of progress. You can measure where you stand by your reaction when someone crosses you: if you flare up, you have realised nothing; if you remain peaceful, something has matured. Qudsī hadith: “My servant does not cease to draw near to Me through the supererogatory works until I love him.” The sign of being loved by God is the transformation of the soul — visible in the character.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I was sent to perfect the noble characters.
The best of you is the one who has the best character.
If you want to know where you stand spiritually, look at how you react when you are crossed.

entries.meditation: Which virtue am I in the process of cultivating — and which have I forgotten?

entries.racine_sens: to be young, valiant — hence fatā (the young brave one), futuwwa (the quality of the fatā)
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual chivalry — the virtue of radical generosity, of self-giving, of inner nobility that always prefers others. A tradition highly developed in the Middle Ages: the futuwwa-clubs were the Muslim equivalent of the brotherhoods of knights.
entries.sens_profond: The word comes from fatā (فَتًى), the young brave one — the one who acts with generosity, courage, disinterestedness. The Quran uses the word for Abraham breaking the idols (21:60: “We heard a young man (fatan) speak of them; he is called Abraham”) and for the Companions of the Cave (18:13: “They were young men”). Futuwwa, as a social and spiritual virtue, flowered particularly from the 9th century onward, in parallel with Sufism. Its principles: to give without reminding, to prefer the other to oneself, to excuse the one who has offended you, never to humiliate. ʿAlī (اَلْعَلِيّ) ibn Abī Ṭālib is its archetypal model: it was said that lā fatā illā ʿAlī — there is no fatā but ʿAlī. A very beautiful futuwwa developed in the urban guilds: artisans, soldiers, Sufis, who grouped themselves into chivalric brotherhoods. This tradition has survived to our day in certain aspects of Iranian and Anatolian culture. It is the highest expression of human brotherhood: to prefer others not by calculation, but by nobility.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Lā fatā illā ʿAlī (There is no fatā but ʿAlī).
Futuwwa is to see excellence in others and fault in oneself.
Futuwwa is that you serve when it is expected that you be served.

entries.meditation: Today, did I prefer myself or did I prefer the other — in a situation where I had the choice?

entries.racine_sens: to succeed, to replace — hence khalīfa, the successor, the lieutenant
entries.definition_concise: Vicegerency — the function by which God makes the human being His lieutenant upon the earth (Quran 2:30). At once the universal human condition (Adam) and a politico-spiritual function (the caliph).
entries.sens_profond: The Quran opens with the extraordinary announcement: “I am going to create upon the earth a khalīfa” (2:30). Adam — therefore humanity — is constituted vicegerent of God upon the earth. What a responsibility: to represent God, to prolong His action, to manifest His Names. For the Sufis, the Adamic khilāfa is the ontological vocation of the human: he is not a creature among the creatures, he is the image of the Creator within creation — charged with maintaining it, beautifying it, reconciling it with its Source. This doctrine gives the human being a considerable dignity, and correspondingly a crushing responsibility: ecological degradation, for example, is a betrayal of this khilāfa. On the politico-historical plane, the khilāfa was also the institution of succession to the Prophet: Abū Bakr, ʿUmar, ʿUthmān, ʿAlī (اَلْعَلِيّ) were the four rightly-guided caliphs. This political dimension has known a thousand evolutions across history — but the essence remains: a just khalīfa represents God in justice. Any human power that departs from this loses its legitimacy as khilāfa.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I am going to establish upon the earth a vicegerent.
Adam is the khalīfa because he carries all the Names — he is the only one able to manifest them together.

entries.meditation: If I am the lieutenant of God upon the earth — what do I hold from Him that asks to be well held?

entries.racine_sens: to be present, to attend
entries.definition_concise: The presence of the heart before God — a state in which one is wholly there, without dispersion. The opposite of ghafla (الغَفْلَة) (forgetfulness, distraction). It is the soul of the dhikr (الذِّكْر) and of every authentic practice.
entries.sens_profond: Ḥuḍūr is not an effort of mental concentration — it is a tipping-over of the heart. In ghafla, one is dispersed, dragged by thoughts, distracted by solicitations. In ḥuḍūr, one is recollected: all is gathered into a single point, here and now. The hadith says: “A prayer is never complete without ḥuḍūr.” You can perform a thousand prayers without presence — they are worth little. You can perform a single prayer with ḥuḍūr — it is worth everything. The degrees of ḥuḍūr: presence of the body (to be physically there), presence of the tongue (to recite consciously), presence of the heart (to have the heart set upon what one is doing), presence before God (to know that one is under His gaze), presence in God (the saint who has nothing but Him before his eyes). This last is rare. But already the third radically transforms the prayer: a quarter of an hour of truly present prayer changes the soul more than hours of distracted recitation. Ḥuḍūr is iḥsān in act.

voix_des_maitres.texte: How many who pray have of their prayer only the fatigue — for having missed the ḥuḍūr.
Ḥuḍūr is the secret of invocation: without it, you speak alone; with it, you speak to Him.

entries.meditation: At this instant — am I truly there, or dispersed among ten places?

entries.racine_sens: to become intoxicated, to lose one's reason
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual intoxication — a state in which the servant, seized by love or by the divine vision, loses the awareness of himself and of the world. A powerful but transitory state, which must give way to ṣaḥw (sobriety) in order to be fully integrated.
entries.sens_profond: Sukr (السُّكْر) is the gripping experience in which God manifests Himself with such force that the soul can no longer contain itself: it overflows, loses its bearings, sometimes speaks incoherently. Bisṭāmī and Ḥallāj are the classical figures of Sufi intoxication: “Subḥānī! How great is my own glory!” says Bisṭāmī in intoxication — understand: it is not he who speaks, it is God in him. “Anā-l-Ḥaqq (أَنَا الْحَقّ) — I am the True,” says Ḥallāj: it is not he who attributes divinity to himself, it is the disappearance of his self that leaves nothing but Him speaking through his mouth. But here it is: these words, pronounced in intoxication, are not understandable by the common. They cost Ḥallāj his life. This is why al-Junayd, his master, corrected him: “He spoke of what he saw — but he should not have said it.” The sober way holds that sukr is precious as an experience, but that one must return from it to ṣaḥw (sobriety) in order to be able to serve, to teach, to live with others. Sukr is a passing grace; ṣaḥw is the lasting station.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Subḥānī! How great is my own glory!
Anā-l-Ḥaqq — I am the True.
They spoke of what they saw; they should not have said it.

entries.meditation: Was there a moment when I was seized beyond myself — and what did I do with it?

entries.racine_sens: to be awake, sound of mind (after a fainting, after intoxication)
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual sobriety — the state of return to oneself after intoxication (sukr (السُّكْر)), without having lost what one received in it. For the great tradition (al-Junayd), ṣaḥw is higher than sukr: it is the integration of grace into ordinary life.
entries.sens_profond: Two schools oppose one another in classical Sufism: the sukārā (the intoxicated — Bisṭāmī, Ḥallāj, ʿAṭṭār) and the ṣāḥūn (the sober — al-Junayd, al-Qushayrī, Ibn ʿArabī for the most part). The intoxicated stress the ecstatic experience, overflowing, sometimes imprudent in its words. The sober stress integration, measure, fidelity to the appearances of the straight path. Al-Junayd decided the matter: “Sobriety after intoxication is higher than intoxication — for intoxication is only a stage, sobriety is the dwelling.” Why? Because in intoxication, the saint is separated from the human community — he can no longer teach, nor serve, nor assume his duties. In ṣaḥw, he has all that intoxication gave him, and he is restored to the world — he can therefore transmit. The way does not stop at intoxication; it traverses intoxication in order to go further, toward a ṣaḥw that is not the sobriety from before intoxication, but a sobriety after, irrigated by what intoxication has brought.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Sobriety after intoxication is higher than intoxication — for intoxication passes, sobriety remains.
The sober one lives in the world while remaining elsewhere; the intoxicated one leaves the world without being able to return to it.

entries.meditation: Do I prefer the moments of lightning — or the calm and continuous light?

entries.racine_sens: to move vigorously, to overflow
entries.definition_concise: The ecstatic utterance — a seemingly scandalous declaration pronounced in spiritual intoxication (sukr (السُّكْر)). An entire literary genre of classical Sufism. Taken literally, many are heretical; read in their context, they bear witness to fanāʾ (فَنَاء).
entries.sens_profond: The shaṭḥ (شَطْح) is that overflowing of words which springs up in the Sufi in intoxication, and which seems, from the outside, to transgress the limits of orthodoxy. The most famous is that of Bisṭāmī: “Subḥānī mā aʿẓama shaʾnī — Glory to Me, how great is My majesty!” In Arabic, subḥān is what one says of God — Bisṭāmī applies it to himself. Heresy? No, say the Sufis: it is that at that moment, his self has disappeared, and it is God who speaks through his mouth. The most terrible shaṭḥ is that of Ḥallāj: “Anā-l-Ḥaqq (أَنَا الْحَقّ) — I am the True,” which cost him his life. Ibn ʿArabī, later, in his Mawāqif, in the Munājāt, is filled with shaṭaḥāt. The genre was codified by Rūzbihān Baqlī in the 12th century in his Sharḥ-i shaṭḥiyāt. The sober ones like al-Junayd do not deny the veracity of the shaṭaḥāt — but they blame their public expression: “They spoke of what they saw; they should not have said it.” For the ordinary reader, the shaṭḥ may seem blasphemous — that is what makes it so difficult to transmit.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Beneath my garment, there is nothing but God.
Anā-l-Ḥaqq.
The shaṭaḥāt are the lightning flashes of the lovers — they burn the one who approaches them without preparation.

entries.meditation: Was there a moment when I should not have said what I saw — out of concern for those who could not hear it?

entries.racine_sens: to pass through the mind, to traverse
entries.definition_concise: The passing thought that crosses the heart — it may come from God, from the angel, from the nafs (النَّفْس), or from the demon. The art of spiritual discernment consists precisely in recognising their source.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufis have developed a fine science of the khawāṭir (plural of khāṭir): the thoughts that pass through the heart. Four possible sources: (1) khāṭir raḥmānī — a thought coming from God, peaceful, just, which points toward the good; (2) khāṭir malakī — a thought coming from an angel, firm, clear, which calls to the sunna; (3) khāṭir nafsānī — a thought coming from the nafs, carnal, self-interested, which wants pleasure or recognition; (4) khāṭir shayṭānī — a thought coming from the demon, cunning, which urges toward evil while disguising it as good. To discern between the four is the major art of the traveller — it is what the Christian tradition calls the discernment of spirits. Criteria: does the thought urge toward God or away from Him? Does it bring peace or agitation? Is it in accord with the sharīʿa or does it contradict it? Does it increase humility or pride? Without this discernment, one takes for inspiration what is only disguised egoism, and one rejects out of scruple what was the just voice. The Sufi master is the one who has refined this discernment through years of observing his own heart.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Watch the khawāṭir: each of them is a message, but they do not all come from the same sender.
Ask counsel of your heart — even after a hundred requests to other people.

entries.meditation: What thought crosses my heart at this moment — where does it come from?

entries.racine_sens: to arrive, to descend — hence wārid, that which comes to you
entries.definition_concise: A sudden arrival in the heart — a state, a light, or a thought that descends from the Most High without one's having prepared it. More powerful than the khāṭir: the wārid shakes, transforms, often lastingly.
entries.sens_profond: The wārid is more intense than the khāṭir: it is not a mere passing thought — it is a landing of grace, which can overturn the course of a life. It may take varied forms: a flash of understanding that resolves a long difficulty; a wave of love that brings one to tears; a sudden and clear decision (to leave something, to begin something else); a sensation of divine presence so strong that it overturns the day. The wārid is not provoked — it is given. But one can be prepared for it: a heart polished by the practices is better disposed to receive the wārid than an encumbered heart. Like the rain: it falls upon the just and the unjust, but only the cultivated soil draws fruit from it. For the advanced Sufis, the wāridāt follow one another: the way is not a straight line but a sequence of successive descents, which raise one each time by a degree. Important: not to demand the wāridāt; that would be spiritually vicious. One receives them, one gives thanks for them, one lets them depart.

voix_des_maitres.texte: If you awaited the wāridāt as you await your provisions, you would die of their awaiting — but they descend when you do not ask.
The wārid tears apart what was bound and binds what was torn — it remakes the human being in silence.

entries.meditation: Was there in my life a descent that changed everything — and did I give thanks for it?

entries.racine_sens: to open
entries.definition_concise: Opening — the moment when God suddenly opens to the servant a spiritual door that was until then shut. Stronger than mere kashf (الكَشْف) (the unveiling of a particular truth): the fatḥ opens a horizon, a new phase of the way.
entries.sens_profond: The fatḥ is the major spiritual event — the one one sometimes awaits for years, and which comes suddenly. Its characteristic: it opens what was shut. You read the Quran without understanding it — one day, fatḥ: it lights up. You practised the dhikr (الذِّكْر) without any savour — one day, fatḥ: you begin to feel it. You tried to leave an attachment — one day, fatḥ: it falls away of itself. The fatḥ cannot be forced. It comes when the soul is ready — and that preparation is the work of the years of faithful practice even when one feels nothing. The principal work of Ibn ʿArabī is called al-Futūḥāt al-Makkiyya — The Meccan Openings, because written during a stay in Mecca where he received a cascade of fatḥs. All his complex cosmology is the writing of what was opened to him. For the ordinary traveller, the fatḥs are rare but crucial: they mark the thresholds. Sūra 110 of the Quran is entitled an-Naṣr (the Succour) but opens with: “When the succour of God comes, and the opening (fatḥ)…” The outer fatḥ (the victories of the Prophet) is also the inner fatḥ (the opening of the heart).

voix_des_maitres.texte: We have indeed granted you a resounding opening (fatḥ).
The fatḥ comes when one has ceased to wait — when the soul has renounced its own effort.

entries.meditation: Is there a door I feel to be shut — that I should cease to push, and let be opened?

entries.racine_sens: to serve — the root shared by ʿibāda (worship), ʿabd (servant), ʿubūdiyya (servanthood)
entries.definition_concise: The servant of God — the highest title given to the human being. The Prophet Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) himself is called ʿabdihi wa rasūluh (His servant and His messenger) — servant before messenger.
entries.sens_profond: For the Sufis, to truly become ʿabd (servant) is the summit of the way — higher even than to become walī (وَلِيّ) (saint). Why? Because the walī may still have charisms, manifestations, a renown — so many things into which the ego can slip. The pure ʿabd has nothing of his own left: neither a will of his own, nor an expected gain, nor a sought renown. He is transparent to his Lord. The Prophet, the most perfect of human beings, chooses this designation: “I am only a servant.” And at the beginning of sūrat al-Isrāʾ (17:1), God designates Him for the nocturnal Journey: “Glory to the One who made His servant journey.” Not His prophet, not His friend, not His messenger — His servant. It is as ʿabd that he ascends to the heavens. This intuition is crucial: pure servanthood is higher than apparent sainthood. The summit is not to become someone — it is to be nothing more than Him through oneself. This is why the true saints always insist: “I am nothing.” This word, in their mouth, is not modesty — it is an exact diagnosis.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Glory to the One who made His servant journey from the sacred Mosque to the far-distant Mosque.
I am only a servant; I eat as the servant eats, and I sit as the servant sits.
Pure servanthood (ʿubūdiyya (عُبُودِيَّة)) is higher than sainthood — for the saint still has something; the servant has nothing left but Him.

entries.meditation: If I were asked tomorrow what I am — would I answer first ‘servant,’ or something else?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as ʿabd, ʿibāda — the condition of servant
entries.definition_concise: Pure servanthood — the state of the soul that has stripped itself of every will of its own, of every claim, of every interference. Distinct from ʿibāda (عِبَادَة) (the act of worship): ʿubūdiyya is the state from which worship flows.
entries.sens_profond: For the Sufis, there is a subtle hierarchy: ʿibāda is the act of worshipping (to pray, to fast, dhikr (الذِّكْر)); ʿubūdiyya is the being of the servant, which makes all the acts natural. You can do the ʿibāda without having the ʿubūdiyya — you practise out of discipline, without your being being imbued with it. You can have the ʿubūdiyya without displaying much ʿibāda — your whole life has become worship, whether it takes the form of prayer or of a meal taken in presence. Al-Qushayrī, in his Risāla, says: “ʿubūdiyya is higher than ʿibāda.” Why? Because ʿibāda is still something that you do — hence still a trace of you. ʿubūdiyya is that you are no longer — there remains nothing but Him acting through you. It is the very condition of Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد): “It was not you who threw, when you threw, but it was God who threw” (Quran 8:17). This word, addressed to the Prophet, is the very definition of ʿubūdiyya: your act is His act; you are no longer anything but the hand by which He acts.

voix_des_maitres.texte: ʿubūdiyya is higher than ʿibāda — the one is the act, the other is the being.
It was not you who threw, when you threw — it was God who threw.

entries.meditation: Is there within me an act that has become a being — where I no longer even have to ‘do’?

entries.racine_sens: to see, to be seen — hence riyāʾ: to make oneself seen, to act in order to be seen
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual ostentation — to act piously not for God, but in order to be seen by people. A subtle and ravaging vice: called by the Prophet “the lesser shirk” (hidden associationism).
entries.sens_profond: The hadith says: “What I fear most for my community is the lesser shirk. — What is the lesser shirk? — Riyāʾ.” Why is it so dreaded? Because it devours the works: you may perform acts apparently perfect — prayer, charity, fasting — but if a fraction of you wanted to be seen or admired, the act is spoiled. The mīzān (the Balance) weighs only what is for Him. Riyāʾ is insidious because it mingles imperceptibly with the intention: you pray in the night, sincerely, but in the morning you catch yourself mentioning it to a friend so that he may know. All is lost. The defence: a constant examination of the intention (muḥāsaba), hidden practice (to hide one's acts more than to hide one's faults — according to the counsel of the ancients), to refuse flattery. Hadith: “When you see those who are declared saints, throw dust in their beards” (to break the excessive flattery that would ruin them). And the ultimate criterion: the act you could do in the same way, whether seen or not, is sincere. The act you modify according to whether you are watched or not is tainted with riyāʾ.

voix_des_maitres.texte: What I fear most for my community is the lesser shirk — riyāʾ.
Find sincerity, you will find life. Find riyāʾ, you will find death in disguise.

entries.meditation: This act I have just done — would I have done it exactly the same way if no one had known?

entries.racine_sens: to marvel, to admire
entries.definition_concise: Self-complacency — the self-admiration of one's own works or qualities. A vice subtly different from riyāʾ: riyāʾ wants to be seen by others; ʿujb sees itself with pleasure. Often harder to detect.
entries.sens_profond: If riyāʾ is ostentation turned toward the outside, ʿujb is ostentation turned toward oneself. One looks at oneself and finds oneself good. “How sincere I am! How humble I am! How detached I am!” — as soon as one congratulates oneself, one is lost. ʿujb is more subtle than riyāʾ: one can be alone, in secret, and fall into ʿujb. Many aspirants fall into this trap after a few years of practice: they see their progress and admire themselves for it. All is then lost at a stroke. The antidote: to recall that what you have, you have received. If you pray in the night, it is He who woke you. If you fast, it is He who gave you health. If you are patient, it is He who gave you patience. Every merit you attribute to yourself is a theft: it was not yours. The hadith of Jonah is striking: fallen into the belly of the whale, he says: “Lā ilāha illā Anta, subḥānaka, innī kuntu min aẓ-ẓālimīn” — “No god but You, glory to You, I have indeed been among the wrongdoers.” Jonah is a prophet. And he says “I have been a wrongdoer.” It is this gaze upon oneself that saves, not the reverse.

voix_des_maitres.texte: ʿujb destroys fifty years of works in an instant.
If you see good in yourself, know that it comes from Him; and if He takes it from you, nothing will remain to you.

entries.meditation: When I admire myself — for what, exactly? And who had given it to me?

entries.racine: ن ف س + ل ه م
entries.racine_sens: nafs (soul) + mulhama (inspired)
entries.definition_concise: The fourth degree of the nafs (النَّفْس) in the expanded Sufi cartography — between the lawwāma (which reproaches itself) and the muṭmaʾinna (مُطْمَئِنَّة) (at peace). The soul to which God begins to inspire directly the discernment between good and evil.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran (91:7-8) says: “And by the soul, and the One who fashioned it; and He inspired into it (alhamahā) its waywardness and its piety.” Every human being receives, to a degree, this inspiration — but in the nafs mulhama (النَّفْسُ المُلْهَمَة), it becomes clear, reliable, distinct. This degree is intermediate: the soul is not yet wholly at peace (muṭmaʾinna), but it has come out of mere self-criticism (lawwāma). It perceives directly what must be done, by an inner light that guides. Many classical authors expand the Quranic triad (ammāra, lawwāma, muṭmaʾinna) into seven degrees: (1) ammāra, (2) lawwāma, (3) mulhama, (4) muṭmaʾinna, (5) rāḍiya (رَاضِيَة) (content), (6) marḍiyya (مَرْضِيَّة) (accepted), (7) kāmila (perfect, like that of the major saints). This sevenfold cartography corresponds also to seven inner colours seen in khalwa (الخَلْوَة) (retreat), according to Najm al-Dīn al-Kubrā. At each degree, the practice changes: the ammāra aspirant needs firm discipline; the mulhama needs a fine attention to the inspirations that descend. The way is never the same according to where one is.

voix_des_maitres.texte: And by the soul, and the One who fashioned it — and He inspired into it its waywardness and its piety.
When the nafs becomes mulhama, it hears God speak to it without words.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a voice that knows, without having learned?

entries.racine_sens: to walk, to travel
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual journey. A classical distinction of Ibn ʿArabī: sayr (السَّيْر) ilā Llāh (the journey toward God — from the servant to his Lord); sayr fī Llāh (the journey in God — in which one traverses His Names and His Attributes).
entries.sens_profond: For Ibn ʿArabī and the Akbarian tradition, the spiritual journey has several stages: (1) the journey toward God (sayr ilā Llāh) — the ascending phase, in which the servant moves away from the dunyā and draws near to the divine; (2) the journey in God (sayr fī Llāh) — the inner phase, in which, having found, one explores the depths: the Names, the Attributes, the infinite theophanies; (3) the journey from God toward the creatures (sayr min Allāh (اَللَّٰه) ilā-l-khalq) — the descending phase, in which the accomplished saint comes back down into the world to serve, to teach, to transmit. This last journey is essential: the walī (وَلِيّ) is not the one who remains lost in God — it is the one who returns from Him to help others to come. It is the movement found in the myth of Plato's cave: the philosopher goes out toward the light, but must come back down to free the others. The Prophet is the archetype of these three complete movements: ascension during the Miʿrāj, journey in God during the revelation, return to found the community.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Three journeys: toward Him, in Him, from Him. As long as you have not made all three, you are not accomplished.
The traveller stops nowhere — neither in the dunyā, nor even in the ākhira; he walks until Him.

entries.meditation: Where am I in my journey — at the beginning, on the way, or beginning to return to help others?

entries.racine_sens: to denude, to strip — hence mujarrad: bare, pure
entries.definition_concise: Stripping-away — to detach the soul from everything other than God. More radical than zuhd (الزُّهْد): one strips oneself not only of goods, but of the subtle attachments — opinions, identifications, self-satisfaction.
entries.sens_profond: Tajrīd is the work of the advanced traveller. At the beginning, zuhd suffices: to detach oneself from material goods. But then one must go further — to strip oneself of the subtle dependencies: one's social identity, one's status, one's reputation, one's political opinions, one's moral judgements upon others, and even one's spiritual states (the wāridāt one would like to keep). All that one becomes attached to becomes a veil. Tajrīd is the continuous operation of loosening these attachments. But beware: true tajrīd is not a flight. One can be married and a father of a family while being in tajrīd: you live with what is given to you, you give thanks for it, but you know that none of these things is yours. When they leave you, you do not collapse. Criterion: what makes you suffer to lose is what you are still attached to. Hadith: “Love what you will, but you will be separated from it; live as long as you will, but you will die; do what you will, but you will be requited for it.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: Love what you will, but you will be separated from it; live as long as you will, but you will die.
You will truly strip your soul only in proportion to what you know of God — love for Him drives out the lesser loves.

entries.meditation: What it would make me suffer the most to lose — what does that tell me of my tajrīd?

entries.racine_sens: to be alone, unique, isolated
entries.definition_concise: Singularisation — the inner corollary of tajrīd. If tajrīd strips from the outside, tafrīd singularises the heart by leaving in it God alone. A constant pair: tajrīd zāhir, tafrīd bāṭin.
entries.sens_profond: Tafrīd is the complementary and inner movement of tajrīd. While you strip yourself on the outside (tajrīd: fewer possessions, fewer dependencies, fewer attachments), you singularise yourself on the inside — you no longer place God in competition with anything whatever. Your heart, which once sheltered a thousand preoccupations, becomes a singular: Him, and nothing else. This does not mean that you cease to love, to care, to act in the world — on the contrary, you do all this better, because everything henceforth passes through Him. You love your child in Him, you work for Him, you rest because of Him. Nothing more is a competitor. It is what the qudsī hadith says: “My servant does not cease to draw near to Me through the supererogatory works, until I love him. And when I love him, I become his hearing by which he hears, his sight by which he sees.” Tafrīd is that moment when God becomes the unifying centre — not an additional object in the consciousness, but the subject of consciousness itself.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Tajrīd is the work of the hand; tafrīd is the work of the heart.
Tafrīd: that your heart no longer has to choose between Him and anything else.

entries.meditation: If I could keep only one thing in my heart — what would it be?

entries.racine_sens: to direct toward rectitude — hence rushd, the right path
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual guide — a near-synonym of shaykh (الشَّيْخ), with a nuance: murshid stresses the function of directing, of leading. The word is much used in the Persian and Turkish tradition.
entries.sens_profond: If shaykh is the honorific title of the master (the elder), murshid stresses his function: he directs the disciple upon the straight path (rushd). The two words are nearly interchangeable, but murshid evokes more the active direction — that of a mountain guide, who knows the passes, anticipates the difficulties, opens the way. In the Turkish tradition (Mevleviyya, Bektashiyya), mürşid is the common title. In Iran: morshed. The true murshid has three qualities according to Najm al-Dīn al-Kubrā: (1) to have himself traversed the path (only the one who knows by experience directs well); (2) to have been authorised by his own master to teach (the silsila (السِّلْسِلَة)); (3) to have the discernment needed to adapt his teaching to each disciple (the way is not the same for all). The false murshid is recognised by several signs: he flatters, he asks for money, he claims to possess hidden secrets in exchange for payment, he denigrates the other masters, he attaches himself to his disciple rather than orienting him toward God. To be fled.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Whomever God wishes to guide, He opens his breast to islam — and sends him a guide.
Three conditions for the murshid: to have traversed the path, to have been authorised, to know how to adapt.

entries.meditation: Who has truly guided me in my life — in the sense that he corrected my steps?

entries.racine_sens: literally old, aged — hence the spiritual master as the aged sage
entries.definition_concise: The pīr — the Persian equivalent of the Arabic shaykh (الشَّيْخ). A word much used in the Persian, Turkish and Indian brotherhoods. The pīr is the elder, the sage who guides.
entries.sens_profond: The pīr is, in the Persian and Indian tradition, what the shaykh is in the Arabic tradition: the spiritual master, the initiatic authority. The word comes from the Persian pīr, which literally means old, aged. This etymology is not trivial: it says that wisdom comes with experience and with time. A pīr is not only someone who knows — it is someone who has lived, who has held out in the long term, who has worn out his shoes on the path. This idea is common to both traditions: one calls the master shaykh (the elder) in Arabic or pīr (the elder) in Persian — always, it is the initiatic age that counts, not the biological age. One can be a shaykh at 30 (Rūmī was) or never be one at 80 (most of us). In Persian poetry (Ḥāfiẓ, Saʿdī, Rūmī), the pīr-i mughān (the magian master, the master of the Magi) often denotes the Sufi master disguised as a pre-Islamic figure — in order to escape religious censorship while saying the universal spiritual truth. The pīr is thus at once rooted in Islam and open to the ancient wisdom.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Without the pīr, do not enter the winehouse; without him, you would lose yourself before the first cup.
The pīr is the echo of the first Voice — whoever does not recognise his voice has lost himself in the noise.

entries.meditation: If I had to name an elder in my life — who would correspond to this image?

entries.racine_sens: to make grow, to raise — a root shared with rabb (Lord, the one who makes grow)
entries.definition_concise: Spiritual education — the patient work of the shaykh (الشَّيْخ) upon his disciple, which makes him grow in the manner of a gardener with a young shoot. Distinct from the mere transmission of information: tarbiya (التَّرْبِيَة) transforms.
entries.sens_profond: The root r-b-w is rich: it yields rabb (Lord, the one who makes grow), tarbiya (education), ribā (usurious interest — what grows without legitimate work). Tarbiya is therefore to make grow — patiently, as the gardener makes the tree grow. Five paths are distinguished in the shaykh-disciple relationship: (1) shaykh at-taʿlīm — the master of teaching, who transmits the doctrinal knowledge; (2) shaykh at-tarbiya — the master of education, who forms the character; (3) shaykh as-sulūk (السُّلُوك) — the master of wayfaring, who guides the spiritual way; (4) shaykh at-tarqiya — the master of elevation, who raises one above oneself; (5) shaykh as-suḥba (شَيْخُ الصُّحْبَة) — the master of companionship, who forms by his mere presence. Tarbiya, the second of these paths, is the one that forms the character (akhlāq). It is done through a thousand small gestures: a correction at the just moment, a silence that says much, a service asked that reveals a failing, a compliment that defuses a pride. The master looks at his disciple as one looks at a plant: he knows what light it needs, what watering, what pruning. And he knows above all when to wait.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The shaykh does not correct you by words alone; he corrects you by the trials that his presence attracts.
Our tarbiya is silent — it is transmitted through hearts, not through mouths.

entries.meditation: Who has already made me grow in my life, by his mere presence?

entries.racine_sens: the same root as ḥuḍūr — to be present, to attend
entries.definition_concise: The ritual gathering of dhikr (الذِّكْر) — a collective session in which the brothers of the ṭarīqa (الطَّرِيقَة) gather to invoke God together, often with chant and movement. A central moment of Sufi life.
entries.sens_profond: The ḥaḍra is the experience of shared presence. Alone, one prays. Together, one enters another field — one heart aflame kindles another, and the warmth rises. Each ṭarīqa has its own ḥaḍra: some are silent (Naqshbandiyya — the dhikr of the heart), others loud (Shādhiliyya, Qādiriyya — the dhikr of the tongue with movement), others danced (Mevleviyya — the whirling samāʿ). The typical unfolding: an opening with the Fātiḥa and invocations upon the Prophet; the collective recitation of formulas (lā ilāha illā Llāh (لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّٰه), Allāh (اَللَّٰه), Hū) according to established rhythms; sometimes a gradual rising toward trance; a closing with silent dhikr and duʿāʾ. The ḥaḍra is not a concert nor a spectacle: it is a discipline of shared presence, in which each one effaces himself in the group and the whole group effaces itself before God. When it is well led, the ḥaḍra produces in a few hours transformations that would have taken months in solitary practice. This is why all the masters have held this practice to be irreplaceable.

voix_des_maitres.texte: God has troops of angels who roam the earth, seeking the circles of dhikr; when they find one, they sit down with the invokers and spread their wings over them.
The ḥaḍra makes of the individual dhikr a cosmic dhikr — suddenly, the angels recite with you.

entries.meditation: Have I already felt, in a gathering, that a field was being created beyond the sum of those present?

entries.racine_sens: to form a circle, a ring
entries.definition_concise: The circle — the ritual arrangement in which the Sufis gather for the dhikr (الذِّكْر). Symbolic: no horizontal hierarchy in the circle, all equidistant from the invisible centre (God).
entries.sens_profond: The ḥalqa is the geometric form of the ḥaḍra. The circle has a profound meaning: all the points of the circle are at an equal distance from the centre. When the Sufis gather in a circle, it is to signify visibly their equality before God — no first, no last, no one nearer to the shaykh (الشَّيْخ) on the spiritual plane. The shaykh, if he is present, places himself within the circle (and not above it); some orders rotate the shaykh's position at each ḥaḍra to avoid any fixation. More deeply, the circle is also cosmic: it says that every thing turns around a Centre that is not seen — like the planets around the sun, like the electrons around the nucleus. And at the centre of the ḥalqa: not an object, but the invisible — Allāh (اَللَّٰه). The invocations set out from the circle and return to the centre, like the rays that set out from the sun and return to it. This sacred geometry is found everywhere: the Kaʿba around which one turns, the Mevlevi dervishes who turn around their heart, the angels around the Throne. The circle is the image of the return to the One.

voix_des_maitres.texte: God has angels who roam the earth seeking the ḥalqas of dhikr.
We turn in a circle around a centre that does not turn — and it is that centre that makes us turn.

entries.meditation: What, at this instant, is at the centre of my life — and does all the rest turn around it?

entries.racine_sens: corner, recess — hence zāwiya, the angle, the secluded place
entries.definition_concise: The Sufi oratory — a mainly Maghrebi term. A secluded place where a Sufi community gathers around a shaykh (الشَّيْخ) for practice, teaching and service. Often organised around the tomb of a founding saint.
entries.sens_profond: The zāwiya is the institutional lung of Maghrebi Sufism. The word literally means the angle, the secluded corner — and denotes by extension the whole: a building that includes the mosque for prayer, the hall for the ḥaḍra, the tomb of the founding saint, sometimes a Quranic school, rooms for the pilgrims, a garden, kitchens. In Morocco, Algeria, Tunisia, Mauritania, the zāwiyas structured for centuries the spiritual, social and educational life — often more effectively than the state institutions. Many major historical figures of the Maghreb came out of zāwiyas: the education of elites, the training of judges, the welcoming of the poor and of travellers, the mediation of tribal conflicts. The zāwiya also has an ecological dimension: it sometimes manages agricultural lands, distributes water, protects forests. Today, many zāwiyas survive — the Shādhiliyya, the Darqāwiyya, the Tijāniyya, the Qādiriyya are active. The equivalent in other regions: khānaqāh (Iran, Central Asia, India), tekke (Ottoman Turkey), ribāṭ (medieval, more military in origin).

voix_des_maitres.texte: The zāwiya is the womb in which the soul is reborn — do not enter it if you do not wish to die to yourself.
Our zāwiya has no visible walls — it is everywhere two brothers remember God together.

entries.meditation: Have I a place that I enter to remember Him — were it but a corner of a room?

entries.racine_sens: literally house of hospitality
entries.definition_concise: The Sufi convent in the Persian, Indian and Turkic world. The Eastern equivalent of the Maghrebi zāwiya. A centre of practice, teaching and welcome. The Chishtiyya in India and the Naqshbandiyya in Central Asia made khānaqāhs their lung.
entries.sens_profond: The word khānaqāh comes from the Persian: khāna (house) + qāh (place) — the house-place, the open house. It is the opposite of a private house: the khānaqāh is a house of hospitality, which welcomes pilgrims, the poor, travellers, without condition. The Chishtiyya in India carried this practice to a summit: the great khānaqāhs of Niẓām al-Dīn Awliyāʾ in Delhi fed thousands of poor people every day, open seven days a week, without distinction of religion. Whoever entered received food, listened to the sacred music (qawwālī), could ask for counsel. For the Chishtiyya, it is khidma (الخِدْمَة) (service) that proves the sincerity of the way: you can perform a thousand practices, if you do not feed the hungry neighbour, you have understood nothing. The Naqshbandiyya in Central Asia evolved the khānaqāh toward a more discreet model: less public service, more silent meditation, but still a place where the brothers on the way come together. The great khānaqāhs of Bukhara, Samarkand, formed generations of scholars and saints. Today, many have been destroyed (Soviet, Chinese regimes) or turned into museums — but the model continues, sometimes discreetly, in private houses.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Our khānaqāh is open to all — whoever knocks at the door receives what there is, were it the last piece of bread.
The khānaqāh is not a wall — it is the invitation that radiates from a silent heart.

entries.meditation: Is there, in my life, a place one enters without asking — and what does one receive?

entries.racine_sens: hawā — to fall, to descend; to incline, to desire
entries.definition_concise: The inclination of the heart toward what is not God — the natural slope of the nafs (النَّفْس) toward its own enjoyments, blindly. The hawā is the inner idol: one obeys it without even knowing it.
entries.sens_profond: The Arabic root h-w-y means to fall: the hawā is what makes the soul descend from its vertical orientation. The Quran says: “Have you seen the one who has taken his hawā for a god?” (45:23) — the hawā takes the place of God in the heart, and the one who follows it does not even notice it, for he believes he is following his own will, when he is obeying a disguised inner god. For the Sufis, the hawā is not desire in itself (which can be healthy), but desire become master, which dictates to the heart what it must love and flee. All the work of the way is to unlearn this blind obedience — to turn the soul back toward God alone (ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص)). Al-Junayd: “The way is closed to every creature, save the one who follows the footsteps of the Messenger and who opposes his hawā.” The hawā is the most intimate enemy: it speaks with your voice.

voix_des_maitres.texte: As for the one who fears the standing before his Lord and who forbids his soul the hawā — the Garden will be his dwelling.

The root of all disobedience, of all heedlessness and of all passion is to be content with oneself. And the root of all obedience, of all vigilance and of all chastity is not to be content with oneself.

entries.meditation: What is the hawā that I have not yet recognised — the one I serve while believing I serve God?

entries.racine_sens: shahā — to desire ardently, to covet
entries.definition_concise: The appetite that pushes the soul toward what it savours — food, sex, possession, recognition. The shahwa (الشَّهْوَة) is not bad in itself: it becomes a trap when it takes the place of the goal.
entries.sens_profond: The shahwa denotes every desire of appropriation — to eat, to possess, to enjoy, to be seen. The Sufis distinguish the shahawāt of the belly, of sex, of the gaze, of the tongue. None is condemned in itself: the way is not a refusal of the body, but a re-centring. The danger comes when the shahwa becomes a goal instead of a sign — when one eats in order to eat, and not in order to subsist; when one shows oneself in order to be seen, and not in order to transmit. Al-Ghazālī devotes an entire book of the Iḥyāʾ to the shahawāt of the belly and of sex: not in order to suppress them, but in order to inhabit them with awareness. The classical Sufi discipline (fasting, retreat, vigil, silence) aims to reduce the grip of the shahawāt until the soul is light enough to hear the subtle voice. The opposite of the shahwa is not asceticism: it is ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص) — the simplicity of desire oriented toward God alone.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The belly is the source of all the shahawāt and the hearth of all the illnesses.

entries.meditation: Which shahwa governs me without my knowing it — to the point that I call it “need”?

entries.racine: ح ب ب / د ن و
entries.racine_sens: ḥubb — love; dunyā — the near, the here-below
entries.definition_concise: Attachment to the here-below as to a goal in itself. According to a famous prophetic saying: “The love of the world is the root of every fault.” Not the world as such — but the love that holds back.
entries.sens_profond: The word dunyā means the nearest — the immediate, present life. The way does not ask one to flee the world (classical Sufism condemns rahbāniyya, monasticism), but not to take it for the ultimate. Ḥubb ad-dunyā (الدُّنْيَا) is to love creation as one should love the Creator: to confuse the sign with what it indicates. The hadith says: “The love of the world is the root (raʾs) of every fault.” Why the root? Because all the other illnesses — pride, envy, greed, anger — are born of this fundamental confusion: to take what passes for what remains. Sufi detachment is not an obligatory material poverty — it is inner faqr (الفَقْر), the soul that knows that nothing of what it possesses is its own. Al-Ḥasan al-Baṣrī: “The world is a bridge — cross it, but do not build yourself a dwelling upon it.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: The love of the world is the root of every fault.
The world is a bridge — cross it, do not build upon it.

entries.meditation: What, in my daily life, chains me to what passes — as if it did not pass?

entries.racine_sens: kabura — to be great; kibr — to make oneself great
entries.definition_concise: To believe oneself above others — the illness that closes the heart to truth and to creatures. For the Sufis, it is the first veil and the last to fall.
entries.sens_profond: Kibr is defined by the hadith as “the rejection of truth and the contempt of people.” It is the most dreaded spiritual illness because it hides better than the others: one can accomplish a thousand practices while being inwardly superior — and this invisible superior poisons everything. Iblīs did not fall through covetousness but through kibr: he refused to prostrate himself before Adam because he believed himself better. Kibr is therefore the origin of the fall, the cosmic original sin. The Sufis distinguish it from ʿujb (العُجْب) (self-complacency, which can exist alone): kibr expresses itself against the other. Its cure is not outward humiliation — which can feed a more subtle pride (“look how humble I am”) — but the clear awareness that every quality comes from God, and that nothing of what one is belongs to oneself. The opposite of kibr is not baseness, it is just ʿubūdiyya (عُبُودِيَّة): to be in one's place as a servant, neither more, nor less.

voix_des_maitres.texte: He will not enter the Garden who has in his heart an atom of kibr.
When the ego effaces itself, the King enters. The only obstacle is you.

entries.meditation: Before whom do I feel superior — even without saying it? And who am I really, without what I have attributed to myself?
