entries.racine_sens: gharra — to deceive, to lure
entries.definition_concise: To let oneself be lured — by the world, by oneself, by one's own piety. The ghurūr is the veil that makes one believe one has arrived, when one is only just beginning.
entries.sens_profond: The ghurūr covers all the forms of false security: to believe one has time, to believe one is saved, to believe that God makes an exception for oneself, to believe that one's practices suffice. Al-Ghazālī closes the Iḥyāʾ ʿulūm ad-dīn with an entire book (Kitāb al-Ghurūr) on the delusions of the devout — he distinguishes the ghurūr of the scholars, of the ascetics, of the Sufis, of the rich, of the poor: each has his way of believing himself to have arrived. The most dangerous ghurūr is not in the sinners (who know their state), but in the pious who take their own works for a capital. The hadith says: “If God wished to punish a man by his best act, He could.” Every work is suspect as long as it has not taught us poverty (faqr (الفَقْر)). The way out of ghurūr passes through muḥāsaba (المُحَاسَبَة) — the severe examination of the soul — and through the remembrance of death, which brings one back to the truth.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The worst of the deluded is the one who takes his delusion for a certainty.

entries.meditation: What is the delusion about myself that I dare not look in the face?

entries.racine_sens: ḥasada — to envy, to desire the loss of a good in another
entries.definition_concise: To wish the disappearance of a good in another. Ḥasad accuses God — it says: You have shared badly. It consumes the one who feels it before any other.
entries.sens_profond: Ḥasad goes further than simple jealousy (which can be healthy, the ghibṭa, the desire to have too). Envy is black: it desires that the other lose. For the Sufis, it is a direct accusation brought against God: “You have given to that one what You should have given to me.” The hadith says: “Ḥasad devours the good deeds as the fire devours the dry wood.” Ḥasad is also the cause of the first death on earth — that of Abel by Cain — and of the fall of Iblīs (who envied Adam). It is therefore, like kibr (الكِبْر), a cosmogonic illness. Its cure passes through: recognising that every gift comes from God and is not a due; blessing the other inwardly each time one has a movement of envy; and, more deeply, rejoicing because the other receives — becoming maḥabba (مَحَبَّة), capable of joy for what does not come to us.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Beware of ḥasad: it devours the good deeds as the fire devours the dry wood.

entries.meditation: Before the grace received by another, what is my first thought — truly?

entries.racine_sens: ghaḍiba — to flare up, to take fire
entries.definition_concise: The fire that rises when the self is wounded. Ghaḍab is not bad in itself (it has its place in defending the truth), but become master, it blinds.
entries.sens_profond: Ghaḍab is, for the Sufis, one of the three inner fires (with the shahwa (الشَّهْوَة) and ḥasad). It comes when the nafs (النَّفْس) feels itself attacked, scorned, crossed. The way does not ask one to extinguish this fire — there is a just anger, that of the prophets against injustice — but to contain it. The Prophet said: “The strong one is not the one who throws men down, but the one who masters himself in anger.” The ancients recommend: to change posture (standing → seated → lying down), to fall silent, to perform the ablutions (water extinguishes fire), to defer the response. More deeply, ghaḍab unveils what one truly loves: one flares up for what threatens one's idols. To examine one's angers is to map one's attachments. The opposite term is ḥilm (حِلْم) — gentle patience, which is one of the most beautiful Names of God: al-Ḥalīm (اَلْحَلِيم), the Forbearing.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The strong one is not the one who throws men down, but the one who masters himself in anger.

entries.meditation: What did my recent angers want to defend — truly?

entries.racine_sens: waswasa — to whisper, to suggest in secret
entries.definition_concise: The low voice that suggests doubt, transgression, discouragement — coming from the shayṭān, from the nafs (النَّفْس), or from a folding of the heart upon itself. To recognise the waswasa is already to be no longer its dupe.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran names the enemy al-waswās al-khannās — the one who whispers and withdraws. The waswasa is the art of suspicion: it does not attack frontally, it insinuates. You are not sincere. Your prayer was worthless. God does not listen to you. You can put it off until tomorrow. The Sufis distinguish four possible sources for a thought that surges in the heart (the khawāṭir (خَوَاطِر)): divine (rabbānī), angelic (malakī), of the nafs (النَّفْس) (nafsānī), satanic (shayṭānī). To learn to distinguish them is a whole art of discernment. The simple rule: what diminishes you in trust and in the impulse toward God is never from Him. The waswasa can also become pathological — scruples about the ablutions, doubts about the prayer redone ten times. The masters then recommend not to answer it: the waswasa goes out as soon as one ceases to argue with it.

voix_des_maitres.texte: From the evil of the sly whisperer — who whispers in the breasts of human beings.

entries.meditation: What is the low voice that recurs most within me? If I listen to it, where does it lead me?

entries.racine_sens: zakā — to grow, to become pure; tazkiya — active purification
entries.definition_concise: The central work of Sufism: to purify the nafs (النَّفْس) of its veils so that it may reflect the light it receives. “Blessed is the one who purifies it” (Quran 91:9).
entries.sens_profond: The word tazkiya contains two senses: to purify and to make grow. It is not only to remove flaws — it is to nourish a quality until it takes up all the room. The Quran systematically links the prophetic mission to tazkiya: “He recites to them His signs, purifies them, and teaches them the Book and wisdom” (62:2) — the order matters: one truly receives knowledge only after the purification. For the Sufis, all the way is tazkiyat an-nafs (النَّفْس): from the combat against the illnesses of the heart (kibr (الكِبْر), ḥasad, ḥubb ad-dunyā (حُبُّ الدُّنْيَا), hawā, riyāʾ…) to the transformation of the human qualities into divine attributes (takhalluq bi-akhlāq Allāh (اَللَّٰه)). It is what is sometimes called the spiritual chemistry: to transmute the lead of the nafs ammāra (النَّفْس) into the gold of the nafs muṭmaʾinna (نَفْسٌ مُطْمَئِنَّة). The true master is the one who knows how to guide this purification — it is his first role, more even than to transmit knowledge.

voix_des_maitres.texte: By a soul and the One who fashioned it, and inspired into it its sin as well as its piety! Blessed is the one who purifies it, ruined the one who corrupts it.

entries.meditation: What is the illness of the heart that I am working on at this moment — and the one I dare not even look at?

entries.racine_sens: anā — me, I; ananiyya — the fact of saying ‘I’
entries.definition_concise: The feeling of being a separate, autonomous I — the fundamental veil that the way must traverse. As long as I truly say ‘I,’ I have not yet begun.
entries.sens_profond: Ananiyya is more radical than the nafs (النَّفْس) ammāra. It is not a moral flaw: it is the structure of the self inasmuch as it believes itself autonomous. As long as I think, judge, act from the I centre, I am in ananiyya — even when I do good, even when I pray, even when I believe I love God. It is the most subtle egoity: the one that attributes its good actions to itself. Bāyazīd al-Bisṭāmī: “I saw God in a dream and He said to me: what do you bring Me? I said: asceticism. He said: I have no need of your asceticism. I said: poverty. He said: nor of that. I said: then what? He said: leave your ‘me’ and come.” Fanāʾ (الفَنَاء) is precisely the extinction of ananiyya — not the suppression of the person (that would be absurd), but the transparency of the I to the Thou. When al-Ḥallāj says Anā-l-Ḥaqq (أَنَا الْحَقّ) — I am the True — it is not his self that speaks: it is ananiyya itself that has dissolved, and there remains only the One who says Anā.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Leave your ‘me’ and come.
When the ego effaces itself, the Beloved enters by the door.

entries.meditation: When I say ‘I,’ what do I designate — truly?

entries.racine_sens: hamma — to conceive, to set oneself a purpose; himma — the force that strains toward
entries.definition_concise: The concentrated force of the heart strained toward its goal. The himma makes the aspirant; without it, every practice dilutes itself. For Ibn ʿArabī, it has a cosmological power: it orients the real.
entries.sens_profond: The himma is not mere psychological will. It is the unified resolve of the whole being toward a single point — God. The masters say: “You are only your himma.” The one who wants God from everywhere, with all his time, with all his desire — that one is his himma. The one who wants God a little, in his spare moments — that one is dispersed. For Ibn ʿArabī, the himma has a cosmological dimension: the heart of the saint, by its unified concentration, orients events themselves. This is why the prayer of the true servant is never formal — it holds the world. The opposite of himma is not laziness: it is tafriqa (تَفْرِقَة), dispersion — the soul pulled toward a hundred objects, which ends by no longer truly wanting any. The way begins by gathering the himma: making all the inner currents converge into a single bed.

voix_des_maitres.texte: How could the one whose himma is in the here-below claim to want God?
You are only your himma.

entries.meditation: My himma, at this instant — toward what is it truly strained?

entries.racine_sens: jadhaba — to pull, to attract
entries.definition_concise: The movement by which God pulls the soul to Himself without effort on its part — the grace that precedes all practice. A single instant of jadhb, it is said, is worth the work of the two worlds.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufi tradition recognises two complementary movements: the sulūk (السُّلُوك), the active wayfaring (the aspirant walks toward God), and the jadhb, the passive attraction (God pulls the aspirant to Himself). The classical adage says: “A single attraction of the True is worth all the works of human beings and jinn.” The jadhb may strike without warning — in the midst of a reading, an encounter, a samāʿ, sometimes with no apparent cause. It destabilises: the one seized by it may, for a time, no longer be able to speak, to eat, to keep his habitual gestures. It is the state of the majdhūb — the attracted one. The masters say that the complete way unites the two: sālik (سَالِك) majdhūb (the attracted wayfarer) — the one who walks, but whom God accompanies by pulling him. Without sulūk, the jadhb leaves the aspirant disordered; without jadhb, the sulūk dries up. Grace and effort seek each other mutually.

voix_des_maitres.texte: A single attraction of the True (jadhba) is worth all the works of human beings and jinn.

entries.meditation: Was there, in my life, a moment when I was pulled — without willing it, without meriting it?

entries.fr: The attracted one, seized by God
entries.racine_sens: majdhūb — the one who has been pulled, attracted
entries.definition_concise: The one whom God has seized by the jadhba (الجَذْب) — who remains in the attraction without always being able to traverse the ordinary wayfaring. A paradoxical figure: very near, and yet often disordered.
entries.sens_profond: The majdhūb is the one whose balance has tipped toward the side of attraction. He may lose the common usages — cleanliness, hours, social speech — without thereby being mad: he is inhabited. Traditional Muslim societies recognised the majādhīb as figures to be respected (and not imitated): they carry a raw presence of the sacred, but cannot guide — they lack the sulūk (السُّلُوك). Conversely, the pure sālik (سَالِك) without jadhb (الجَذْب) may know everything of the maps without ever having been touched. The classical ideal is the sālik majdhūb — the aspirant who wayfares and who is attracted. It is the profile of the true shaykh (الشَّيْخ): he has walked, he has been pulled, he knows at once the road and the seizing. Beware, say the masters, both of the madman who takes himself for a saint, and of the scholar who believes himself to have arrived without having been shaken.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The majdhūb is not a madman; the madman is not a majdhūb. And only an awakened heart knows how to distinguish the two.

entries.meditation: Am I only a wayfarer who walks — or have I accepted, sometimes, to be pulled without understanding?

entries.racine_sens: waqata — to fix a time; waqt — a precise moment
entries.definition_concise: The instant as it is given by God — the only place where the way is lived. The Sufi is the child of the instant: he does not run ahead, he does not linger.
entries.sens_profond: The waqt, in the technical vocabulary, does not denote time in general but the state in which God places the aspirant at a given moment — joy or tightening, opening or contraction, presence or absence. The golden rule: “The Sufi is the child of the instant” (ibn al-waqt (اِبْنُ الوَقْت)). It is not a matter of fleeing the past or the future: it is a matter of recognising that the only prayer possible is made now, with what God has given me now. If I am in joy, I must not scorn it in the name of an austerity of yesterday; if I am in tightening, I must not pretend to be elsewhere. Al-Junayd: “The waqt is precious: if you let it pass, it does not return.” Higher still, some masters speak of ṣāhib al-waqt (صَاحِبُ الوَقْت) — the one who is no longer the child of the instant but the master of the instant: God obeys him, in a manner of speaking, because he has wholly handed himself over to Him.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The Sufi is the child of the instant.
The waqt is precious: if you let it pass, it does not return.

entries.meditation: What is the state that God gives me now — and do I have the courage to take it as it is?

entries.racine: ب ن و / و ق ت
entries.racine_sens: ibn — son; waqt — instant
entries.definition_concise: The accomplished Sufi: the one who takes what God gives him in the present instant, without regret for what has been, without anticipation of what will come.
entries.sens_profond: The expression ibn al-waqt (الوَقْت) is one of the classical definitions of the Sufi: “The Sufi is the child of the instant.” To be the child of the instant is to obey what this instant requires — not what another instant required. If the instant asks for joy, I do not pretend to be in sadness. If the instant asks for effort, I do not take refuge in a past grace. It is a subtle discipline: the self would like to continue what it loved, or to anticipate what will come. The ibn al-waqt breaks with this heaviness. But beware: it is not a matter of inconstancy — quite the contrary, it is the greatest fidelity. Faithful not to one's own states, but to the One who gives the states. Rūmī: “Be like a cup that does not know what is poured into it — be the child of the instant.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: The Sufi is the child of the instant.

entries.meditation: Am I living this instant, or am I trying to make another instant live again within it?

entries.racine_sens: nafs — soul; nafas — breath (the same root)
entries.definition_concise: The breath — a fundamental unit of the spiritual life. Each breath is a capital: “Count your breaths,” say the masters. The Naqshbandī hūsh dar dam — awareness in the breath — makes the breath itself the place of the dhikr (الذِّكْر).
entries.sens_profond: For the Sufis, the breath (nafas (النَّفَس)) and the soul (nafs (النَّفْس)) have the same root: what animates a body is what breathes it. Each inhalation receives, each exhalation gives — the spiritual economy is modelled on this alternation. The masters say: “The sage counts his breaths.” This means two things: first, precariousness — each breath may be the last; then, dignity — each breath is a gift, an instant offered. The Naqshbandī way systematises this work in the rule hūsh dar dam (in Persian: awareness in the breath) — each breath is inhabited by the remembrance of God. At the inhalation, the Name is breathed in; at the exhalation, it is offered. The breath becomes itself a prayer. Ibn ʿArabī speaks of the nafas ar-Raḥmān (نَفَسُ الرَّحْمَٰن) — the Breath of the Merciful: God, by His Breath, gives existence to every thing. Our breath, then, is a participation in this universal Breath.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Hūsh dar dam — awareness in the breath. Each respiration inhabited by God.

entries.meditation: This breath I am taking now — did I truly feel it, or has it already passed without me?

entries.racine_sens: yaqiẓa — to awaken, to come out of sleep
entries.definition_concise: The very first station of the way: to awaken. As long as one sleeps in ghafla (الغَفْلَة), one is dead. The yaqẓa is the instant when one perceives that one has lived without truly having been there.
entries.sens_profond: Before any other station, the yaqẓa is needed. It is what makes the way possible. Without it, every practice is mechanical, every pious thought is on the surface. The hadith says: “People are asleep; when they die, they awaken.” The yaqẓa is not to wait for death in order to awaken. It can spring up in a thousand ways: the meeting of a master, the reading of a verse, a trial, the simple looking of death in the face. Once awakened, one can no longer not know. Ordinary sleep becomes impossible — not the sleep of the body (which remains healthy), but that of the soul. The yaqẓa precedes the tawba (التَّوْبَة): one repents only of what one has seen. And the tawba itself becomes permanent — not an event, but a quality of the gaze. Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh (اَللَّٰه): “The sleep in the heart of the heedless is more deadly than the sleep of death.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: People are asleep; when they die, they awaken.
How would your heart be illumined while the images of the here-below are engraved upon its mirror?

entries.meditation: What has already awakened me in my life — and have I remained awake?

entries.racine_sens: ghafara — to cover, to forgive; istighfār — to ask for the covering of forgiveness
entries.definition_concise: To ask God to cover one's faults. A daily practice: “Astaghfiru Llāh” — I ask forgiveness of God — repeated like a heartbeat, which ceaselessly cleanses.
entries.sens_profond: The word ghafara means first to cover — like a mantle that covers a wound. To ask for forgiveness is not to efface (God alone effaces), it is to ask to be covered by His mercy. Istighfār, in the tradition, is one of the most powerful daily practices — the Prophet said: “I ask forgiveness of God and turn to Him more than seventy times a day.” He who was without sin! This shows that istighfār is not only a remedy for faults: it is an act of tenderness, of return, of nearness — a permanent movement of the heart toward God. The simplest formula — Astaghfiru Llāh — can be repeated like a wird (الوِرْد), like a breath. More deeply, the masters say: make istighfār even of your istighfār — for even your asking of forgiveness is a human work, which can grow proud.

voix_des_maitres.texte: By the One who holds my soul in His hand, I ask forgiveness of God more than seventy times a day.

entries.meditation: When did I last ask forgiveness — not for a precise act, but simply because I was not turned toward Him?

entries.racine_sens: daʿā — to call, to invoke
entries.definition_concise: The word addressed to God — not as an obligatory ritual, but as a call. The hadith says: “The duʿāʾ is the essence of worship.”
entries.sens_profond: The duʿāʾ is distinguished from the ṣalāt (الصَّلَاة) (which is ritualised): it is the free word addressed to God, in one's own language or in Arabic, at any moment. One invokes for oneself, for one's loved ones, for the living, for the dead, for enemies. The Quran multiplies the models of duʿāʾ — that of Adam after the fall, that of Ibrāhīm, that of Mūsā, that of Maryam… One learns there the grammar of the call: to begin by praising, to acknowledge one's poverty, to ask, to give thanks. For the Sufis, the duʿāʾ has a paradoxical dimension: one asks, but one knows that God knows better than we do what is good for us. The true answer to the duʿāʾ may be the opposite of what one was asking. Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh (اَللَّٰه): “Let His delay in answering, despite the insistence of your requests, not cause you to despair — He has guaranteed that He would answer in what He chooses for you, not in what you choose for yourself, and at the moment He wills, not at the moment you will.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: The duʿāʾ is the essence of worship.
He answers, but in what He chooses for you — not in what you choose for yourself.

entries.meditation: If I had only a single duʿāʾ to offer God at this instant, what would it be — truly?

entries.racine_sens: ʿakafa — to abide near, to persist; iʿtikāf — the retreat
entries.definition_concise: To withdraw into a mosque — particularly during the last ten days of Ramadan — to do nothing but pray, read, meditate. A short and intense form of the khalwa (الخَلْوَة).
entries.sens_profond: The iʿtikāf is a prophetic practice: the Prophet spent the last ten days of Ramadan in the mosque, leaving it only for his essential needs. It is a withdrawal from the social world, from affairs, from ordinary pleasures — a condensation of attention. For three, seven, ten days, life is reduced to: praying, reading the Quran, doing dhikr (الذِّكْر), sleeping a little, eating soberly, waiting. It is precisely the waiting that matters: one awaits the Night of Destiny (Laylat al-Qadr), on which the Quran was revealed, and on which God is nearer. The modern iʿtikāf remains accessible: three days in a mosque, or even in a dedicated room. The deep condition is not the enclosure, it is the stopping. One ceases the ordinary chains. One lets God come. Like the khalwa of the brotherhoods, the iʿtikāf is an acceleration of spiritual time: one traverses in a few days what would require months.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The Prophet, during the last ten days of the month, withdrew in iʿtikāf, and he put much of himself into it — he kept vigil and woke his family.

entries.meditation: When did I last truly stop — for nothing other than Him?

entries.racine_sens: ṣamata — to be silent
entries.definition_concise: Intentional silence — not the absence of speech, but the choice not to speak. A first discipline of the way: “Whoever is silent is saved.”
entries.sens_profond: The tongue is, for the Sufis, the most dangerous organ after the heart: through it escape the lie, slander, vain chatter, flattery, boasting. As long as one has not governed one's tongue, one governs nothing. Silence is therefore a preliminary discipline. Many masters imposed on their aspirants weeks, sometimes months, of absolute silence — not in order to silence speech, but in order to hear what speech was covering. Beneath the chatter, there is another voice: that of the heart. One hears it only in silence. But Sufi ṣamt does not stop at the silence of the lips: there is also inner silence (to no longer monologue, to no longer judge, to no longer comment), which is much more difficult. And higher still, silence before God — where one ceases even to ask, where one ceases even to give thanks, where one only stands, present. The masters say: “When you know God, your tongue grows weary.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: Whoever believes in God and the Last Day, let him say good, or let him be silent.
When you know God, your tongue grows weary.

entries.meditation: Today, what word should I have kept silent — and what silence should I have broken?

entries.racine_sens: ʿazala — to separate, to set apart
entries.definition_concise: To set oneself aside from the vain frequentations in order to preserve the heart. Not the flight of the world — but the rigorous selection of what deserves to occupy the soul.
entries.sens_profond: The ʿuzla is not total withdrawal (that would be rahbāniyya, monasticism, which Islam refuses). It is a discipline of sorting: to choose with whom one speaks, whom one listens to, where one goes. The Sufis distinguish outer ʿuzla (to reduce frequentations) and inner ʿuzla (to be alone even in the midst of the crowd, not to let the heart be carried away). This second form is higher: one can be alone before one's screen, and have the heart dispersed in a hundred affairs. Al-Junayd said: “I frequented sixty shaykhs (الشَّيْخ) — I drew from it only one thing: the ʿuzla.” This does not mean that he ceased to teach — he continued until the end. But his heart held itself apart. The ʿuzla has a prerequisite: one must be ready to bear one's own company. Many flee others and discover that they cannot bear themselves alone — which is precisely the sign that the ʿuzla is necessary.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I frequented sixty shaykhs — I drew from it only one thing: the ʿuzla.

entries.meditation: What are the frequentations that disperse me — and what prevents me from moving away from them?

entries.racine_sens: rabaṭa — to bind, to attach; rābiṭa — the bond
entries.definition_concise: The inner connection that the aspirant maintains with his shaykh (الشَّيْخ) — particularly developed in the Naqshbandiyya. To visualise the master, to feel his presence, to receive through his intermediary.
entries.sens_profond: The rābiṭa is one of the most subtle techniques of the way, and one of the most debated. In the Naqshbandiyya, it takes a codified form: the aspirant visualises the face of his master at the moment of meditating, and through this bond — like a channel — the grace of the Prophet, transmitted from master to master, reaches him. The opponents see in it a risk of idolatry; the defenders answer that the master is not worshipped, he is a mirror — one looks through him at what one cannot look at directly. As long as the mirror is true, the light reflected in it is that of the Prophet, and behind him, that of God. The rābiṭa requires an authentic master (a false master would reflect back his own darkness) and a constant vigilance (one does not stop at the reflection). More broadly, in all the ways, the simple fact of frequently thinking of one's shaykh, of asking oneself “what would he say of it?”, is already a form of rābiṭa.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Let your heart remain bound to the heart of your master — it is through him that the light passes.

entries.meditation: Is there, in my life, a face that brings me back to God merely by appearing to me?

entries.racine_sens: wajh — face, countenance; tawajjuh — to turn one's face toward
entries.definition_concise: To turn one's face — outer and inner — toward God. The tawajjuh (التَّوَجُّه) is also the gesture of the master who orients his aspirant by the sole force of his attention.
entries.sens_profond: The word wajh means face, countenance — but also, metaphorically, direction. Tawajjuh is to turn one's face: outwardly toward the qibla, inwardly toward God. Ibrāhīm says in the Quran: “I turn my face toward the One who created the heavens and the earth” (6:79). Every prayer begins with a tawajjuh — one orients oneself, one chooses. Beyond ritual prayer, there is a tawajjuh of every instant: with each act, with each word, toward what is my heart turned? Toward God, toward myself, toward the gaze of others? In the Naqshbandī tradition, the tawajjuh also denotes a particular grace: the master turns his attention toward the aspirant, and by this simple inner gaze, transmits a light. A famous account: a disciple asks his shaykh (الشَّيْخ) for a few words; the shaykh says nothing, simply looks at him — and the disciple departs transformed. It is the tawajjuh of the master.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I turn my face toward the One who created the heavens and the earth, as a pure monotheist — and I am not among the associators.

entries.meditation: Toward where is my face turned — visible and invisible — at this instant?

entries.racine_sens: nāba — to come back, to return; ināba — repeated return
entries.definition_concise: The permanent return of the heart toward God — deeper than the tawba (التَّوْبَة) (which is the initial act). The ināba is the habitus of return: each time one moves away, one returns.
entries.sens_profond: The tradition distinguishes several degrees of the return: the tawba (التَّوْبَة) is the event (one turns back, one leaves the fault), the nadāma is the regret, the ināba is the lasting quality of the heart that no longer knows how to return anywhere else. The Quran multiplies the terms: munīb — the one who returns ceaselessly — is one of the qualifiers of the friends of God. And orient yourselves toward your Lord (anībū ilā Rabbikum), and submit yourselves to Him (39:54). The ināba is the inverse of inertia: at each distraction, at each estrangement, at each breath, one turns back. It is less an act than a heartbeat. The heart of the munīb beats like the heart of a lover who no longer knows how to do anything but return. Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh (اَللَّٰه): “Return to Him, were it after a long absence; He welcomes with the same gentleness the first day and the thousandth.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: Orient yourselves toward your Lord, submit yourselves to Him before the chastisement comes upon you.

entries.meditation: When I move away — how long do I take to return?

entries.racine: ل و ن / م ك ن
entries.racine_sens: lawn — colour; talwīn — change of colour. makana — to be stable; tamkīn — establishment.
entries.definition_concise: The talwīn is the state of the aspirant still changing — one day joy, the next a tightening, one day present, the next absent. The tamkīn is the state of the master who has arrived: he no longer changes, because he is in God.
entries.sens_profond: This technical pair describes two stages of spiritual maturation. As long as one wayfares, one is coloured by the states: joy fills us then leaves us, the tightening comes then goes. One depends on the aḥwāl (الأَحْوَال) — the gifts of God that pass. This is the talwīn. Then comes (sometimes, for some) the tamkīn — the establishment. It is not that the states cease — they continue — but one is no longer carried away. One remains oneself in joy as in the tightening. One is anchored. Al-Qushayrī writes: “The talwīn is the attribute of the traveller; the tamkīn is the attribute of the one who has arrived.” But beware: some masters (Ibn ʿArabī notably) reverse the perspective — the tamkīn within the talwīn is higher than pure tamkīn, for then one is stable while receiving all the states. It is the stability of the servant who changes with the divine breaths without ever losing his centre.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The talwīn is the attribute of the traveller; the tamkīn is the attribute of the one who has arrived.

entries.meditation: Am I coloured by my states — or do I hold within me something that does not change?

entries.racine: ج م ع / ف ر ق
entries.racine_sens: jamaʿa — to unite, to gather; faraqa — to separate, to distinguish
entries.definition_concise: The jamʿ is the vision in which one sees only God — all is He, nothing else. The farq is the distinct vision — God is God, creation is creation. Both are necessary.
entries.sens_profond: It is one of the finest pairs of speculative Sufism. In the jamʿ, the aspirant is seized by unity: he sees nothing more than God behind each thing, and finally he forgets the things themselves. It is the state of fanāʾ (الفَنَاء) — in which duality goes out. In the farq, on the contrary, he returns to the distinct vision: God remains God, the creature remains creature, and each has its place. Without the farq, the way becomes confusion (“I am God,” with no nuance, no servant, no Law). Without the jamʿ, it remains at the surface (“there is God, and there is us,” with no intimacy). The higher degree is the jamʿ al-jamʿ (جَمْعُ الجَمْع) — the union of the union — in which one sees at once God and creation, neither separated nor confused. It is the vision of the perfect one: to see Him in all, and all in Him, without forgetting anything.

voix_des_maitres.texte: True tawḥīd (تَوْحِيد) is that you be Him through Him — and that you be a servant to Him through yourself.

entries.meditation: Do I see God and the creature as two — or as a single one? And do the two visions truly exclude each other?

entries.racine_sens: faqr — poverty; iftiqār — felt need, inner destitution
entries.definition_concise: To feel, at each breath, that one has nothing — neither being, nor breath, nor thought. The iftiqār is the truth of the creature: need, need, need.
entries.sens_profond: The faqr (الفَقْر) is the outer station (one possesses little); the iftiqār is its inner dimension (one feels that one is nothing). One can be materially poor and secretly nourish the vanity of the poor; one can be rich and inhabited by the iftiqār — to feel, at each breath, that one depends totally, that one maintains nothing oneself. The Quran says: “O humankind, it is you who are the poor in need of God, and God is the Rich, the Worthy of praise” (35:15). The iftiqār is the acute awareness of this structure: to be created is to be in lack of one's Creator, at every instant. The next breath is not guaranteed. Nor is the next thought. All is held. The more one is aware of this, the more the heart is gentle, open, supplicating. The iftiqār is also what extinguishes the kibr (الكِبْر): one cannot believe oneself great when one feels that one does not stand upright by oneself.

voix_des_maitres.texte: O humankind, it is you who are the poor in need of God, and God is the Rich, the Worthy of praise.

entries.meditation: Do I feel, in this very breath, that I do not hold it — that I receive it?

entries.racine_sens: ṣafā — to be pure, limpid; ṣafāʾ — transparent purity
entries.definition_concise: The state of the polished heart, in which God is reflected without blemish. One of the most accepted etymologies of the word ṣūfī (الصُّوفِي). Limpidity — not absence, but transparency.
entries.sens_profond: The ṣafāʾ (صَفَاء) is not a state but a lasting quality of the heart — its transparency. For the Sufis, the heart is a mirror: what it reflects depends on its cleanliness. A tarnished mirror reflects blurred, deformed, partial things. A polished mirror reflects without deforming. The work of the way is entirely a work of polishing (tajliyat al-qalb (القَلْب)). When the heart is limpid, it reflects: God first, then all that is, seen in God. The ṣafāʾ allows the just vision — not only of God, but also of oneself (without illusion), of others (without projection), of the world (without greed). It is doubtless this limpidity that the gentlest etymology of the word ṣūfī (الصُّوفِيّ) denotes — the one whose heart is limpid. Higher than limpidity, some masters speak of the ṣafwat aṣ-ṣafwa — the limpidity of limpidity — in which even the awareness of being limpid has disappeared: pure transparency without a witness.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The ṣūfī is the one whose heart has become limpid for God.

entries.meditation: My heart, at this instant — what does it reflect? If God looked into it, would He see Himself without distortion?

entries.racine_sens: jabara — to compel, to impose; jabarūt — absolute sovereignty
entries.definition_concise: The highest of the created worlds — that of the divine Attributes in themselves, before all distinct manifestation. Above the Malakūt, below the Lāhūt.
entries.sens_profond: Classical Sufi cosmology distinguishes several worlds (ʿawālim (عَوَالِم)) that are not spatial but ontological — degrees of reality. The lowest is the nāsūt (the human, the sensory). Above, the mulk (the structured visible). Higher still, the malakūt (the world of the angels, of the acting Names). Higher, the jabarūt — in which the divine Attributes stand in their purity, without yet being distributed to beings. At the summit, some masters place the lāhūt — the pure divinity, the Essence before the Attributes. The jabarūt is the world of the aʿyān thābita — the immutable essences, the eternal models of each thing in the divine knowledge. When a saint accedes to the jabarūt, he sees things no longer as they are below, but as they are in His thought. It is the horizon of prophethood.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The jabarūt is the world in which things are seen as they are in the divine knowledge, before their descent.

entries.meditation: This thing I hold in my hand, this face I look at — what is their reality up there, before they descended here?

entries.racine_sens: ilāh — divinity; lāhūt — divinity as such
entries.definition_concise: The absolute summit: God in Himself, the Essence before all relation, before all distinct Attribute. No created being accedes to it as such — it is the pure Mystery.
entries.sens_profond: The lāhūt denotes divinity considered in itself, with no relation whatever to the creatures. It is the pure Essence — al-Dhāt (الذَّات) — before the Names, the Attributes, the Acts unfold. No contemplation, no ecstasy, no vision reaches the lāhūt as such — for it is by definition beyond all manifestation. It is the ultimate horizon: not a place where one goes, but a point that orients. Ibn ʿArabī speaks of the ghayb (الغَيْب) al-ghuyūb — the mystery of mysteries — which is this lāhūt. The Sufis, speaking of the lāhūt, do not describe: they fall silent, or speak by negations (“neither this, nor that”). The absolute tanzīh (التَّنْزِيه) is here. Why, then, speak of it? Because it is the constant reminder: all that you know of God is not God. Every image, every Name, every ecstasy — are gifts, but God remains, behind, the Unfathomable.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The lāhūt is the mystery of mysteries — the one of which one speaks only by falling silent.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a place where I know that God is more than all I can say of Him?

entries.fr: The human world, the sensory world
entries.racine_sens: nās — human beings; nāsūt — humanity
entries.definition_concise: The sensory world — the one in which we are. The lowest in the hierarchy, and yet the one in which the way is lived. The nāsūt is not scorned: it is the place of the trial.
entries.sens_profond: The nāsūt is the world of flesh, of the senses, of time, of human affairs. In certain rigid readings, it is what must be left. But the majority Sufi way teaches something else: the nāsūt is not to be fled, it is to be inhabited otherwise. It is here, in this world, in this body, in this ordinary life, that the whole wayfaring is played out. To eat, to sleep, to work, to marry, to raise children — so many occasions of presence or of forgetting. The true master is the one who, having traversed the higher worlds, comes back down into the nāsūt without losing anything — and teaches there. The prophetic trajectory is exactly this: Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) ascends in the miʿrāj (the nocturnal ascension), traverses the seven heavens, reaches qāba qawsayn (the distance of two bows' length or nearer still to God), and then returns — to transmit. Without the descent, the experience is barren.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Be in the world without being of the world.

entries.meditation: Am I inhabiting this world — or waiting to leave it?

entries.racine_sens: rūḥ — spirit, breath; arwāḥ — spirits (plural)
entries.definition_concise: The world in which the spirits pre-existed before the descent into bodies. It is there that the day of alast took place — the primordial pact in which God asks: “Am I not your Lord?”
entries.sens_profond: According to Sufi cosmology, the spirits are created before the bodies. They exist in the ʿālam al-arwāḥ, a luminous, immaterial world, where they contemplate God directly. It is in this world that the scene of the mīthāq — the primordial pact — takes place: God gathers all the spirits and asks: “Am I not your Lord?” (alastu bi-rabbikum). All answer: “Yes, we bear witness” (balā shahidnā, Quran 7:172). Then the spirits descend into the bodies. They forget. All the way is a remembering of this moment. When the aspirant does dhikr (الذِّكْر) — remembrance — what he tries to revive is this balā of the day of alast, this primordial yes that precedes him. Rūmī: “Do not ask why the nightingale seeks the rose — it heard it sing over there.”

voix_des_maitres.texte: Am I not your Lord? — Yes, we bear witness.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a memory that has no date — as if I had already said yes before being able to speak?

entries.fr: The world of the divine Command
entries.racine_sens: amr — command, order
entries.definition_concise: The world of the Command — in which God creates by His word: “Be,” and the thing is. Opposed to the ʿālam al-khalq, the world of progressive shaping.
entries.sens_profond: The Quran establishes this distinction: “His Command, when He wills a thing, is only that He say: Be! — and it is” (36:82). The Sufis draw from it a cosmology: there are two modes of creation — by khalq (shaping, fashioning, in time, through secondary causes) and by amr (immediate command, without intermediary, outside time). The world of the amr includes the spirits, the angels, the divine kun, the acting Word. It is also the world of the miracle — in which the habitual order of causes is suspended by a direct command. When a saint accedes to the world of the amr, he can, by divine permission, speak to a thing as God speaks to it: “Be thus.” But few accede to it, and those who do rarely use it — for power is never the goal. The knowledge of the ʿālam al-amr is above all a key: to understand that beneath the world of causes (in which we ordinarily live), there is a world of direct command, and that the second is more real than the first.

voix_des_maitres.texte: His Command, when He wills a thing, is only that He say: Be! — and it is.

entries.meditation: This life I hold — is it the effect of a chain of causes, or the consequence of a “Be”?

entries.racine_sens: wathiqa — to be firm, bound; mīthāq — a solemn pact
entries.definition_concise: The pact that God concluded with all the souls before the creation of the bodies. He asks them: “Am I not your Lord?” All answer: “Yes.”
entries.sens_profond: The mīthāq is the central cosmogonic scene of Sufism. Quran 7:172: “And when your Lord drew from the children of Adam, from their loins, their descendants, and made them bear witness against themselves: Am I not your Lord? They said: Yes, we bear witness.” All human beings, in their pre-existence, said yes. This yes precedes birth, education, culture, the professed religion. It is the original ground of every soul. Every spiritual quest, in the Sufi reading, is a remembering of this moment. When someone, reading a verse, hearing a music, meeting a face, feels himself traversed by something he knows without knowing it — it is the mīthāq rising up. Conversion is never anything but a return. The fitra (الفِطْرَة) (the original nature) is precisely this deposit of the mīthāq in every being. Nothing to learn — everything to remember.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Am I not your Lord? — Yes, we bear witness.

entries.meditation: What is, within me, this yes that I have not learned — and that I cannot take back?

entries.racine_sens: alastu — Am I not?
entries.definition_concise: The day of the mīthāq (المِيثَاق) — the one on which God asked the souls: alastu bi-rabbikum? For the Sufi poets, particularly the Persians, it is the day of the first meeting, and all of life a nostalgia.
entries.sens_profond: If the mīthāq is the doctrinal concept, yawm alast is its poetic name. It is the abridged expression of the verse: alastu bi-rabbikum (“Am I not your Lord?”). For Rūmī, Ḥāfeẓ, ʿAṭṭār, Sanāʾī, all mystical love is but a resumption of that instant. The soul saw the Face before descending, it forgot it beneath the veil of the body, and all its life it weeps this separation — sometimes without knowing what it weeps. Ḥāfeẓ: “From the day of alast to the day of Judgement, my contract with the Beloved has not changed by a single letter.” The saint, the poet, the true lover, the poor one (faqīr (فَقِير)) — these are those who have not forgotten. All the way consists in awakening the memory. The samāʿ (السَّمَاع), particularly, is a technique for this: to hear a melody that resembles the melody of alast, and suddenly to remember.

voix_des_maitres.texte: From the day of alast to the day of Judgement, my contract with the Beloved has not changed by a single letter.
Listen to this reed, how it laments — it is the nostalgia of alast.

entries.meditation: Is there within me a nostalgia for what I have never consciously known?

entries.racine: ح ق ق / ح م د
entries.racine_sens: ḥaqīqa — reality, essential truth; Muḥammad — the much-praised
entries.definition_concise: The spiritual reality of Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) — not the historical man alone, but the first creation of God, the original mirror through which the whole world was created.
entries.sens_profond: For Ibn ʿArabī and all the metaphysical tradition that follows him, Muḥammad is not only the historical Prophet of the 7th century — he is also a cosmic reality, created before the world. A famous hadith: “The first thing God created was my light.” This first light — the nūr muḥammadī (النُّورُ المُحَمَّدِيّ) — is the prototype, the intermediate mirror between God and creation. Every being that comes into existence comes through this reality. Each prophet (Adam, Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus…) is a part of this Muhammadan reality; Muḥammad alone gathers it whole. This is why he is called khātam al-anbiyāʾ — the seal of the prophets. For the Sufis, the ḥaqīqa muḥammadiyya is also the archetypal insān kāmil (إِنْسَانٌ كَامِل): the accomplished human, the model of all spiritual wayfaring. When an aspirant practises the rābiṭa (الرَّابِطَة) with his shaykh (الشَّيْخ), it is, beyond the shaykh, to this ḥaqīqa that he binds himself.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The first thing God created was my light.
He is the first manifestation and the last understanding.

entries.meditation: When I say the name Muḥammad, is it a man of the past, or a light that precedes me?

entries.racine: ن و ر / ح م د
entries.racine_sens: nūr — light; muḥammadī — relating to Muḥammad
entries.definition_concise: The first created light — a pure divine manifestation, through which all the rest was created. A subtle substance that the prophets, then the saints, inherit.
entries.sens_profond: The nūr muḥammadī is the luminous aspect of the ḥaqīqa muḥammadiyya (الحَقِيقَة المُحَمَّدِيَّة). Before the world was, God created this light; then, from this light, He created all things. It is not a physical light but a subtle reality — pure divine manifestation, the first veil. This light was transmitted prophet after prophet, from Adam to Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد), like an inheritance that is not diminished by being shared. At the death of the Prophet, it is distributed among the saints (awliyāʾ) who carry it thereafter — each according to his capacity, each according to his function. Every authentic master carries a share of it. This is what explains, for the Sufis, the experience of baraka (البَرَكَة) in the presence of a saint: one is touched by a light that, through its silsila (السِّلْسِلَة), reaches back to the Prophet, and beyond to the first creation. The pilgrimage to the tombs of the saints is not the worship of the saint, but a visit to a place where this light has settled.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The first thing God created was my light.

entries.meditation: Are there, in my life, moments when I have touched a light that did not come from the here-below?

entries.racine_sens: ʿaṣama — to protect, to preserve
entries.definition_concise: The preservation that God grants to His prophets against sin and error — and, to a lesser degree, to His saints against the major deviations.
entries.sens_profond: The ʿiṣma denotes, in the strict sense, the infallibility of the prophets: they are preserved from grave sin, from lying, from doctrinal error. It is not that they are incapable of sinning (that would contradict their freedom), but that God protects them at the instant when the fault might occur. Theology distinguishes absolute ʿiṣma (total, after the prophetic mission) from relative ʿiṣma (which can admit a few minor errors, quickly corrected). For the Sufis, an attenuated form of ʿiṣma is granted to the saints: not infallibility, but a protection against the major deviations. The authentic saint is kept — by God, by his silsila (السِّلْسِلَة), by his own vigilance — from what could make him fall. This protection is never a due: it can withdraw if the saint forgets his rank. True ʿiṣma, it is said, is invisible: those who claim it have already lost it.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The prophets are protected from sin and error — not because they cannot, but because God prevents them.

entries.meditation: Is there a place within me that knows that I am held — that I will not fall lower than what God permits?

entries.racine_sens: maḥā — to efface, to obliterate
entries.definition_concise: The effacement of the human attributes to make room for the divine attributes. Less radical than fanāʾ (الفَنَاء): not the disappearance of the self, but the striking-out of what in it prevents God.
entries.sens_profond: The maḥw is the inverse operation of ithbāt (establishment, affirmation). One effaces from the heart what should not be there: the flaws, the attachments, the dead habits, the old identities. One establishes in their place what should be there: the divine qualities reflected in the servant — patience, generosity, truthfulness, mercy. The pair maḥw / ithbāt is also known under other names: takhliya / taḥliya (emptying / adorning), tafrīq / jamʿ. It is the constant movement of the way: one removes, one puts back. One deconstructs the veils, one installs the presences. The maḥw demands a constant vigilance — without it, what one has effaced returns. And a single forgetting can undo years of effacement. Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh (اَللَّٰه): “How would your heart be illumined while the images of the here-below are engraved in it as in a mirror?” — the cure is to efface the images, one after another.

voix_des_maitres.texte: How would your heart be illumined while the images of the here-below are engraved in it as in a mirror?

entries.meditation: What, within me, asks to be effaced — not because it is bad, but because it takes the place of Him?

entries.racine_sens: thabata — to be firm, established; ithbāt — establishment
entries.definition_concise: The counter-movement of the maḥw (المَحْو): to establish in the heart, in the place of what one has effaced, the divine qualities that must inhabit it. Without ithbāt, the maḥw leaves the heart empty.
entries.sens_profond: To efface is not enough. If one establishes nothing in its place, the emptiness attracts other images, sometimes worse. This is why the tradition always couples maḥw and ithbāt: “Empty the house of its idols — then make the King enter it.” The ithbāt establishes in the heart the reflected divine qualities: ṣabr (الصَّبْر) (patience) in place of impatience; ikhlāṣ (إِخْلَاص) in place of duplicity; maḥabba (المَحَبَّة) in place of fear; shukr (الشُّكْر) in place of complaint. But the ithbāt goes further than mere moral discipline: it establishes the presence of God Himself in the heart, through the repeated dhikr (الذِّكْر), through the murāqaba (المُرَاقَبَة), through constant ḥuḍūr (الحُضُور). In the end, the servant no longer establishes himself in qualities, he establishes himself in Him — it is the baqāʾ (البَقَاء), the subsistence through God.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Empty the house of its idols — then make the King enter it.

entries.meditation: If I let God fill the space He has made me — what would take the place of what I want to keep?

entries.racine_sens: jamaʿa — to unite; jamʿ al-jamʿ — the union of the union
entries.definition_concise: The summit of the vision: to see God and creation, neither separated, nor confused. Beyond the fanāʾ (الفَنَاء) (in which the creature effaces itself) and the farq (الفَرْق) (in which it distinguishes itself), it is the vision that contains both.
entries.sens_profond: The jamʿ al-jamʿ is, for the speculative Sufis, the highest vision. In pure jamʿ, one sees only God — creation effaces itself, the aspirant disappears, there remains only Him. It is the state of fanāʾ. In pure farq, one sees the creature distinct from God — God is God, the creature is creature. It is the juridical, normal vision, which saves from confusion. The jamʿ al-jamʿ contains both: one sees God in creation, and creation in God, without separating, without confusing. It is the vision of the perfect one, of the insān kāmil (إِنْسَانٌ كَامِل). Its paradoxical formula: “He is He, and He is not He; He is the creature, and He is not the creature.” Ibn ʿArabī, in the Fuṣūṣ al-Ḥikam (فُصُوصُ الحِكَم), makes of it the ultimate horizon. Concretely: one accomplishes the Law perfectly (farq), while knowing that God acts through each gesture (jamʿ), and that this double truth does not contradict itself — it resolves itself in the heart of the accomplished saint.

voix_des_maitres.texte: He is He, and He is not He — the one who understands this knows the jamʿ al-jamʿ.

entries.meditation: Am I able to see, in a single gaze, the unity of all and the particular dignity of each thing?

entries.racine_sens: fāḍa — to overflow, to pour forth; fayḍ — the effusion that issues from itself
entries.definition_concise: The effusion by which God pours Himself into creation — not by necessity, but by an overabundance of love. A central concept of Sufi metaphysics, particularly in Ibn ʿArabī.
entries.sens_profond: The fayḍ is a metaphor as old as philosophy: God, in His plenitude, overflows. The world is not an arbitrary act but an effusion — as the water of a spring cannot help issuing forth. Why? Because of the qudsī hadith: “I was a hidden treasure, I loved to be known — so I created the beings that they might know Me.” God's love for His own manifestation is the cause of the fayḍ. Ibn ʿArabī distinguishes several levels: the fayḍ al-aqdas (the most holy effusion) — God in His self-manifestation to Himself; the fayḍ al-muqaddas (the holy effusion) — God manifesting Himself through the immutable essences; and finally the concrete creation, which flows from it. Each breath of each creature is, at every instant, sustained by a fayḍ — without it, existence would collapse. The grace that touches the saint, the inspiration of the poet, the very breath of the ordinary man — all is fayḍ.

voix_des_maitres.texte: I was a hidden treasure, I loved to be known — so I created the beings that they might know Me.

entries.meditation: If each breath is a fayḍ — what prevents me from receiving it as such?

entries.racine_sens: badaʾa — to begin, to start
entries.definition_concise: The first moment of the way — the one in which one is no longer in ghafla (الغَفْلَة), but in which one has not yet traversed anything. The bidāya is precious because everything in it is still possible.
entries.sens_profond: The Sufis distinguish bidāya (beginning) and nihāya (end) — not as two moments in time, but as two qualities of the way. The beginner (mubtadiʾ) needs efforts, practices, readings, a master near, encouragements. Everything is new for him: each verse, each meditation, each experience can overturn him. It is also the most fragile period — in which one can abandon everything for a single difficulty. The masters recommend to the beginner: regularity before intensity, suḥba (the company of the brothers), patience with oneself. The bidāya has a beauty of its own: the freshness of the first time. Some masters even say that every great wayfarer keeps, beneath all his years, a beginner's heart — that is, the capacity to marvel, to receive, to weep before a verse. Without this heart, one becomes a dry scholar.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Keep the heart of the beginner — it is the one that weeps, and that sees.

entries.meditation: Am I still able to weep before a verse — or have I already heard?

entries.racine_sens: nahā — to arrive at the end
entries.definition_concise: The term of the way — not a place where one stops, but a state in which one has become what one was seeking. The nihāya rejoins the bidāya: at the end, one is naked as at the beginning.
entries.sens_profond: The nihāya is not an end in the sense that the way would stop: as long as one breathes, one wayfares. But it is the state of the muntahī — the one who has arrived. No more need for artificial efforts: the practice has become his nature. No more need to seek: what he was seeking has found him. He no longer has to remember God — he lives in Him. And yet, the central paradox of mysticism: at the end, one is brought back to the beginning. One rediscovers the freshness of the child, the simple yes of the fitra (الفِطْرَة). The masters say: “The end of the wayfarers is the beginning of those who arrive.” And higher: “The return is more complete than the going.” The muntahī returns among human beings — not by a falling-off, but to transmit. He teaches. He welcomes. He no longer distinguishes himself outwardly from his brothers — it is inwardly that he is wholly other. Junayd says: “The colour of the water is the colour of its vessel.” The muntahī takes the form of every vessel without losing anything of his water.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The colour of the water is the colour of its vessel.

entries.meditation: Is there a part within me that has already arrived — that knows, without seeking?

entries.racine_sens: ibtadaʾa — to begin; mubtadiʾ — the one who begins
entries.definition_concise: The aspirant to the way who is at the very beginning — still full of zeal, of questions, of naïve errors. The mubtadiʾ needs a master near, clear rules, and the suḥba.
entries.sens_profond: The mubtadiʾ traverses a thrilling and dangerous period. Thrilling: everything is new, each experience can be overwhelming, the heart catches fire easily. Dangerous: one can take a strong emotion for a station, a reading for a realisation, a master for a god. Many fall at the beginning — not into fault, but into excess (rigorism, scruple, exaltation), or into disappointment (when the initial enthusiasm dies down). The masters insist: the mubtadiʾ must attach himself to a living guide, follow the basic practices with regularity (prayer, dhikr (الذِّكْر), reading, suḥba), and not rush onto the works of the great openers (Ibn ʿArabī, Niffarī, al-Ḥallāj) before having the foundations. The golden rule: regularity before intensity. It is better to do ten minutes of dhikr (الذِّكْر) every day for ten years than five hours one day and nothing the following week.

voix_des_maitres.texte: For the beginner, two enemies: haste and discouragement.

entries.meditation: Am I a mubtadiʾ who believes himself advanced — or an advanced one who has forgotten to be a mubtadiʾ?

entries.fr: The one who has arrived, the accomplished one
entries.racine_sens: intahā — to arrive at the end; muntahī — the one who has arrived
entries.definition_concise: The one who has arrived at the term of the way — not as a finished acquisition, but as a lasting quality of the being. The muntahī no longer signals himself by feats: he has become simple.
entries.sens_profond: The muntahī has traversed the efforts of the mubtadiʾ and the experiences of the wāṣil (the one who is arriving). Now, he is. His first characteristic is simplicity: he no longer distinguishes himself outwardly, he no longer wears signs, he lives like a man among men. But those who have the eye recognise in him a particular presence — the sakīna, the peace that descends. He has knowledge without needing to show it; the powers (if they are given to him) without needing to exercise them; spiritual authority without imposing. The muntahī, paradoxically, has often returned to ordinary practice — he performs the prayers like everyone, he fasts like everyone, he lives. But behind the simplicity of his gestures, there is a plenitude that others perceive only by drawing near. The masters further distinguish the muntahī al-muntahīn — the one who has arrived among those who have arrived — who has become transparent to such a point that he disappears into God without ceasing to be present to human beings.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The muntahī is no longer seen — that is what distinguishes him.

entries.meditation: Is there, in my life, a being whom I have recognised as arrived — not by his words, but by his mere presence?

entries.racine_sens: ʿadala — to be just; iʿtidāl — balance, temperance
entries.definition_concise: To hold the middle between the extremes. The classical Sufi way is not extreme: it balances work and rest, asceticism and joy, withdrawal and presence to human beings.
entries.sens_profond: The iʿtidāl renders into Arabic the idea of the middle way. The Quran names the Muslim community ummatan wasaṭan — the community of the middle (2:143). For the Sufis, this holds as much for the outer practice as for the inner conduct: neither the joy-killing asceticism, nor the laxity that dilutes everything. Neither the dryness of the jurist, nor the effervescence of badly controlled jadhb (الجَذْب). Neither total isolation, nor dispersion in the world. The true master teaches one to hold the middle, and each middle is unique: the just measure of one aspirant is not that of another. It is precisely the role of the shaykh (الشَّيْخ) — to sense, for each one, where the line of balance passes. The way demands much, but not everything — it requires patience with oneself as much as with others. The Prophet, the model of models, is the icon of the iʿtidāl: he fasted and ate, kept vigil and slept, withdrew and returned to his own. Classical Sufi sainthood is not superhuman — it is human, but inhabited.

voix_des_maitres.texte: And thus have We made of you a community of the middle — that you may be witnesses among human beings.

entries.meditation: Where, at this moment, am I in the extreme — and where would the true middle be, here?

entries.racine_sens: ramaḍ — the burning heat of the ground
entries.definition_concise: The ninth month of the calendar — that of the obligatory fast, and the one in which the Quran descended. For the Sufis, it is the great annual training ground.
entries.sens_profond: The month of Ramadan condenses all the major practices: fasting from sunrise to sunset (water, food, conjugal intimacy excluded), supererogatory nocturnal prayers (tarāwīḥ), intensified readings of the Quran, increased charity, and — for the Sufis — often a retreat (iʿtikāf (الاِعْتِكَاف)) for the last ten days. The Night of Destiny (Laylat al-Qadr), situated in the last ten odd-numbered days, is “better than a thousand months” (Quran 97:3) — it is the night on which the Quran was revealed, and on which every act is multiplied. For the Sufi, Ramadan is not only privation: it is a change of mode. The body slows down, the soul grows light, the heart opens. The veils fall more easily, the inspirations flow in. The brotherhoods intensify their dhikr (الذِّكْر), their samāʿ (السَّمَاع), their vigils. The month is also a microcosm of the whole way: one begins with zeal, one traverses a trough in the middle, one rises at the end — as in every wayfaring. This is why it is repeated every year: so that no one believes himself to have arrived.

voix_des_maitres.texte: The month of Ramadan, during which the Quran was sent down, a guidance for human beings, clear signs of guidance and of discernment.

entries.meditation: If Ramadan were to begin tomorrow, what would I want to have laid down before it ends?

entries.racine_sens: walada — to be born; mawlid — birth
entries.definition_concise: The celebration of the birth of the Prophet Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) — the 12th of the month of Rabīʿ al-awwal. A central feast for most Sufis, an occasion for chant, meditation, transmission.
entries.sens_profond: The celebration of the mawlid is, for the great majority of Sufis (and beyond, for a great part of the Muslim world), a high moment of the year: one reads the sīra (the biography of the Prophet), one chants the odes in his praise (the Burda of al-Būṣīrī above all), one meditates on his light (nūr muḥammadī), one shares meals, one visits the brotherhoods. For the Sufis, the love of the Prophet is not optional — it is the door of the love of God. “You will love God only if you love me first,” say the masters in commenting on the verse: “Say: if you love God, follow me, God will love you” (3:31). The mawlid is therefore a visible act of love — not an anniversary in the Western sense, but an actualisation of the prophetic presence. Some brotherhoods, like the Naqshbandī or the Tijānī, make of the dhikr (الذِّكْر) upon the Prophet (ṣalāt ʿalā-n-nabī) a central axis. The ṣalāt al-fātiḥ of the Tijānī, for example, is a blessing upon the Prophet considered equivalent to the whole of creation.

voix_des_maitres.texte: God and His angels pray upon the Prophet. O you who believe, pray upon him and greet him abundantly.

entries.meditation: What is the presence of the Prophet, for my heart — a name of the past, or a living light?

entries.racine_sens: naqsh — the motif, the engraving; the master Bahāʾ ad-Dīn was surnamed an-Naqshband, “the one who engraves the motif (upon the heart)”
entries.definition_concise: A Sunni brotherhood founded in Bukhara in the 14th century by Bahāʾ ad-Dīn Muḥammad (مُحَمَّد) al-Bukhārī, known as Naqshband. The central particularity: the silent dhikr (الذِّكْر) (dhikr khafī (خَفِيّ)), without music, without dance, integrated into the very heart of ordinary life.
entries.sens_profond: Bahāʾ ad-Dīn (1318-1389) received the teaching of the way through Khwāja Muḥammad Bābā as-Samāsī, but his spiritual lineage reaches back, by an alternative filiation, to Abū Bakr aṣ-Ṣiddīq (الصِّدِّيق) — a unique fact among the great brotherhoods, which almost all pass through ʿAlī (اَلْعَلِيّ). This particularity gives the way its colouring: sober, interiorised, transmitting from heart to heart without demonstration. The eleven principles formulated by Bahāʾ ad-Dīn (hūsh dar dam — awareness in the breath; naẓar bar qadam — the gaze upon the step; safar dar waṭan — journey within one's own homeland…) condense a discipline of daily life. The rābiṭa (الرَّابِطَة) — the inner bond to the master — takes a particular centrality in it. The way spread across Central Asia, the Caucasus, Ottoman Turkey, Mughal India, then the whole world in the 20th century.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Our way is the way of suḥba — companionship. And suḥba is more effective than a thousand books.
Hūsh dar dam — awareness in the breath. Each respiration inhabited by God.

entries.meditation: If the teaching passed from heart to heart, without words — which master, in my life, would have already touched me?

entries.racine_sens: qadar — the measure, the decree; the founder ʿAbd al-Qādir = “Servant of the All-Powerful”
entries.definition_concise: One of the oldest and most widespread of the Sufi brotherhoods, founded in Baghdad by ʿAbd al-Qādir (اَلْقَادِر) al-Jīlānī (1077-1166). Characterised by a vocal dhikr (الذِّكْر), a mysticism of love and a great accessibility.
entries.sens_profond: ʿAbd al-Qādir al-Jīlānī, born in Iran (Jīlān), formed in Baghdad, became the most beloved saint of the Sunni world — called al-Ghawth al-Aʿẓam (الغَوْثُ الأَعْظَم) (the Supreme Succour) and Sulṭān al-Awliyāʾ (the Sultan of the saints). His way spread at an extraordinary speed after his death, carried by his sons then by his disciples. Today, one finds Qādirī in almost all Muslim countries — from Morocco to Indonesia, from Senegal to the Caucasus. The transmission of the lineage passes through ʿAlī (اَلْعَلِيّ) (like almost all the brotherhoods). Characteristics: vocal dhikr (often Lā ilāha illā Llāh (لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا اللَّٰه) collectively), great generosity toward the poor, a mysticism of love far more than of asceticism. The daily wird (الوِرْد) is accessible. The karāmāt (miracles) attributed to ʿAbd al-Qādir are countless, and the Qādiriyya is thus the most popular way.

voix_des_maitres.texte: When you find the True, you will be the servant of human beings. Before that, you are still seeking.

entries.meditation: A way open to all, for the one who dares to ask — do I ask, or do I still seek to merit beforehand?

entries.racine_sens: Shādhila — the name of the Moroccan village where the founder was formed; al-Shādhilī = “the Shadhilian”
entries.definition_concise: A Sunni brotherhood founded in the 13th century by Abū al-Ḥasan al-Shādhilī (1196-1258) in the Maghreb then in Egypt. The central principle: to live the way without showing it — no ostentatious asceticism, no particular garment, sainthood in ordinary clothes.
entries.sens_profond: Abū al-Ḥasan al-Shādhilī, born in Morocco, formed by ʿAbd as-Salām (اَلسَّلَام) ibn Mashīsh (the hidden master of the Rif), settled in Alexandria. His way became one of the most influential in Sunni Sufism. The particularity: al-Shādhilī does not ask his disciples to leave the world. His disciples are merchants, jurists, peasants — the way is lived in the suḥba of the master and in the interiorisation of the ordinary acts. The Ḥizb al-Baḥr (Litany of the Sea), composed by al-Shādhilī, has become one of the great prayers of the Muslim world. His disciple Ibn ʿAṭāʾ Allāh (اَللَّٰه) al-Iskandarī fixed the doctrine in the Ḥikam (Aphorisms) — a summit of Sufi literature. The Shādhiliyya has engendered numerous major branches: Darqāwiyya (Morocco, 18th c.), ʿAlawiyya (20th c.), Burhāniyya, Buṭchichiyya. A central tradition in contemporary Sufism.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Our way is not a way of drawing near through works, but a way of effacement and of poverty before God.
Your best parcel of time is the one in which you acknowledge your poverty and take refuge in your humility.

entries.meditation: Can one be fully of this way without anyone around oneself knowing it?

entries.racine_sens: Tijān — the name of the founder, himself descended from an ancient tribe of Aïn Madhi (southern Algeria)
entries.definition_concise: A Sunni brotherhood founded at the end of the 18th century by Aḥmad (أَحْمَد) at-Tijānī (1735-1815) at ʿAïn Madhi (Algeria) then Fez (Morocco). Very influential in West Africa. Characteristic: the absolute centrality of the prayer upon the Prophet, in particular the ṣalāt al-fātiḥ.
entries.sens_profond: Aḥmad at-Tijānī received his investiture, according to the Tijānī tradition, directly from the Prophet in the waking state (fī l-yaqẓa lā fī l-manām) — an affirmation that distinguishes this brotherhood. His daily wird (الوِرْد) (lāzima) is demanding: precise prayers morning and evening, prayer upon the Prophet a thousand times or more, and the ṣalāt al-fātiḥ — a blessing upon the Prophet considered by the Tijānī as equivalent in value to the whole of creation. The way does not mix with others: a Tijānī cannot be affiliated to a second brotherhood. A strong characteristic: the claim of an exceptional spiritual rank for its adherents (quṭb (القُطْب) al-aqṭāb). The expansion in West Africa, from the 19th century onward (notably via al-Ḥājj ʿUmar Tall, then Ibrāhīm Niasse), makes it one of the major spiritual forces of the continent — in Senegal, Mali, Mauritania, Nigeria, Chad. Branches: Niassene, Ḥamāwiyya.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Our way is easy through love, and difficult through exclusivity.

entries.meditation: A way that asks for everything — fidelity, exclusivity, regularity — is it a burden or a liberation?

entries.fr: The order of the Mevlevî (whirling dervishes)
entries.racine_sens: Mawlānā — “our master” (the title given to Jalāl ad-Dīn Rūmī by his disciples)
entries.definition_concise: A brotherhood founded in Konya (Anatolia) by the disciples of Jalāl ad-Dīn Rūmī (1207-1273) after his death, principally by his son Sulṭān Walad. A world-famous characteristic: the samāʿ with whirling dance — the whirling dervishes.
entries.sens_profond: Rūmī himself did not formally found a way: he lived as a saint, wrote the Mathnawī and the Dīwān-i Shams, and left his son Sulṭān Walad to codify the teaching after his death. The ceremony of the samāʿ — in which the dancers whirl slowly, the right palm raised toward heaven to receive, the left palm turned toward the earth to give — has become the universal emblem of Sufism in the eyes of the world. But the samāʿ is not a dance: it is a prayer in movement, in which the body imitates the cosmic rotation of the spheres and the atoms around their centre. The tradition remained concentrated in Ottoman Anatolia, then was forbidden by Atatürk in 1925 — surviving above all in the cultural tradition. A contemporary renaissance: brotherhoods active in Turkey, branches in the West. The Mathnawī of Rūmī remains one of the great mystical books of humanity.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Listen to this reed, how it laments — it is of separation that it makes its tale.
Come, come, whoever you are — pagan, idolater, fire-worshipper. Come even if you have broken your vow a hundred times. Our door is not a door of despair. Come, as you are.

entries.meditation: If the body that whirls becomes prayer — what, in my life, could become prayer without saying it?

entries.racine_sens: ʿalā — to be elevated; ʿAlawī — from the founder Aḥmad al-ʿAlawī, who took this name
entries.definition_concise: A modern branch of the Darqāwiyya (itself issued from the Shādhiliyya), founded by Aḥmad (أَحْمَد) al-ʿAlawī (1869-1934) in Mostaganem (Algeria). Important for having been the first Sufi brotherhood to carry the teaching into Europe in the 20th century.
entries.sens_profond: Aḥmad ibn Muṣṭafā al-ʿAlawī, formed in the Darqāwiyya, founds his own branch in 1909. His teaching combines the classical Shādhilī rigour (effacement of self, faqr (الفَقْر), no extravagance) with a remarkable openness to modern questions. It is through him that the way arrives in Europe: his French disciples (Frithjof Schuon, Titus Burckhardt, Martin Lings) would become major figures of traditionalist thought and of the transmission of Sufism to the West in the 20th century. The ʿAlawiyya remains active in Mostaganem (the original zāwiya) and spreads across the world, notably in France and the United Kingdom. A particularity: a strong contemplative dimension, an emphasis on the khalwa (الخَلْوَة) and on the reading of the great texts (Ibn ʿArabī notably).

voix_des_maitres.texte: The way is simpler than is said. What is difficult is to make oneself simple.
His mere presence in a room changed everything.

entries.meditation: Can a great way cross languages, countries, cultures, and remain itself?

entries.racine_sens: Chisht — a village of Afghanistan, the place of origin of the lineage
entries.definition_concise: A major brotherhood of the Indian subcontinent, founded at Ajmer (India) by Muʿīn ad-Dīn Chishtī (1141-1230), originally from Afghanistan. Characteristics: openness to all (Muslims and non-Muslims), samāʿ and qawwālī chant, an emphasis on universal love and the service of the poor.
entries.sens_profond: Muʿīn ad-Dīn Chishtī arrives at Ajmer in India after a long formation in Khorasan. There he establishes a zāwiya that becomes one of the most important spiritual centres of the subcontinent. His tomb (dargāh) still attracts millions of pilgrims a year today, Muslims and Hindus alike. The great successors — Quṭb ad-Dīn Bakhtiyār Kākī, Farīd ad-Dīn Ganj-i-Shakar, Niẓām ad-Dīn Awliyāʾ, Naṣīr ad-Dīn Chirāgh-i Dehlī — extend the way to all of northern India. Salient characteristics: total openness (whatever the colour, the caste, the religion: the heart alone counts); the primacy of the samāʿ and of the qawwālī (devotional Persian/Urdu chant, of which Nusrat Fateh Ali Khan in the 20th century remains the world master); a categorical refusal of any relationship with political power; the centrality of the common table (langar) where rich and poor eat together.

voix_des_maitres.texte: Love everyone and love everything — for this nature is also the nature of God.
There are many paths to God. But my preference is the one that passes through the service of the created.

entries.meditation: If the love of all is a divine attribute — whom am I in the process of excluding from my love?
